text: AUSTI, a village of Austria in Moravia, at the junction of the head-streams of the Baeczwa, near the base of the Jablunka mountains, and about 3 m. S of Wsettin.
AUSTIA. See Aussig.

AUSTIN, a county of Texas, bounded on the N by the co. of Washington and Montgomery; on the NE by that of Harrisburg; on the SE by the co. of Fort-bend; on the SW by the co. of Colorado; and on the NW by that of Fayette. It is intersected by the Rio Brazos de Dios, and by several of its tributaries.—Also a town of Texas, the cap. of the co. of Bastrop, on the l. bank of the Rio Colorado or Flores.
AUSTINBURG, a township of Ohio, U. S., in Ashtabula &., 201 m. NE of Columbus. Pop. in 1840, 1,047.
AUSTINTOWN, a town of Ohio, U. S., in Trumbull co., 12 SE of Warren. Pop. in 1840, 1,245.
AUSTONLEY, a township in the parish of Almondbury, W. R. of Yorkshire, 7 m. SE of Huddersfield. Area 1,760 acres. Pop. 1,940.

AUSTRALIA. Some geographers have given the name of Oceania or Oceanica to the whole collection of those almost innumerable islands which are scattered over the Pacific ocean, and between it and the Indian ocean, to the SE of Asia and the W of America, between the 24th parallel of N and the 50th of S lat., and between 92° E and 109° W long. Within these limits, stretching 10,000 m. in every direction, we have a vast profusion of islands,—one of them rather a continent than an island,—two each three times the size of Great Britain, and two each larger than Great Britain and Ireland put together. The total superficial area of the various masses of land within the limits thus defined, probably exceeds 3,000,000 sq. m.,—a larger area than that of Europe. The great mass of this land lies between the 95th and 106th meridians of E long. On inspection of the map, it will be found that the vast assemblage of islands included within these limits may be divided into three groups:—The 1st, and smallest group, lies to the NW, and comprehends the islands in the Indian ocean and to the S of Asia, which are separated from the Indian continent by the straits of Malacca and the Chinese sea, viz., Japan, Formosa, the Philippines, the Moluccas, and a long chain of islands beginning with Timorlaut on the E, and terminating with Java on the W. The 2d, or central division, sometimes called Australasia, or Australia, comprehends New Holland, Van Diemen's Land, Papua or New Guinea, the Admiralty groups, the archipelago of New Britain, the Solomon islands, the archipelago of Queen Charlotte, the New Hebrides, the archipelago of New Caledonia, and New Zealand. The 3d, or eastern division—sometimes, from the multitude of islands of which it consists, called Polynesia—comprehends the Mindanao archipelago, the Society islands, the Harvey islands, the Friendly islands, the Carolines, the Pelew islands, the Mariannes or Ladrones, the Sandwich islands, the Bonin Sima islands, Mulgrave's islands, and several others. Walckenaer, and some other geographers, have laboured to establish such a physical relationship among these three groups of islands as should entitle them to be classed together, and treated as one great general division of the globe; but we have already intimated our intention to classify the first of these divisions under the general head of Asia; the second division only we shall comprehend under that of Australia,—a term which seems to be generally preferred to that of Australasia. The latter name was originally proposed by M. de Brosses, a skilful geographer, who meant to comprehend under it all the countries S of the continent of Asia, including New Holland, New Zealand, and New Guinea. This suggestion proceeded, in a great measure, from the belief that a southern continent towards the pole, denominated Terra Aus- I. tralis incognita, remained to be discovered and explored; but, independent of this circumstance, his reasons bear much weight when considered in another point of view: "In the immense extent of regions about to be explored," the learned President observes, "how various are the countries, the climates, the manners, and races of men! Were not some fixed points then to be assumed from place to place, our judgment would be bewildered. Divisions, corresponding to the progress of our knowledge, and also preserving due regard to physical circumstances, should be laid down. Four great portions of land, Europe, Asia, Africa, and America, constitute the known world: and there are three wide extents of ocean,-the Indian, or Ethiopic,-the North, or Atlantic,-and the South, or Pacific. The unexplored southern regions may be divided into three portions corresponding to these; each division being to the southward of one of the three portions of land. That to the S of Asia, in the Indian ocean, I shall, on this account, call Australasia." The countries to the S of America De Brosses proposed to call Magellanica; and those in the S part of the Pacific ocean, Polynesia. Australia, according to the limits which we now ascribe to it, is larger than the whole of Europe. The principal islands it includes are:-1. The immense island,-if it may be called such,-of New Holland, nearly 2,000 m. in breadth, and almost 1,700 m. in length; 2. Van Diemen's Land; 3. Papua, or New Guinea; 4. New Britain, and New Ireland, and the adjacent islands; 5. The Arsacides, or Solomon's islands; 6. New Caledonia; 7. New Hebrides; 8. New Zealand and islands to the S. Besides these,—which present the most prominent terrene objects in Australian geography,—there are hundreds, probably thousands, of smaller detached islands and coral reefs scattered over the Australian seas, many of which have never been accurately surveyed, and it is highly probable that many are still unknown.—In the present article we shall take a brief retrospect of the progress of discovery of the various countries comprised under the general denomination Australia; and then give some general notices, which are resumed, in greater detail, under other articles of our work. Progress of discovery.] The discovery of New Holland has commonly been ascribed to Dutch navigators of the 17th cent. M. de Brosses refers it to an earlier period, judging it probable that it was discovered by Paulovier de Gonneville in 1503. Gonneville sailed from Honfleur in June of that year. Off the cape of Good Hope he was assailed by a furious storm, in which he lost his reckoning, and was driven into an unknown sea. Here, as he saw the birds flying from the S, he sailed towards that quarter, and reached an extensive country which he called Southern India, and in which he spent six months refitting his vessel. On comparing the meagre accounts which he gives of the manners of the natives, with those of any of the Australians according to the earliest narratives, we conclude that they were different races, and that it was the coast of Madagascar upon which this early French navigator was driven. A set of maps, constructed in the year 1542, or perhaps earlier, has been found, wherein some part of New Holland is supposed to be laid down under the name of Great Java. But we are so little acquainted with these maps, that we cannot venture to maintain an opinion concerning them; at the same time we ought to remark, that repeated instances occur, - as will be seen in the course of this article,—where later navigators claim the merit of discoveries which belong to those who lived centuries before them. Early Dutch discoveries.] Early in the 17th cent., the Spanish navigator Quiros is conjectured to have seen the N or NE coast of New Holland. In 1606 he traversed the Australian seas with a fleet; and, on attending to his tract, the fact that he, or rather his second in command, Torres, may have seen New Holland, is far from improbable. But the Dutch were the first who became acquainted with any considerable extent of the coast of New Holland. They made several successive voyages to it, and gave names to the N and W parts of that great island which are retained to the present day. Doubts have been started whether any of them effected a landing previous to Pelsart, in 1629; but these have been removed by the French expedition in 1801 finding a tin platter on an island called Dirk Hartighs' island, close to New Holland, which bore the following inscription rudely graven: "1616, On the 25th of October, the ship Endraght of Amsterdam arrived here; first merchant Gilles Miebais Van Luck; Captain Dirk Hartighs of Amsterdam. She sailed on the 27th of the same month. 2 E Bantum supercargo; Janstins chief pilot; Pieter Ecoores Van Bue...... year 1616." Hartighs' vessel was on a voyage to India at the time of touching on this coast, which in the Dutch charts is called Landt Eendraght Van. In 1618, another Dutchman, Zeachen, ran along the N coast of New Holland; and several of his countrymen, in years immediately subsequent, extended the knowledge of this great island.-Edel's Land was called after a navigator of that name in 1619; Leuwin's Land was discovered in 1622; in 1627 Pieter de Nuytz, in a vessel named the Guldee Zeepard, fixed the position of different points, which later voyagers acknowledge to be uncommonly correct. William de Wit, Vlanen, and Carpenter, a Dutch general, were on the S and W coasts of New Holland in 1627 and 1628. In the subsequent year, Francis Pelsart, whose ship was separated in a storm from ten others, approached the W coast of New Holland; where, in lat. 28° S, he was cast away on Houtman's shoals, which the French affirm are about 8 leagues from the main land. The crew and passengers were saved by the boats, and reached a small island, about 3 leagues distant, which we apprehend to be Turtle-dove island; but finding no water there, Pelsart was led to examine a number of others. Having put a deck on his boat, and ascertained the position of the islands to be 28° 13' S, he stood on for the land. In 22° 17' S, he saw the natives at a distance, who fled whenever his people approached. Circumstances, which it is unnecessary now to recapitulate, induced Pelsart to run for the coast of Java. The chief discoveries which the Dutch made in Australia during the 17th cent., were those by Abel Jansen Tasman, in 1642-4. Tasman sailed from Batavia in August 1642, with two vessels under his command, the Heemskirk and Zeehaan. On the 24th of November, when in lat. 42° 25' S, and long. 163° 50' E, he saw land, bearing NE, 10 m. distant, which he called Van Diemen's Land. Running along the coast, he anchored in a bay, which he called Frederic Henry's bay, in lat. 43° 10', long. 167° 55'. This land, discovered by Tasman, was long esteemed the southern part of New Holland; but later discoveries have proved it to be a different country. In 1696, Vlaming sailed from the Texel in quest of a Dutch East Indiaman, supposed to have been lost somewhere on the coast of New Holland, during a voyage from the cape of Good Hope to Batavia. In December of the same year he made the coast in 31° 58' S lat., and 130° 13' E long. He landed with a number of men, and having found the tin plate before alluded to, left by his countrymen, in 1616, nailed to a post, added the following inscription, as the French found it in 1801: "1697, on the 4th day of February, the ship Geelvink of Amsterdam arrived here; Wilhelm de Vlaming, captain commandant; John Bremen of Copenhagen, assistant, Michael Bloem Van Estight of Bremen, assistant. The dogger Nyptangh, Captain Gerrit Colaart of Amsterdam; Theodore Hiermanns, of the same place, assistant; first pilot, Gerrit Gerritzen of Bremen. The galley Net Weseltje, Cornelius de Vlaming of Vlielandt, commander; Coert Gerritzen of Bremen, pilot. Our fleet sails hence, leaving the southern territories, for Batavia." This plate was discovered half-buried in sand, attached to the remains of a wooden post, in 1801. The inscriptions were carefully copied; and the plate replaced on the N point of Dirk Hartighs' isle, where it was found. Dampier's voyages.] Omitting other expeditions of less importance, we ought not to overlook the voyages of William Dampier, one of the most intelligent navigators who ever sailed from Britain. He twice visited the Australian regions, and landed on the coast of New Holland. In his first voyage he remained on it two months, from January 1688; and he gives a deplorable picture of the country. It was flat, low, and sandy, and afforded no fresh water except what was dug out of wells; few fishes inhabited the sea; the traces of no quadruped, excepting one, had been seen by the party; and scarcely any birds larger than a blackbird. The natives were described as appearing to him the most miserable creatures in the universe, almost stark naked, and without houses or covering: they had no religion or government, and cohabited promiscuously. Dampier observes, that New Holland is a very large tract of land, but that it was not then fully determined whether it is a continent or an island; he was certain, however, that it joined neither Asia, Africa, nor America. Discovery of New Guinea and other islands.] The unfavourable appearances exhibited by the greater part of the coast of New Holland, and the islands in its vicinity, restrained exploring voyages towards them. But, while little progress was made in this part of Australia, some other islands had occasionally been visited; and of the whole, it is not unlikely that the islands of Papua, or New Guinea, were among the first discovered by navigators. A Portuguese officer, Don Jorge de Menezes, in a voyage from Malacca to the Molucca islands, in 1526, wintered in a port immediately N, it would seem, of the great land of Papua. This port was probably in one of the adjacent islands. Other islands are mentioned, and all are said to be inhabited by the Papuans, or Papoos, the name by which the natives are still known. A squadron, fitted out in 1526, for the purpose of discovering spice islands in the South seas, sailed from Mexico, under the command of Alvarez de Saavedra, a Spaniard. A long time seems to have been occupied in this search; in returning from which, Saavedra also discovered the land of Papua. Having been led to believe that the country which he saw abounded in gold, he called it the Isla del Oro; it afterwards received the name of New Guinea, as some affirm, from navigators thinking it opposite to Guinea, on the coast of Africa, or perhaps from a supposed resemblance between the inhabitants of the two countries. Saavedra describes them as black, with short curled hair, and as going naked; but their civilization even then far exceeded that of the present natives of Australia, for they had not only swords of iron, but other arms of the same metal. Saavedra ran along the same land 100 leagues to the southward; and in a second voyage traversed more of the coast of Papua than any subsequent navigator has done. Ruy Lopez de Villalobos ranged along the same coasts in 1543, and, ignorant of its having before been visited by Europeans, he conferred upon it the name of New Guinea. In 1616, James le Maire and William Schouten, both skilful navigators, approached the coast of Papua. Alvaro Mendana probably saw some of the islands near Papua, in 1595; and likewise Tasman, in 1642, and Dampier, in 1699. Geographers have supposed that what is described by both these authors as Papua, was in fact New Britain; and that Dampier, in particular, never landed on that island. New Britain was certainly discovered by the latter navigator in 1699. Ranging along the coast to the most eastern part of New Guinea, he found it interrupted; and a lesser division, being an island, he called it New Britain. Dalrymple seems to have conceived the New Britain of Dampier to be the same as the Solomon islands. No part of all the Australian regions has been the subject of greater doubt or controversy than the Arsa cides, or Solomon islands. Even their existence was long denied after the original discovery. During the course of a voyage by Alvaro de Mendana, from Lima, for the purpose of discoveries in the South sea, in 1567, he fell in with a great shoal in Australia. This he called Baxos de la Candelaria, or 'Candlemas shoal,' which name it yet retains: it extended 15 leagues, and the middle lay in 6° 15' S lat. Mendana next fell in with a large island, and several smaller ones, where, finding good materials, he built a brigantine, which he sent out on further discoveries. In consequence of a design entertained by the Spanish government in South America, of establishing a settlement in Australia, Mendana sailed on another voyage with four vessels in 1595. His former discoveries among the islands had been considerable; and in the course of again endeavouring to find them, he discovered an island to the eastward, which he called Santa Cruz, and which is now known by the name of Egmont's island. New Caledonia was not known at the period of which we are now treating, as it belongs to the discoveries of the 18th cent.; but the New Hebrides were known to Quiros in 1606. One of the islands was then called Manicolo, or Mallicolo, by the natives, as it is at the present time; and it is evident that he not only landed on the largest, but visited others in the vicinity. New Zealand was discovered by Tasman, the navigator already named. On the 5th of December, 1642, while searching for Solomon islands, he was obliged to alter his course, and on the 13th came in sight of a very high and mountainous country, which he believed to be the Terra Australis. He called it Staaten Land, and anchored in a bay on the NE, where he had an interview with the natives. Supposing that a friendly intercourse could be carried on with them, he prepared to land; but a treacherous attack was made on him, wherein he lost several men, and thence called the place of his anchorage Murderer's bay. He traversed some of the coast, which was of a pleasing and fertile appearance, and next stood to the northward, discovering some small islands on his way. Progress of discovery from 1700 to 1810.] Although many islands, rocks, and shoals, were noted by the old navigators, we see, from this cursory retrospect, how very limited our knowledge of the Australian regions was previous to the 18th cent. There is a possibility that New Holland was discovered before the year 1600; but it is not yet supported by unquestionable evidence. In the subsequent century, various vessels visited small portions of its coasts; and Van Diemen's Land was supposed to be part of the same immense tract or continent. New Britain and New Ireland were almost totally unknown; the New Hebrides had been visited by one navigator: and New Caledonia was undiscovered. The position of New Zealand was hardly fixed, and only the general appearances of the country indicated. If there be any exception to these general remarks, it applies to the islands of the Arsacides; for few later navigators have left observations on them equally accurate with those of him by whom they were discovered. But a vast field of discovery opened in Australia at the beginning of the 18th cent. A few years after Dampier's return to Britain in 1700, two vessels were sent to the South seas, commanded by Woodes Rogers, an Englishman. Their design was the capture of some of the Spanish towns and shipping in South America, in which they fully succeeded. During 1710, the vessels, in one of which Dampier himself was pilot, passed through the straits of New Guinea, where he had been twice before; and Rogers was impressed with a high opinion of the richness of these insular regions. In 1721, Roggewein, a Dutch commander, pursuing ideas originally suggested by his father, was sent out with a small squadron to make discoveries in Australia. He lost one of his ships just about the eastern limits of Australia, and immediately afterwards discovered Aurora island, so named by him from coming in view of it as morning dawned. His chief design was to rediscover the Solomon islands, and the lands described by Quiros, but particular incidents obliged him to steer a different course. He landed in New Britain in 1722, where he was attacked by the inhabitants, people of a yellow colour, tall in stature, slender, and with black hair hanging down to the girdle. He next trafficked with the inhabitants of Moa and Arrimoa, two islands not far from the Papuan coasts, so named by Schouten. Roggewein continued his navigation in a region of innumerable islands, which on that account he called 'the Thousand Islands.' He describes their inhabitants as a race of people black and hairy, and of a treacherous and malevolent aspect, entirely naked, except in wearing a girdle about two inches broad with hogs' teeth interlaced, and strings of the same teeth around their legs and arms. Carteret.] Approaching nearer to our own times, we find several discoveries by former navigators confirmed. The group of islands containing Santa Cruz, where Mendana landed in 1595, was visited by Captain Carteret in 1767, while on a voyage of discovery from England. Falling in with a cluster of seven islands, he named the group Queen Charlotte's islands, and changed the name of Santa Cruz to Egmont island. Captain Carteret also discovered a strait, dividing New Britain from New Ireland, and sailed through it. Bougainville.] M. de Bougainville, a French officer, having gone out on a similar voyage of discovery, found himself within the limits of Australia in May 1768. After giving names to several islands in his way, he landed on Lepers Island, on which he was induced to bestow that name from observing that the natives in general were afflicted with leprosy. In May, M. de Bougainville sailed along the N coast of an extensive land, low, and covered with trees. Ignorant that this part of Australia had been already visited, and having found a number of islands lying near each other, he called the whole 'the Archipelago of the Great Cyclades.' The more extensive coast afterwards proved to be the 'Tierra Austral del Espiritu Santo,' where Quiros had remained a month. Pursuing a NW course M. de Bougainville discovered a new region of Australia. Standing along the S shore, he saw several islands, and after doubling a cape on the E, he called the land La Louisiade. A few degrees N of Louisiade he found a number of islands which bear his name, as also the straits to the NW of Solomon's islands. Bougainville next made the coast of New Ireland, and anchored in a bay, formerly named Gower harbour, by Captain Carteret, and now called Praslin's bay. Having skirted the N coast of New Guinea, he directed his voyage homewards. Surville.] Nearly about the same period, M. de Surville, also a Frenchman, undertook a voyage to Australia on some commercial speculation. He commanded a vessel of considerable size and force, and sailed from Pondicherry in June 1769. On the 6th of October, being in 6° 56' S lat., and long. 151° 30' E of Paris, he saw an island which he named First-Sight island, and then came in view of land with a great chain of mountains extending as far as the eye could reach. The lat. of this island he fixed at 7° 15', and its long. at 155° E of Paris. Fifteen leagues south of First Sight island high mountains were seen, and Surville proceeding along, passed many islands, and at length anchored in a fine harbour with reefs and islands at the entrance, which he named Port Praslin. On comparing the result of M. de Surville's observations, it has been concluded that he was navigating among the Solomon islands. The islands which he discovered were named by him the lands of the Arsacides; and to warn future navigators of their treacherous inhabitants, he left inscriptions in Port Praslin. Marion.] M. Marion du Fresne was employed by the French government to conduct a native of Otaheite to the Isle of France, and also to proceed on a voyage of discovery. But the Otaheitan -who had been brought to Europe by M. de Bougainvillehaving died at the island of Bourbon, M. Marion altered the course of the two ships under his command, and in February 1772, found himself within the region of Australia. He spent six days in Frederic Henry's bay, in Van Diemen's Land, searching in vain for fresh water, and then made the Bay of islands in New Zealand. Here he exerted himself to conciliate the good will of the natives, and apparently succeeded; but they found means to surprise two boats, and massacre 27 persons, among whom was M. Marion himself. Cook.] The voyages of Captain Cook are too well known, and too easily accessible, to require any notice in illustrating the progress of discovery in Australia. It is only incumbent on us to observe, that, independent of the new regions which he himself explored, he cleared up not a few of the doubts of preceding navigators concerning different Australian countries, and accurately fixed their position on the globe. In his first voyage, he visited the coast of New Holland, and gave names to numerous islands, bays, and capes. In his second, he came in sight of Aurora island in July 1774, which he found lay in 168° 30' E long., and then saw Lepers isle. He landed on Mallicolo; and on another island, Erromango, 2° S of Mallicolo, he was treacherously attacked by the natives. In August 1774, Cook anchored in a great bay of the Tierra del Espiritu Santo, 60 m. in extent of coast, and effected an accurate survey of the whole island, which he found 22 leagues long, 12 broad, and 60 in circuit. Of the chain of islands which he here visited, the Pic de l'Etoile is the most northern, and the island of Annatom the most southern. The whole lie between lat. 14° 29' and 20° 4' S, and long. 166° 41', and 170° 21' E; and extend 125 leagues from NNW to SSE. He called these islands the New Hebrides; thus changing their name a third time. Leaving the Tierra Austral, he next discovered an extensive country apparently about 87 leagues in length, and 10 in breadth, which he called New Caledonia. The people were courteous and friendly, and, unlike any other nation in the South sea, were not the least addicted to pilfering. After discovering the Isle of Pines, Botany isle, and several inconsiderable objects, Cook left the coast, and made sail for New Zealand. In the way thither, he discovered Norfolk island. During his third voyage round the world, Cook visited the same regions in 1777. He anchored a considerable time in Adventure bay in Van Diemen's Land, and then made | nan, the most eastern of the New Hebrides, and made the island the coast of New Zealand, where he obtained numerous interesting illustrations of the nature of the country, and the manners and disposition of its inhabitants. Australia was less the object of this voyage than the NW coast of America, for which he departed in the end of February. Forrest.] While new countries were sought for with such avidity in the southern latitudes of Australia, some of the more northern ones were also explored Captain Forrest, an intelligent navigator, undertook a voyage from India, the leading design of which was to ascertain the practicability of forming a settlement on an island near the northern promontory of Borneo. This voyage was performed in a vessel of only 10 tons, between the years 1774 and 1776. Captain Forrest examined the N coast of Waygiou, extending 15 leagues, and the extremity of which is immediately under the equator; and he anchored in Offak harbour in 10' N lat., and 127° 44' E long. After visiting several small islands, he gained Dory harbour, on the N coast of Papua. The natural history of these islands was illustrated by M. Sonnerat, who, nearly about this period, spent some years among them. Maurelle.] Don Francis Anthony Maurelle, during a voyage in 1781, crossed the line towards the northern parts of New Guinea, and steered a SE course through some portion of Australia. He discovered an island, the N coast of which extended 11 leagues apparently in about 150° long. E of Paris, and be tween 2° and 3° S lat. The natives resembled the Negroes of Guinea, in colour, hair, lips, and eyes. This island was called Maurelle, and two of six others, discovered on the same day, St. Michael and Jesus Maria. Steering through different clusters of islands to the N of New Ireland, he appears to have approached the Arsacides, and advanced towards the Candlemas shoals. But the particulars of his voyage are indistinctly narrated. La Perouse.] In the years 1786 and 1787, La Perouse navigated some portion of Australia. The only accounts we have of his voyage were transmitted by M. Lesseps from Botany bay. Shortland.] In July 1788, three ships, commanded by Lieutenant Shortland, sailed from Botany bay for England. Owing to the advanced state of the season, he resolved, instead of steering a southern course, to go to the northward, and either pass through Endeavour straits to the N of New Holland, or round the E coast of New Guinea. Not long after leaving the settlement, he fell in with an island called Simbu by its natives. Standing on his course, he discovered other islands, and entered a strait; and supposing himself the first navigator who had penetrated it, he called it Shortland's straits. It is unnecessary for us to examine the further progress of his voyage; for M. Fleurieu, in a learned critical discussion on the subject, has proved that Simbu is the same as Choiseul island; the straits, those before called Bougainville's straits; and the other islands, part of the lands of the Arsacides. Vancouver.] In 1791, Captain Vancouver explored 110 leagues of the SW coast of New Holland; where he discovered King George's sound, and some clusters of small islands. The former lies in lat. 35° 5' S, and in 118° 17' E long. Mr. Broughton, who commanded another vessel on the same expedition, discovered a fertile island, in S lat. 43° 49', and 183° 25' E long., which he called Chatham island. Labillardiere.] A voyage by the French in search of their unfortunate countryman La Peronse, contributed to throw light on those distant regions. Two vessels, the Recherche and Esperance, sailed from France in September 1791, fully stored with all that was most likely to insure the safety and success of the undertaking. In April 1792, they came within the limits of Australia, and were some time occupied in observations on Van Diemen's Land, in the course of which they conjectured that the S cape was separated from the main land; they also discovered a great harbour, which they called Port D'Entrecasteaux. They next saw the Isle of Pines, which received its name from high rocky elevations resembling trees at a distance, and laid down its position in 22° 42' S lat. They then ran along an immense chain of reefs extending 324 m. on the coast of New Caledonia. Their attention was chiefly directed to the SW coast, Captain Cook having surveyed the land to the N; and, in the course of their examination, they saw numerous mountainous islands, and detached rocks, with their points above water encircled by dangerous reefs. The difference was no more than 4' between the position of New Caledonia as fixed by Cook and the officers of this expedition. In July 1792, they saw the islands of the Arsacides, and ascertained that the Treasury islands were five or six in number, or more, though when seen from a distance they may be mistaken for only one. They lie in 7° 25' S lat. Sailing thence to the Admiralty islands, the situation of several places was determined in the way. Nearly under the line a number of islands were discovered, all connected by chains of reefs, low, and covered by lofty trees. After navigating among them, the vessels sailed to the Papuan islands, the situation of several of which was determined. In the subsequent year, 1793, the French made Lewin's land on the coast of New Holland, where they found that the latitudes had been ascertained with remarkable precision by its first discoverers. Many small islands were seen, which had not been previously visited; and having traversed several degrees on the SW, they anchored in Port D'Entrecasteaux, as they had done in the preceding year. They next sailed for New Zealand, and fixed the latitude of the Three King's islands in 34° 20' S. An island discovered in 29° 20' S lat., near the coast, was called Recherche. Some time afterwards the French got sight of Erro- ぶへんがいへい たんりんりょう カリチャリック デリリ デリリタ リリリタ リリリ リリリリ リリング アン・ファン・ファン・ファン・ファン・ファン・ファン・ファン・ファン・ファン・ファ of Tanna, where they enjoyed a brilliant spectacle arising from the vivid flames emitted during night by a volcano. Having made New Caledonia, their observations were resumed. From New Caledonia the French repaired to the Arsacides, and then passing Louisiade, steered for the coast of New Britain, where they discovered several mountainous islands before unknown. Keeping to the N, they anchored off the island of Waygiou, called by the natives Ouarido. Although this voyage did not add much to geographical discovery in Australia, it was valuable on account of its illustration of the natural history of the different countries, and the accuracy with which the astronomical observations seem to have been made. Bass and Flinders.] During the interval at which we are now arrived, between the expeditions of M. de la Perouse in 1786, and M. Labillardiere in 1794, the occasional voyages of the English between New Holland and Britain, or her eastern possessions, had been productive of some less important discoveries. In the course of the extraordinary voyage performed by the ship Duff, a group of about eleven islands, lying in 9° 57' S lat., and 167° E long., was discovered in 1797. But we hasten to a voyage of greater importance, by Captain Flinders, which first ascertained the fact of Van Diemen's land being no part of New Holland. The merit of this discovery does not belong to him alone, for Mr. Bass, surgeon of the ship Reliance, had made an excursion in an open boat to the southward of Port Jackson, towards the end of the year 1797. He sailed as far as 40° S lat., and visited every opening in the coast during his voyage. Between 39° and 40° S lat., he thought he had sufficient reason to believe that there was an extensive strait, or rather an open sea; and conjectured that Van Diemen's Land consisted of a group of islands. The want of a better vessel prevented him at that time from completing their circumnavigation. This was remedied by the governor of the English colony sending out Captain Flinders, accompanied by Mr. Bass, on a voyage of discovery. They sailed for Van Diemen's Land in October 1798, and visited Furneaux's islands, discovered in 1774 by the captain of the Adventure. On standing further into the supposed strait, they discovered a large harbour which they called Port Dalrymple, in S lat. 41°, on the N of Van Diemen's Land. An island 15 or 20 m. in circuit, was next discovered; and another in lat. 40° 24', E long. 145° 2', which, from the innumerable quantities of albatrosses frequenting it, they called Albatross island. Ten other islands were discovered in the vicinity, and the whole received the general name of Hunter's isles. In this latitude the navigators concluded that they had passed through a strait between one and two degrees in width, which separated New Holland from Van Diemen's Land. The appearance of the coast changed; a great swell rolled in, and a surf breaking on a bold shore announced the vicinity of the open ocean. On the 8th of January they passed the SW cape of Van Diemen's Land; and what had hitherto been universally described as part of New Holland, proved a large island completely separated from the continent. The discovery of this passage, -named by the governor Bass's straits,—promised great advantages. In 1804, the China fleet entered the strait on the 28th of October, passed it in safety, and reached the coast of China on the 28th of December.—Captain Flinders was soon afterwards engaged in subsequent voyages of discovery. In the earlier part of the year 1802, he surveyed King George's sound, and the W coast of New Holland, from Leuwin's land on the S coast to Western Port. He again left Port Jackson in July of the same year, and steering N, explored Northumberland and Cumberland islands, and the Great Barrier reefs of coral rock; after which, continuing his course northwards, and sailing through Torres or Endeavour straits in 36 hours, he arrived in the great gulf of Carpentaria in the latter end of the season, every part of the E side of which he minutely surveyed. The sickness of his crew obliged him to interrupt this survey, and sail for Timor in March 1803, but returning, he completed the circumnavigation of New Holland in eighteen months. In August 1803, he again sailed with the command of the Porpoise and Cato, and a third ship in company. Both the former were wrecked on a reef of rocks in 22° 11' S lat., and 155° 13' E long., nearly 800 m. from Botany bay. A party of his men escaped thither in an open boat; and after procuring assistance from that settlement, Captain Flinders saw the remainder, and all his officers, safely embarked for China. Impatient to convey his papers, plans, and charts of discovery to England, he embarked once more from Port Jackson in a vessel of only 19 tons burden, which carried him across the Great ocean to the Isle of France, where, though he possessed passports from the French government, he was detained with all his despatches. Captain Flinders was detained till 1810. Meanwhile appeared the 1st vol. of M. Peron's account of the voyage of discovery of the Géographe and Naturaliste, in which French names were given to all Flinders's discoveries. Spencer's gulf was named Golfe Bonaparte; the gulf of St. Vincent, Golfe Josephine; the country between Western Port, in Bass's straits, and Nuyt's archipelago, an extent of 900 leagues, Terre Napoleon; and so on; not even the smallest island being left without some identification with French discovery; although the Géographe was just entering the strait from the eastward when met by Captain Flinders in the Investigator, who informed Captain Baudin of his discoveries to the westward. M. Peron's 2d vol. never appeared, nor the charts referred to in his 1st, till, in 1814, when Flinders having published his discoveries in 2 vols., with an atlas of charts, M. Freycinet, the first lieutenant of Captain Baudin's expedition, put forth an atlas evidently founded upon Flinders's surveys, but bearing the French names recorded in Peron's 1st vol. Malte Brun, in a me- ~ moir entitled 'Sur le Plagiat Impérial, relatif a la pretendue Terre Napoleon,' states that both Peron and Freycinet received their instructions from the Minister of the marine as to the manner in which they should advance their pretensions to the first discovery of a tract of 250 leagues extent on the S coast of New Holland. King.] Between the years 1818 and 1822, Captain King examined the unexplored coasts of New South Wales, from Arnheim bay, near the W entrance of the gulf of Carpentaria, westward and southward; and delineated the coast-line between Cape Hillsborough, in lat. 20° 54' S, and Cape York, the N extremity of New South Wales, a distance of 690 m.; also between NW Cape and Depuch island; and the islands of Melville and Bathurst. At the close of Captain King's surveys, in 1822, there remained, between Dampier's archipelago, in S lat. 22°, and Cape Huy, in lat, 14°, about 500 m. of coast of Continental Australia, wholly unsurveyed. Wickham, Stokes, and Blackwood.] In 1837, the Beagle, Captain Wickham, sailed to explore and survey such portions of the Australian coast as were left unexplored by Flinders and King, to examine with minute care the whole space between Cape York and the southern shores of New Guinea. Under Captain Wickham some of the most important objects of the voyage were achieved; but in consequence of his retirement in March 1841, in ill-health, the command of the expedition devolved on Captain Stokes. This survey, between the years 1837 and 1843, was followed up by Captain Blackwood, in the Fly, who, between the years 1842 and 1845, accomplished a minute survey of the unexplored portion of the Great Barrier reef, and the eastern part of Torres strait, besides about 140 m. of the southern coast of New Guinea. Some of the minor islands belonging to A. have been surveyed with great accuracy by English navigators; those especially which were visited by Captain Cook, as New Zealand, the Society islands, the Friendly islands, and the Sandwich islands. Others, as Solomon's archipelago, the islands of Santa Cruz, and the large island of New Guinea, have not yet received this minute examition.—The progress of inland discovery will be related in connexion with the different settlements on Continental A. General physical features.] Malte Brun in Oceanica, as he calls it, sees the "magnificent fragments of a former world scattered over the mighty ocean;" and Walckenaer regards these groups of islands as the debris of an immense portion of the habitable hemisphere which has been overwhelmed by the waves of the ocean. Following out this idea, both these geographers attempt to indicate the points of reunion still to be found amongst the surviving fragments of so mighty a convulsion as they suppose to have here taken place. "Nature," says Malte Brun, "has given this part of the world a very prominent and characteristic physiognomy. No portion of the surface of the globe has more numerous inequalities; and in none, except America, have the chains of mountains so striking a polarity,—so marked a direction from N to S: at the same time, these chains generally present, near the middle, a great bend, having a direction from W to E. The best-marked among them is that formed by the Marianne islands, the Carolines, and the Mulgraves; which is probably connected, by means of St. Augustine's islands and some other links, with the archipelago of the Navigators, or that of the Friendly islands. The general direction of this chain is from NW to SE. Even among the Carolines, where the Polynesian chain turns due E, the several links lie N and S. Another great chain makes its appearance in the isle of Luzon, the largest of the Philippines, which passes, in the island Palawan, into that of Borneo. The direction of this wellknown branch is from NE to SW; and it bounds on one side the basin of the Chinese sea. More to the E, this chain is converted into a number of minor ones, united in groups varying in their structure. The chains of Celebes and Gilolo are well-marked; but a larger and higher one crosses New Guinea, where some of its elevations are covered with perpetual snow. In New South Wales, the long line of the Blue mountains extends to Van Diemen's Land. terminating in South Cape and Cape Pillar,-im mense masses of basalt, which give a magnificent idea of this cordillera of Central Oceanica. The fourth great chain takes its commencement at the Andaman and Nicobar islands, and then gives rise to the islands of Sumatra, Java, Timor, and others. It runs in the form of a bow from NW to SE, and then due E; but it probably passes by Cape Diemen, where it can have no other direction than N and S. All the archipelagos of Eastern Oceanica lie N and S. New Zealand, New Caledonia, and the New Hebrides, form well-marked chains. That of Solomon's islands, bending from SE to NW, is continued in New Ireland, and New Hanover. It not unfrequently happens that the small chains are individually terminated by a larger island than the others of which they are composed. Thus the islands of Otaheite, Owyhee, and Tierra del Espiritu Santo, are found at the extremity of a line of smaller islands. These analogies," M. Brun continues, "might have facilitated the progress of discovery, and especially contributed to make each archipelago more easily recognised. By carefully marking the direction of a chain, navigators might have made themselves almost certain of discovering new islands; and even still they ought to attend to a principle which may put them on their guard against immense reefs which, in all probability, follow the direction of chains at the bottom of the ocean." Coral formations, &c.] Whatever may be thought of the theory of an ancient continent once covering all that space which now presents only scattered groups of islands, at vast distances from each other, Nature seems now busily occupied in these seas in uniting the groups to each other by the formation of coral reefs or islands, which process may in the course of ages unite the present scattered fragments of land into one mighty continent. The innumerable low islands and reefs which are scattered over the Australian seas, seem to owe their origin to this curious process; and there is scarcely a league of sea in which these singular formations are not in progress. Of these islands, some shoot up in a conical form to a great elevation; while others have for their base a reef of coral rocks generally arranged in a circular form. "The middle space," says Malte Brun, " is often occupied by a lagune, the sand of which is mixed with pieces of broken coral and other marine substances; proving that such islands have been originally formed by these coral rocks, which are inhabited and, according to some, created by polypifers, and afterwards augmented and elevated by the slow accumulation of light bodies drifted down to them by the sea. It is however remarkable," he adds, "that among the islands so constituted some are almost level with the sea; while others have hundreds of feet of elevation—of which last Tongatabu is an example—and on the summits of these last are found coral rocks perforated in the same manner with those found at the water's edge. Now the madrepores, millepores, and tubipores, which raise these submarine habitations, (for the true coral polypus is never found here,) grow over the hardened spoils of their dead predecessors; they cannot live above the level of the sea." In this circumstance, M. Brun sees evidence that the sea, at a former period, washed these rocks, and has gradually retired and left them exposed. But this is not altogether demonstrated: for it were easy to imagine that such reefs may have been elevated by a submarine volcano. He goes on, however, to enquire "Whether the zoophytes, or polypifers, have themselves formed the stony bodies which they inhabit? Or, whether they find them ready prepared by the hand of Nature? This is one of the most interesting problems in physical geography; but hitherto the observations made are too vague and too recent to afford a complete solution of it. Messrs. Anderson and Forster incline to think that the animals themselves form the matter which composes the coral rock, and consequently that new islands may be created by their labours. On this point Captain Cook also is decided. Dalrymple thinks that the coral rocks are often formed at the bottom of the ocean, from which they are detached by currents and tempests, and then thrown on the sand banks. This may, in some localities, take place; but it cannot apply to the reefs which rise like walls in the middle of the deepest sea, such as the formidable rocks on which Captain Flinders nearly perished, and which probably proved fatal to La Perouse. The great reef of New Caledonia is so steep that Captain Kent, commander of the Buffalo, sounding at no greater distance than twice the length of the ship, with a line of 150 fathoms, could find no bottom. The reefs round New South Wales also rise like perpendicular walls from a very deep bottom. Such structures must owe their origin to the animals themselves, unless we should advance some such new doctrine as that they grow by a vegetation resembling that of the fuci; and that the polypi found on them are analogous to the insects which take up their abode on herbs and trees;—a theory to which the arborescent appearance of some corals, and the fun gous forms of others, give some countenance." Mr. Darwin, the latest, and perhaps the best-informed, observer of these singular formations, is of opinion that the zoophytes, which build reefs, do not work at greater depths than from 20 to 30 fathoms; and that their greatest activity of operation is manifested on the surface and outer edges of reefs. Whence then -the question recurs-have these vast masses of coralline formation originated which shoot up from a depth of hundreds of fathoms? Mr. Darwin's solution of the difficulty is bold, but the only one yet suggested which appears to account for all the phenomena attendant on these formations, viz. that the areas on which the coral reefs rest have undergone progressive subsidence, such as utterly to withdraw large tracts of land below the surface of the ocean, leaving the coral reefs which encircled or fringed their shores, still on the water's level, and therefore, under circumstances fitted to sustain their growth and position. Thus the atoll, or circular coral ring, with its interior lagune, may mark the place once occupied by a point of land which has subsided downwards into the sea, leaving the coral reef circling round the centre, growing over its surface, and rising upwards by new constructions. Then again, as to the linear reefs, we may suppose a prolonged line of reef to be built up on the shelf of the coast as it declines into the sea, leaving a narrow channel of water between: and that the continental land gradually sinking has left a wider channel between it and the reef, while the latter has retained its position, and is continually replaced upwards by fresh coral as the lower portions subside. Then again, if there be areas of upheaval as well as of subsidence, we may expect to find such coral structures raised in places above the level at which these zoophytes work, of which there are numerous instances. "These reefs render the navigation of this ocean exceedingly dangerous. In some of its seas they reach the surface, while in others they lie dangerously concealed, having over them only a few feet of water. On those which are covered with water are found immense collections of molluscæ and small shells. Muscles of every variety, pearl-oysters, pinnæ marinæ, starfish, and medusæ, also collect here in millions." [M. Brun.] Geology.] The geological formation of these islands is exceedingly various. The basaltic or volcanic formation prevails amongst the great group of islands towards the NW, which we have resolved to treat as appendages to the Asiatic continent; and some of the islands belonging to the A. groups present volcanoes in a state of activity. The great island of A. comprises every variety of geological formation, primitive, secondary, and volcanic. The axis of the great mountain-chain, which rising in Van Diemen's Land, sinks under the sea in Bass's strait, emerges at the S extremity of New Holland, stretches along a great part of the eastern side of the continent, sinks under the sea in Torres strait, and reappears on the New Guinea coast,-is composed of primitive rocks, chiefly unstratified, flanked by palæozoic strata incumbent on them, and mixed with volcanic rocks; succeeding to these are detached coal-formations and superficial beds, representing the tertiary formations of Europe. The islands which traverse Torres strait, in the line of this chain, are all granite, syenite, or old metamorphic rock; while to the E none of these primitive rocks appear, but only low coral isles and reefs; and to the E of these again, all and gold of the purest quality, embedded granularly island. Manganese abounds everywhere in New Zealand; and coal has been discovered on the W coast of Continental A. Lead, rich in silver, has also been found in the southern parts of that great island. Climate.] New Holland, which does not extend beyond the parallel of 39° 15' S lat., has a less elevated temperature than the latitude would lead us to suppose; the winter is not rigorous, but is remarkable for violent tempests of wind; the cold is never of long duration; the epochs of the seasons are exactly opposed to those of Europe. New Zealand has a temperate but humid climate; the small extent of the other islands, and their frequent and abundant rains, procure for them the temperature of the ocean. A portion of the great island is within the region of the variable winds; the rest is affected by the trade winds or monsoons. To the N of the equator, the wind blows half the year from SW, and the other half from NE; to the S of the equator, half of the year from the NW, and the other half from the SE. "The climate of New South Wales and Van Diemen's Land, as we see it in the present day, is very different from what it was before those colonies were brought within the pale of civilization. Records are wanting to show with certainty the extent of the mutation which civilization has effected; reasoning, however, from analogous causes and effects, we cannot much err in affirming that the destruction of thick herbaceous underwood scrubbs, and thick interwoven forest, must have necessarily rendered the climate drier; just as the 250,000 acres of cultivated land in those colonies, freed from the bad conductors of heat which encumbered their surface, have developed their powers of absorption and radiation, and thus naturally contributed towards the increase of the mean annual temperature." [Strzelecki.] The Isothermal line, or line of equal height, at equal distances to the N and S of it, in its circuit of the globe, lies generally within the northern hemisphere; but in A. it is in the southern hemisphere. It crosses the line to the S at Singapore, and after describing an arch with its apex on the E coast of New Holland, under 7° S lat., emerges again from the southern hemisphere in the South Pacific ocean to the E of New Zealand. Hence, it appears, that the whole continent of A. has a degree of temperature throughout the year equal to what would be implied in any other part of the world by a position 7° degrees nearer the line. Thus while the orange cannot be grown at Charleston in South Carolina, in N lat. 32°, it grows magnificently at Hunter's river in A. to the N of Sydney; and while the cotton-plant in South Carolina is an annual, being destroyed every winter by the frost, and requiring to be reproduced from seed every spring,in A. it is a perennial, as it is in the Brazils, in the East Indies, and in Egypt. [Dr. Lang.] Animals. Animal life is comparatively scanty in amount in this region of the globe. None of the great races of the Asiatic quadrupeds has extended to the A, islands; but several of them possess animals peculiar to themselves. Indeed the mammalogy of A., in its continental portion at least, is very peculiar and anomalous. The following table exhibits the general outlines of A. zoology according to Von Ritter:- No. of spe- No. of spe- Whole No. cies common Whole No. cies peculiar of known to A. and of A. species. to A. other contispecies. nents. Total The pig is the only animal found everywhere domesticated; and it is of the same species as that of China. In New Guinea we find the babyrussa, or hog-deer, an animal equally partaking of the hog and the deer. The ornithology of this region also presents some new and singular forms. New Guinea is the native seat of the bird of paradise; but the total absence of gallinaceous birds is very remarkable. No seas abound so much in whales, dolphins, and other cetaceous animals, as those which surround these islands. The abundance of fish, and the facility of taking them, has rendered the fisher instead of the hunterstate, the normal condition of most of the A. tribes. Many species of fish are peculiar to this region; but there are also sharks, bonitas, dorados, tunnies, rays, and mullets. The seals of the Pacific differ from those of the Atlantic. A species of alligator is said to occur on the western coasts of the continent. Plants.] The vegetable kingdom is here exceedingly rich and diversified. The banana, sugar-cane, arum, batata, curcuma, cocoa-nut, and cabbage-tree, bamboos, pepper, and mastic, are all cultivated in the New Hebrides and New Caledonia. In the Solomon islands, ginger and cloves are produced. The numerous family of palms is extended over the most remote and the smallest islands; and another family of nutritious trees of general prevalence is that of the artocarpi, or bread-fruit tree. Continental A. alone is destitute of these lastmentioned vegetable tribes, although its middle and southern parts especially present many genera, and even entire natural orders, of plants peculiar to itself. The orange-tree extends as far as the New Hebrides; the sugar-cane is found as far to the E as Otaheite. It may be remarked generally, that the most peculiar vegetable forms are to be found in the southern portion of the A. countries. The channel of Torres-strait forms a remarkable line of demarcation between two kinds of vegetation; "rude forests of large but rugged stemmed gum-trees, with their almost leafless and quite shadeless branches," constitute the characteristic of the vegetation on the S side of this strait; while on the islands on the N side, not a gum-tree is to be seen; and the dense lofty woods and impenetrable thickets exhibit the richest vegetation, in which the cocoa-nut, the plantain, the bamboo, the sago-palm, and other heat and moisture loving plants, largely intermingle. [Jukes.] Inhabitants.] The Malay stock of the human species is spread over Polynesia, and the eastern part of Australia. They are a yellow or brown-complexioned race, with long lank hair, scanty beards, high cheek bones, large mouths, and short noses with wide nostrils. The second great race of men in this portion of the globe has been denominated by Malte Brun the Oceanian Negroes, who are regarded by Cuvier as a branch of the African Negroes. Compared with the Africans, they are of a diminutive size, being about 4 feet 9 inches high, and never exceeding 5 feet. They have spare and puny frames; thick lips, flat noses, and woolly hair. Their skin is not jet black, like that of the African, but of a sooty brown. Sir Everard Home thus describes one who was sent to England by Sir Stamford Raffles, to distinguish him from the African Negro: "His skin is of a lighter colour; the woolly hair grows in small tufts, and each hair has a spiral twist. The forehead rises higher, and the hindhead is less cut off. The nose projects more from the face. The upper lip is larger and more prominent. The lower lip projects forward from the lower jaw to such an extent that the chin makes no part of the face, the lower part of which is formed by the mouth. The buttocks are so much lower than in the Negro as to constitute a marked distinction; but the calf of the leg is equally high as in the Negro." This race is extended over New Holland, Van Diemen's Land, New Caledonia, the New Hebrides, New Britain, the Solomon islands, and New Guinea, where they go under the Malayan appellation of Papuas. Of these places they have exclusive possession, the Malays having either been expelled, or never permitted to settle. They seem also to have once occupied the Moluccas and the Philippines; but in these places they have been partly destroyed and partly driven into the interior by the Malays. In the Philippines they are called Ygolotes and Negritos. Their features seem to be recognised in the inhabitants of the Andaman islands, and in the Googos of Sumatra. They exist in their purest state in Van Diemen's Land and in New Guinea.—Dr. Latham is of opinion that a fundamental unity may be traced in the languages of the Malayan, Papuan, and Australian tribes, in opposition to the general opinion of their separate character and origin. The beauty and vast extent of the different islands, the salubrity of the climate, and the excellence of the soil, early held forth to Europeans the advantages which would ensue from a permanent colonization in this region of the globe. Centuries ago, the utility of colonizing Australia occupied the attention of Europeans. Mendana established himself in Santa Cruz, an island presenting the most valuable resources to the navigators of the neighbouring seas. His views, however, were not originally directed to that particular spot; it was only in consequence of his search for the Arsacides failing, that he went thither in 1595. On Mendana's decease, his widow made another attempt to discover the Arsacides. The voyage was short and unsuccessful, and a sudden determination was adopted to sail for the Spanish settlements in South America; but Quiros, who had been chief pilot to this expedition, renewed the design of a colony, after having a second time traversed the Australian ocean. Whether or not he carried out people and materials for such a purpose, or what was the specific intention of his voyage, is uncertain; but he addressed a memoir on the subject to Philip of Spain, which was published in 1610, a few years after his return. He entertained a different opinion from Mendana, with regard to the most suitable place for a settlement, judging the Tierra Austral del Espiritu Santo, lying some degrees farther S, as the best. It is not surprising that he should have felt partiality towards a territory which he himself discovered. In enumerating its qualifications, he proceeds: "Finally, sire, I can, with confidence, assert, that the bay of Vera Cruz, situated in 15° 20' S lat., presents the greatest advantages for the foundation of a large city, and the establishment of a numerous colony. I can but imperfectly describe the riches awaiting those Europeans who shall visit such delightful countries; time, in unfolding them, will make amends for my inability; and I doubt not that this colony will become the centre of communication and future mart for all the commerce of Chili, Peru, Ternate, the Philippines, and other remote kingdoms under the dominion of your majesty." Quiros pledged himself for the success of any enterprise which should be devised for establishing a colony; and, as an inducement, gave in detail a flattering picture of the soil, climate, beauty, and salubrity of that and other Australian regions.-The learned President de Brosses terminates his work on southern navigations with an ingenious and enlightened disquisition on the establishment of colonies. He shows the various ways in which they may prove useful to the mother-country,—either serving for the deportation of criminals, or for the ends of commerce. After treating at large of the different regions where settlements might be made, he thus decides in preference of Australia: "Any colony established in New Britain, New Guinea, New Holland, or among the Papuans, will have the clue of Ariadne at command. With time, perseverance, and some expense-which cannot be regretted, as it will return an hundred-fold-all will be successively attained. This, it is true, will not be the work of a day. Great projects presume the necessity of great exertions, and these long continued. The calculation of time by years belongs to individuals, a whole nation counts only by ages. Powerful kingdoms are adapted for extensive views; and their kings, animated by the love of glory, by attachment to their country, and benevolence towards mankind, ought to consider their people as always in existence, and labour for an infinite protraction of time." M. de Brosses, after reviewing the state of all the countries then known, seems to think that New Guinea is the most favourable site for an Australian colony. He enters into considerable detail concerning the persons, utensils, and materials requisite for founding an establishment, and the advantages which might be expected to result from it. The mode to be pursued in carrying on an intercourse with the natives next engages his attention: he suggests what difficulties will be opposed by them, and recommends the utmost moderation in all the dealings of the settlers. In a work entitled Terra Australis Cognita, published ten years afterwards, in Edinburgh, by a Scottish advocate, the same ideas are farther insisted on. The progress of colonization in this region has hitherto been confined to Continental Australia, Van Diemen's Land, and New Zealand; and is detailed under these heads. GENERAL VIEW OF CONTINENTAL AUSTRALIA. To decide whether New Holland ought to be called an island or a continent, is not easy, nor is it necessary; for the truth is that the meaning of the word continent, like that of the word sea, is not exactly determined. If it were made to depend upon size merely, New Holland-now generally known by the name of Australia-might be called a continent as well as Europe. Whether or not Europe would have been called a continent, had it been entirely surrounded by the sea, is matter of doubt. Dimensions.] If New Holland be accurately delineated in the late maps, it extends from York Cape, its northernmost point, in 10° 37' S lat., to Cape Wilson, its most southern point, in 39° 12' S lat.; and from its most western point, in 112° 20', to Point Danger, its most eastern cape, in 153° 30' E long. So that its length from E to W is 2,227 geog. m.; while its breadth from N to S is 1,680 geog. m. If we suppose its medial length to be 1,730 geog. m., and its medial breadth 1,260 m., its superficial contents will be 2,179,800 sq. geog. m. It is washed by the Indian ocean on the W, and by the Pacific on the E; to the S lies the island of Van Diemen; and on the N it is separated from New Guinea and the Moluccas by the straits of Torres and of Timor. Timor, the nearest of the Asiatic islands, is about 280 m. distant from Cape Talbot. Coasts.] The outline of this large island, or continent, is singularly regular and compact, presenting only two large indentations throughout a coast-line of nearly 8,000 m. in extent. Its minor gulfs, bays, and harbours seldom penetrate any considerable distance inland; and the navigable estuaries of its rivers are few in number, and bear no comparison in point of size with those which we find in other quarters of the globe. On the N coast the great gulf of Carpentaria, between the meridians of 136° and 142°, runs up to the parallel of 18° S. On the S, Spencer's gulf, between 136° and 138°, forms a deep but comparatively narrow indentation. The E coast, from Torres strait to Bass's strait, is bounded by a ridge of mountains, which in some places approaches to within 30 m. of the coast, and in others recedes inland to treble that distance, leaving only a narrow stripe of cultivable land, watered by rivers of brief course, between the foot of the high lands and the coast, which, in several places, is high and precipitous. Cook named this coast New South Wales, from its supposed resemblance of aspect to that of the N shore of the British channel. On this coast, between Cape York and Wilson's promontory, proceeding southwards, we have Shelburne bay, Temple bay, Princess Charlotte's bay, Bathurst bay, Trinity bay, Rockingham bay, Halifax bay, Repulse bay, Shoalwater bay, Hervey's bay, Wide bay, in lat. 26°, the extreme point of the New South Wales settlements, Glasshouse bay, Moreton bay, Broken bay, Botany bay, Jervis bay, Twofold bay, and Corner inlet; and Capes York, Grenville, Weymouth, Sidmouth, Melville, Flattery, Bedford, Tribulation, Grafton, Cooper, Sandwich, Cleveland, Bowling-Green, Upstart, Gloucester, Palmerston, Townsend, Clinton, Manifold, Capricorn, Sandy cape, Moreton, Lookout, Danger, Byron, Lennox, Smoky cape, Plomer, Hawke, Elisabeth, Sugar-loaf, Black-head, St. George, Dromedary, Green cape, Howe, the SE point of A., in 37° 30' S, Ram-head, Point Hicks, and Wilson's promontory. The long chain of coral reefs, called the Great Barrier reef, extends from Break-sea Spit, a narrow sand-bank which runs 20 m. N from Sandy cape, in S lat. 24° 30′, along the NE coast of A. for upwards of 1,200 m.—The S coast, from Cape Leuwin to Spencer's gulf, an extent of nearly 25° of long., is generally low and sandy, with only a few scattered sandy eminences; between the latter gulf and Wilson's promontory there is greater comparative diversity of feature, and a considerably indented line of coast; from Wilson's promontory to Cape Howe, the land for several miles from the beach is low and sandy, and partly covered with bushes. The principal indentations of this coast, proceeding westwards, are Western port, Port Philip, the large gulf of St. Vincent, which is separated from Spencer gulf by Yorke peninsula, Sleaford bay, Coffin bay, Anxious bay, Streaky bay, Denial bay, Fowler bay, the Great Australian bight, Esperance bay, Doubtful Island bay, King George's sound, Torbay, Wilson inlet, Parry inlet, and Flinders bay. The capes are Cape Liptrap, Patterson, Schank, Otway, Nelson, Northumberland, Lannes, Bernouilli, Jervis, Spencer, Catastrophe, Willoughby, Radstock, Brown, Bell, Fowler, Adieu, Pasley, Nuyts, Chatham, D'Entrecasteaux, Beaufort, and Leuwin.- On the W coast, from Cape Leuwin to Point Naturaliste, the country is gently undulating and moderately wooded; and this character, diversified by extensive lagunes, is maintained to the estuary of the Swan river. The Darling range of mountains gradually approaches the coast as it proceeds N, and bold precipitous cliffs rise abruptly from a narrow girdle of flat and often inundated coast. To the N of the Swan river, an open forest-country, generally of a sandy character, lines the coast, along which limestone ridges of moderate elevation appear; and from Cape Cuvier to Cape Londonderry, whence the shore begins to trend to the SW, the coast exhibits irregular ranges of sandstone hills, about 300 ft. in height, covered with brushwood and prickly grass, and intersected by deep ravines, which rise abruptly from extensive plains of low sterile land; but between these hills fertile valleys occur. Much of the coast is thickly strewed with rocky islets and reefs, nearly all dry at low water. It is remarkable, that while the whole coast on the W side of King's sound, and between Roebuck bay and Point Swan, is entirely of sandstone; the islands, and all the high land on the E side of that sound, are entirely of quartz. [Wickham.] The bays and inlets on the W coast are Geographe bay, Leschenault inlet, Peel's inlet, Cockburn's sound, Gage road, Jurieu bay, Gaultheaume bay, Shark bay, Geographe channel, and Exmouth gulf; beyond which, along the coast, now trending NE, we have Nichol's bay, Desault bay, Lagrange bay, Roebuck bay, Beagle bay, King's sound, Brunswick bay, York sound, Admiralty gulf, and Vansittart bay. The capes and headlands on the W and NW coast are Cape Freycinet, Cape Naturaliste, Bouvard, Peron, Island point, Point Grey, Red point, North point, Steep point, Peron's peninsula, Cape Cuvier, NW cape, Cape Preston, Laubert, Larry, Jaubert, Treville, Villar, Baskerville, Leveque, Point Hall, Cape Voltaire, Bougainville, and Talbot. - From Cape Talbot to Van Diemen's gulf, along the W side of Arnheim's land, the coast is low and thinly wooded, and interspersed with low hills with flat summits. The W and S coasts of the gulf of Carpentaria exhibit the same characteristics of low hills and flat alluvial tracts; and the E coast, to Cape York, appears nowhere higher than a ship's mast-head, but has not yet been minutely examined. The principal bays on the N coast are Cambridge gulf, Queen's channel, Clearance strait, and Van Diemen's gulf, formed by the coast of the continent and the two large islands Bathurst and Melville, Mountnorris bay, Castlereagh bay, Arnheim's bay, Caledon bay, and Lemmen's bight, the three last on the W coast of the great gulf of Carpentaria. The capes are Cape Londonderry, Bernier, Dussejour, Domett, Hay, Ford, Blaze, Cockburn, Turner, Braithwaite, Stewart, Dale, Arnheim, Alexander, Barrow, and Dayfeu point. Islands.] The principal islands are Flinders' island and King's island, in Bass's strait; Kangaroo island, at the mouth of St. Vincent's gulf; Dirk Hartog's island, forming the W side of Shark's bay; Bathurst and Melville islands, off the N coast of Arnheim's land; and Wellesley's islands, at the bottom of the gulf of Carpentaria. Besides these, there are innumerable clusters of islets and coral-reefs scattered along the coasts. Interior.] Our knowledge of the A. continent, except in a few places, scarcely goes beyond the outer margin of this great region. The want of extensive creeks and arms of the sea penetrating deeply into the interior, and of any known navigable rivers of high tidal ascent, have opposed great difficulties to inland researches; and as many of the larger rivers were found, so far as examined, to take an inland direction, it was long supposed that a great mediterranean sea, or at least a succession of swamps and marshes, existed in its central regions, into which the waters from the surrounding ridges fall as from the rim of a basin. [Barrow.] Mr. Eyre, one of the latest and best-informed explorers of A., opposes this theory from the consideration that the oppressively hot winds which are occasionally experienced in South A. always blow from the N, or the centre of the continent, and there seems little probability that such winds can have blown over a large expanse of water; secondly, that such aborigines, inhabiting the skirts of the interior, as he has questioned on the subject, invariably declared that they knew of no large inland body of water, but that all is an arid waste; and thirdly, he infers it from the coincidence observable in the aborigines of opposite points of the continent, whilst no such coincidence exists in the physical appearance or character of those natives inhabiting the intermediate lines of coast, making it apparent that there exist across the continent certain direct lines of route. [Geog. Journ. vol. xvi.] It is hard, however, to conceive anything more strangely melancholy than those vast flats in the interior of this country, where rivers stagnate into non-existence in a wilderness of gigantic reeds, and the traveller scarcely finds a knoll high enough to raise him above the waters in the season of floods; or those wide tracts of thick herbaceous brushwood,-fitly called scrubs, --affording nothing either to sustain or solace those who traverse them. [Quart. Rev. No. lxxvi]. Mountains.] The largest continuous range of mountains yet explored in A. takes its rise on the NE coast near Cape York, and thence runs in a SSW direction, but with some interruptions apparently, to Cape Wilson, intersecting the territory of New South Wales in which it divides the waters flowing W into the interior from those which run with shorter courses to the coast. The most northern section of this great range which has yet received examination is called the Liverpool range. It deviates from the general direction of the range by inclining to a W and E direction under the 32d parallel. The upper parts of its ridges are flat; and in some places they present little hills and valleys covered with grass. Its highest summits, which are greenstone, reach an elevation of 4,700 ft. From the 33d parallel, proceeding southwards, the range assumes the name of the Blue mountains. The average breadth of this range to the W of Sydney is 50 m.; and its summit-heights vary from 2,500 to upwards of 4,000 ft. The third section of this great range, the Warragong mountains, or Australian Alps as they have been termed, extends from the parallel of 35° to Bass's straits. As it approaches the strait, its mountains become bolder. The syenitic peak of Mount Kosciusko here attains an elevation of 6,500 ft.; and it is reported that some detached mountains to the W of the dividing range in this section reach the line of perpetual snow. A chain of bold island-peaks in Bass's strait indicates the course of the submerged range until it breaks out again in Van Diemen's Land, where in Mount Humboldt it attains an alt. of 5,520 ft. Its southern point plunges finally into the great Southern ocean, unless indeed we may regard as prolongations of it the vast volcanic peaks in the newly discovered Antarctic land, which pour forth fire within 11° of the pole. [Quart. Rev., No. lxxvi.] Count Strzelecki has given a detailed and elaborate account of the geological phenomena of this mountainchain. These consist, he says, "partly in the character of the mineral masses which form the dividing range, which are composed of granite, sienite, hialo- | Yarra-Yarra flows into the head of Port Phillip; and micte, protogene, quartz-rock, petrosilex, porphyry, serpentinous hornblende, and augitic rocks; partly in the character of the sedimentary rocks, of silicious, calcareous, argillaceous, aluminous, and bituminous character, which are confined to the eastern and western talus of that range, resting on it either in a vertical, inclined, or horizontal position. The main phenomena are referable to epochs of terrestrial revolutions; some relating to periods marked by a partial quiescence, and the deposition of sedimentary rocks; some to perceptible changes in the condition of the organic life inhabiting the sea; some others, again, to catastrophes which swept from the surface of the earth all its animal and vegetable kingdom." On the W side of the continent a range, or rather successive ranges, run almost due N through the whole extent of the country, nearly on the meridian of 116° E, from Point D'Entrecasteaux to Cape Preston, in the neighbourhood of Dampier's archipelago. This range, known as the Darling range, averages from 30 to 40 m. in breadth, but does not seem to attain a higher elevation than that of 2,000 ft. To the E of the Darling range, a higher parallel range has its S termination near to King George's sound. Cape Leuwin is the S termination of a third parallel range inferior in alt. as well as extent to the other two. A lofty range of mountains, discovered by Major Mitchell in 1836, and called by him the Grampians of the South, extends between E long. 142° 25' and 142° 47', and N lat. 36° 52' and 37° 38', between the Glenelg and the Murray rivers. Its highest and most eastern summit, Mount William, has an alt. of 4,500 ft. Rivers.] The greater number of the known rivers of A. are of short course, and cannot drain above a sixth part of the surface of the continent. In the following bare enumeration of them we shall commence at Cape York and proceed southwards along the E coast. Escape river, a small stream, flows into Newcastle bay; but no other stream appears to intersect the low sandy coast until we pass Cape Bedford, a little to the S of which Endeavour river flows through a level country covered with grass and small timber. To the S of this, until we reach the Brisbane river, the coast appears to be watered only by a series of insignificant rivulets. The chief rivers which rise in the dividing range, N of the Liverpool range, and flow to the coast, are the Brisbane, which is navigable for 75 m., and the Logan, both which discharge themselves into the noble estuary of Moreton bay, the Tweed, the Clarence, flowing into Shoal bay, the Macleay, the Hastings, having its outlet at Port Macquaire, and the Manning. Within the territory of New South Wales Proper we have the Karuah flowing into Port Stephens; the Hunter river flowing into Port Hunter; the Hawkesbury, and its tributary the Nepean, flowing into Broken bay; the Shoalhaven flowing into Shoalhaven; the Clyde flowing into Bateman bay; and the Moruya, forming the southern limit of New South Wales Proper. None of these rivers present facilities for a long internal navigation, though several of them have large estuaries and are navigable for a greater or less distance by steam-boats. From the meridian of 150° to 148° little is known of the country lying between the Australian Alps and the coast; but no large streams appear to discharge themselves on this coast until we encounter the common estuary of the Thomson, Riley, and Arthur rivers, on the E confines of Gipps land.
