The Perry, Dunlop, and Barney unite their waters in one stream, which has its outlet farther to the W; and to the W of that the Maconochie and Latrobe unite their waters before falling into the ocean. The Fitzroy, all small streams, flow into Portland bay; and the larger river, the Glenelg, has its mouth, according to Tyers, on the E of the meridian of 141° E, and consequently within the limits of Phillipsland, but, according to Arrowsmith, on the W of that meridian, or within the territory of South Australia. The next great river, the Murray, flowing into the vast shallow lake or lagune Alexandrina, drains, by its complicated water-system, the whole country to the W of the Blue mountains and Australian Alps, and S of the parallel of 28°. Its remotest headbranch, the Darling, rising on the W side of Mount Mitchell, near the head-streams of the Logan, flowing W. The Darling, in its course to the SW, is probably joined on the l. by the Dumaresque, the Gwyder, the Peel, the Castlereagh, the Macquarie, and the Bogan, all flowing to the W of N. The Lachlan, the Morumbidgee, the Murray, and the Bayungun, all flowing in a W direction, unite their waters before they pass the meridian of 143°, and form the Murray, which flows to the WNW, until it receives the tribute of the Darling, when it changes its course to the S of W, and then, after crossing the meridian of 140°, trends to the S, and flows into Lake Alexandrina, which communicates with the sea at Encounter bay, but by a passage impracticable even for small boats, while the lake itself is only a vast shallow lagune whose depth rarely exceeds 4 ft. The Murray, for 50 m. from its mouth, has an average breadth of 350 yards, and is from 20 to 25 ft. deep. There are no other rivers of any considerable size in Southern Australia. The Torrens and the Gawler are small rivers flowing into the E side of the gulf of St. Vincent.—In Western Australia, the Kalgan flows in a S course into the head of King George's sound; Denmark river into Wilson's inlet; the course of the Shannon, further to the W, has not yet been fully traced; Blackwood river flows into Hardy's inlet, at the mouth of which is Port Augusta. On the N coast, proceeding northwards, we find the Preston and Collie rivers flowing into Leschenhault's estuary; the Murray flowing into Peel's inlet; the Canning, a river of considerable length of course, flowing into the SE corner of Melville water; and the Swan, a larger river, flowing into the same bay on the NE. The navigation of both these rivers, however, is impeded by flats, over which, in dry weather, boats must be pushed. The Moore, Arrowsmith, Hutt, and Murchison, all flow by short courses into the ocean, on the W coast of Edel land, now called Victoria. The Gascoyne flows into Shark bay; but between this point of the coast, under the meridian of 114° E, till we reach the Fitzroy river flowing into King sound, on the N coast, in 123° 38' E, we have no river courses yet laid down. The Northern Glenelg was discovered in 1837, by Lieut. Grey, who struck it in S lat. 15° 43', E long. 124° 44', at which point it was salt. Tracing its course they found it quite fresh, and running at the rate of more than 5 knots an hour, with a depth of <math>2\frac{1}{2}</math> fath. close to the bank, in S lat. 15° 41′ 50", E long. 124° 59'. The country here was low and marshy, and from appearances must be subjected to extensive inundations. Victoria river, flowing into Queen's channel, descends from Fitzroy range. At 50 m. from Port Pearce it is a narrow, rapid, rushing stream, flowing between rocky ranges. The Adelaide, flowing into Clarence strait, opposite Melville island, is navigable 50 m. by vessels drawing 22 ft. water. The two Alligators and the Liverpool river also have their outlets on the N coast of Arnheim's land; but between the last-named river and the Albert, at the bottom of the gulf of Carpentaria, no rivers of any considerable volume of water the Geelong into its W side. The Hopkins, Shaw, and | river and Van Diemen's river, the latter in about 17° S. it seems probable, from the latest discoveries of Sir Thomas Mitchell, that the mouth of a great river will be found on the E coast of the gulf, about the parallel of 15°. Lakes.] Lakes are numerous in Australia, but none of them are large, except when under temporary inundation; and many present only a bed of dry rushes after long-continued drought. Brine-pits are frequently met with in the steppes of the interior. Sometimes, in dry seasons, the evaporation greatly increases the saltness of these lakes; and sometimes, after very rainy seasons, they become so diluted as to be almost fresh; while in those which have been made to communicate with surface-currents, so as to receive and discharge a body of water, the original saltwater has been washed out, and they are now fresh. [Mitchell.] Climate.] One-third of the area of A. lies within the torrid zone; the other two-thirds are within the temperate. Little is yet known of the torrid portion. The mean temp. at Sydney on the E coast is rather above 65°; at Perth, on the W coast, rather below 67½°. At Melville island on the N coast the winteraverage in 1827-8 was 80°, that of the summer 86°. In southern A. during winter the extreme ranges in 1837 were between 43° and 60°. The months of December, January, and February, form the summer quarter; March, April, and May, the autumn; June, July, and August, the winter; and September, October, and November, the spring. In the interior, the wet season commonly takes place during summer; on the coast it commences with winter. Dews are frequent and heavy, sometimes falling like a drizzling rain during the summer-heats. Hail-storms occasionally occur; and frosts and heavy snows are frequent in the neighbourhood of the A. alps, and on the elevated flats and downs. The quick transition from heat to cold, and the reverse, is a peculiarity in the climate on all sides of the continent. A more remarkable peculiarity is the periodical unmitigated droughts which occur at intervals of ten or twelve years, and sometimes last for twelve or even twenty-four months. Close upon every such a season follows a year of continuous rain and floods. The sky is generally clear and brilliant; and the atmosphere dry, pure, and elastic; and the climate generally—with the exception perhaps of some parts of the N coast-may be regarded as highly favourable to the human constitution. Endemic diseases are unknown, with the exception of ophthalmia, which is produced by the scorching N wind. Even small-pox, measles, and hoopingcough have not yet been observed. Dysentery is perhaps the most prevalent disease. Typhus and acute fevers are prevalent on the N coast during the wet monsoons. Animal kingdom.] With the exception of the dog, and perhaps the buffalo, the existence of which has been reported, there is not in A. a single indigenous quadruped known on any other continent; and those which do exist here are as remote from all our common analogies and conceptions as can well be imagined. "There is something," says a quarterly reviewer, "so strangely different in the physical constitution of A. from that of every other part of the world,—we meet with so many whimsical deviations, on the two islands of New Holland and Van Diemen's Land, from the ordinary rules and operations of Nature in the animal and vegetable parts of the creation,-that he must be a dull traveller indeed, who does not glean something new and amusing from these regions which are yet so imperfectly known to us. Thus we have in one or other, or both of these colonies, birds without wings as large as deer, their bodies covered have yet been discovered, after passing Flinders' with hair instead of feathers,—beasts with the beaks of birds, swans that are black, and eagles white. Here, too, we find the ferns, nettles, and even grasses, growing to the size and shape of trees,-rivers running from the sea, and lost in interior swamps,—trees that are ever green in spite of frost or snow,-extensive plains on which, as one writer tells us, one tree, one soil, one water, and one description of bird, fish, or animal, prevails alike for ten miles and for one hundred." "This is New Holland," says Field, "where it is summer with us, when it is winter in Europe, and vice versa; where the barometer rises before bad weather, and falls before good; where the north is the hot wind, and the south the cold; where the humblest house is fitted up with cedar; where the fields are fenced with mahogany, and myrtle-trees are burnt for fire-wood; where the kangaroo, -an animal between the squirrel and the deer,—has five claws on its fore-paws, and three talons on its hind legs, like a bird, and yet hops on its tail; where the mole lays eggs, and has a duck's bill; where there is a bird with a broom in its mouth instead of a tongue; where there is a fish, one-half belonging to the genus raia, and the other to that of squalus: where the pears are made of wood, with the stalk at the broader end; and where the cherry grows with the stone on the outside." Captain Hunter describes birds with the head, form, and plumage of the parrot, and the long slender legs of the sea-gull; and others with the legs and feet of a parrot, the head and neck made and coloured like the common sea-gull, and the wings and tail of a hawk. He also states himself to have seen trees bearing three different kinds of leaves, and to have found others bearing the leaf of the gumtree, with the gum exuding, and covered with the bark of a different kind. The whole number of A. species, in the class mammalia, does not exceed 60, or about a twenty-fourth part of the number of known quadrupeds; and of these one-half are marsupialia. There are no beasts of prey in A.; neither lions, tigers, leopards, hyænas, nor any other description of animals dangerous to man have been found, excepting a few of the serpent-tribe. There is a species of animal called the flying-fox; it is a sort of bat of immense size and hideous appearance, but perfectly harmless. Native dogs, native cats, opossums, and squirrels are numerous. The kangaroo (macropus) is the characteristic animal of the A. colonies, and although ranked among the number of quadrupeds, might be quite as properly described as a biped, its fore feet and legs possessing more of the properties of hands and arms, and being never used in the process of walking or running, except when the animal is feeding. There are a great variety of species of this animal. The larger are not so swift as the smaller size, and seldom run when closely pursued by dogs, but place their backs against a tree, and, using their tails as a support, offer formidable resistance with their hind legs, which are armed with a most formidable claw, with which they sometimes strike the dogs and rip them open; or, in the absence of trees, they will run to a water-hole, wade up to their middle, and then seize the heads of the dogs as they approach and thrust them under the water until they are drowned. Their tails are of immense strength and thickness, and are often 4 or 5 feet in length, and as much as 20 lbs in weight. The females are furnished with a pouch, in which they carry their young. The kangaroo is a sagacious animal, and when tame becomes much attached to those who take notice of it: it will follow them, sit in their laps, and answer to its name like a child. Their skins are tanned for leather, and are used with the hair on for making rugs, which those who travel in the bush use for a bed. There is a smaller species, called a kangaroo-rat, somewhat like a kangaroo in the face, but seldom exceeding the size of a rabbit. The wombat (phascoromys), is a small animal between a kangaroo and opossum. It has four very short legs, and burrows in the sand-hills of the interior. Flying opossums and flying squirrels are a genus exclusively Australian, of which there are five or six species. The dasyures and thylacynes, in their habits and modes of life resemble the martins, polecats, and weasels of Europe. The domestic animals of Europe are now numerous in A.; and their diseases are said to be fewer and more easily healed than in Europe. The introduction of the camel from India has been suggested. This animal having been successfully imported into the Mauritius, might doubtless be brought to Port Essington, and thence generally introduced. The best camel, as a beast of burden, is of the Marwarri breed, and is purchasable in India at 600 to 1,000 rupees, or £6 to £10. Being a browsing rather than a grazing animal it is easily sustained by leaves or young branches gathered by itself en route, or brought to it by a careful driver, who can easily manage three of these animals. They travel in single file, the nose of one being attached by a rope through the cartilage to the crupper of another; and carry 400 lb. if very moderately laden, up to 700 lb. or 800 lb. upon emergency, averaging 31 miles an hour. For the purpose of an expedition, or a long journey in A., therefore, a band of 6 camels would carry 1,600 lb. of provision and kit, and 800 lb. of water in skin bottles. Like the horse, the camel breeds annually, produces one at a birth, and seems adapted to perform good services in journeying the most sandy and scrubby wastes of Australia. The ornithology of A. is somewhat less anomalous than its mammalogy, but contains many new and singular forms, and many of the most beautiful and varied plumage. The parrots in particular are a very numerous class; and the wattled talegalla, or brush-turkey (Talègalla Lathami), has a wide range; honey-eaters, hawks, eagles, regent-birds, satin-birds, wood-pigeons, full-necks (Calodera nuchalis), emirs, straw-necked ibises (Ibis spinicollis), and fan-tails, are numerous; and the surrounding seas "literally teem with the feathered race." [Gould.] Of reptiles, there are several genera of beetles, a species of crocodile or alligator, several species of lizards, and a considerable number of snakes, both venomous and harmless. Vegetation.] It has been remarked, that New Holland presents neither the majesty of the virginforests of the New world, - nor the variety and elegance of those of Asia, -nor the delicacy and freshness of the woods in the temperate countries of Europe. Vegetation is here in general dark and sombre: along the immense line of coast vegetation presents a dull, monotonous, olive-green colour, in every part, and at all seasons of the year; and as far as discovery has yet penetrated, the same monotony strikes and wearies the sense. [Quart. Rev. No. lxxvi.] From the great extent of latitude through which this country passes, we must conclude that those parts which lie nearer the tropic and the equator are capable of yielding products suited to the torrid zone; and that those, on the contrary, which have a higher southern latitude will exhibit the vegetation of colder countries. Forest-timber, brushwood, and grass, are not formed here into zones according to their elevation, as in countries of more uniform surface and severe climate: it is the soil chiefly that determines the native vegetation of Australia. The plants of A. are rather novel than useful. Four-fifths of them, according to Cunningham, are Eucalyptæ, or gum-trees, with their innumerable species and gigantic forms, and other genera of Myrtaceæ. One of them, the blue-gum tree, received its botanical appellation, Eucalyptus piperita, from its yielding an oil that in its nature much resembles our oil of peppermint. Another, the red-gum tree, is so uncommonly productive of resin,—as much as sixty gallons flowing from a single tree,—that it has got the name of Eucalyptus resinifera. The leafless acacias form another numerous family. The native fruit-trees are in general bad, and scarce produce anything worth eating,-but all those that have been transplanted here have thriven beyond calculation: the orange yields its golden fruits, the vine its ruby clusters, and peaches are so plenty that Wentworth tells us he has seen hogs fed on them. The vine indeed thrives luxuriantly; and before many years have passed, the wines of Australia, from the refined claret to the household white wines, will constitute a chief export. The olive also grows luxuriantly, and will soon repay those who turn their attention to the export of its oil. The fig-tree puts forth all its branches, and promises to return a thousand-fold for its importation; and the present generation may live to see the dried fruits of this new region rivalling the fruits of France and Italy. Maize, tobacco, and tea, will hereafter be objects of attention: and in the northern districts, sugar, cotton, indigo, and spices, will rival those of all other countries where they are cultivated. "Looking at the singular and distinctive features by which organic life is here characterized, making this continent as it were a world apart, we cannot but wonder that the same climate under which that life appears, should be likewise so well-adapted to the maintenance of the vegetation and the animals of other hemispheres." [Strzelecki.] If, however, the Englishman should be inclined to form a too "favourable opinion of the capabilities of the country for the growth of fruit where the orange and the grape flourish and yield abundantly in the open air, it will do him no harm to remember that while the Australian colonists gain the orange and the grape, they lose the apple, the currant, the gooseberry, and that most delicious of all fruits, the strawberry. As it is with fruits so it is with flowers. The native flowers are many of them exceedingly beautiful, and the geranium is almost a weed; but very many of the sweetest and most beautiful English flowers will not grow in the climate of New Holland. The native flowers are, with very few exceptions, perfectly inodorous, and they gladden the eye with their grateful presence but for a short period. The dreary wastes are relieved by the varied tints of the native flowers in the spring time only. But few persons, I apprehend, would estimate the beautiful, but scentless native flowers of New Holland beyond the more quiet-tinted, but sweet-smelling flowers of Great Britain. Even were they on a par in point of beauty and fragrance, the English flowers continue blooming a great part of the year, whilst the dull monotony of the arid shrub of A. is relieved for only a short time by beautifully formed and exquisitely tinted, but inodorous flowers. With all the charm of form, the A. flowers must yield to the delicious fragrance and simple colouring of the flowers of the charming hedgerows of 'merry England." [Bartlett.] Aborigines.] The aboriginal tribes of A. are, as already remarked, a branch of the Ethiopic, or black race, and one of the most degraded. They are supposed to belong to the Alforian race, but this fact has not yet been absolutely determined. [Prichard.] Perhaps no people in the world have made less progress towards civilization. The features of the women are not altogether unpleasant. They are distinguished by the want of two joints of the little finger of the left hand. Some of them are almost as black in complexion as the African Negroes, others are copper-coloured; their hair is generally long, and not woolly like that of the Africans. They have flattened or aquiline noses, wide nostrils, hollow eyes, thick eyebrows, thick lips, large mouths, and white regular teeth. Their huts are rudely constructed of the bark of trees, in the shape of kilns. Polygamy is very generally practised amongst them. They have very faint notions of a future state, believing that at death they shall either roam through the regions of the air, or return to the clouds from which they originally came—a strange notion, common to them with the Alforas in the island of Ceram. They employ charms against thunder and lightning: and pretend to foresee future events by the meteors called falling stars. In the estuaries and rivers, and on the coast, there is abundance of fish at certain periods of the year; and kangaroos of various sorts with opossums, and other small animals, are obtained in considerable numbers; these, when they can be procured, form their food. Roots and gums of several kinds are also used by them, and birds' eggs, lizards, frogs, grubs, and cray-fish from the swamps, are resorted to as varieties, or in cases of urgent want. Their habits preclude the possibility of keeping any accumulated stock of the necessary articles, and therefore their time and attention are almost constantly occupied in the pursuit of their daily food. As they have no fixed habitation, and do not practise any art tending to increase the supply which nature has provided, it is probable that their numbers are limited by this circumstance, and that they have been long stationary at their present amount. The law which thus forbids any further increase, is the cause, moreover, of their dispersion throughout the territory, and prevents them from entering into any larger confederacies than those which are necessary for rendering most successful their hunting and fishing occupations. The tribes—or gals, as they are called—usually comprise about 120 persons, of all ages and both sexes; these are connected for the most part by relationship of blood, although it is by no means uncommon to procure wives or to adopt strangers from neighbouring tribes. The only species of control or government under which they live is founded on the influence of the strong over the weak, and the deference which is shown to the aged, and to the pretended powers of the magician or doctor. Certain usages established by custom are frequently appealed to as rules of conduct. Of these, the principal relate to the right of individuals to certain portions of hunting-ground, derived by inheritance from their immediate ancestors; to the practice of boring the cartilage of the nose of the young men on their admission to the rights of manhood; and to retaliation for injuries received, which all are enjoined as well as entitled to seek whether the offender belong to the same or to a neighbouring community. One or two coincidences of customs between the Australians and North American Indians have been noted. Each family in A. adopts as their crest or sign,— kobong the natives call it,—some animal or vegetable; and so great is their reverence for this, that they will not kill the animal, nor eat the vegetable, except with certain restrictions, respectively represented by their family kobong. In the 'Archaiologia Americana' the same custom of taking some animal as a tribe or family symbol is stated to prevail. "Each tribe has the name of some animal. Among the Hurons, the first tribe is that of the bear; the two others of the wolf and turtle." The Tartars have the same rude heraldry. Among the Australians, Captain Grey ascertained that the children of either sex always take the family name of their mother, and that a man cannot marry a woman of his own family name. These institutions are completely coincident with similar ones inviolably maintained among the North American Indians. It has been found very difficult to ascertain the exact locality or tribe to which individuals belong, in consequence of the alliances which are frequent amongst individuals of different tribes; this species of brotherhood by adoption, carries with it the obligation of becoming parties to each other's quarrels, and although it appears to be followed by the advantage of mutual protection, as far as such individuals are concerned, it gives rise at the same time to many hostilities. The intercourse between tribes is seldom of a friendly character; but it is remarkable that their conflicts seldom extend to the loss of lives. Independent of these occasional warlike meetings of tribes, however, almost every native is under an engagement to avenge, at a convenient opportunity, the death of some departed friend, or an insult previously offered to himself; this purpose, which he cannot forego without discredit, gives rise to acts of the greatest treachery, and not unfrequently ends in the surprise and sudden death of some individual belonging to the same tribe with the avenger. The spear seldom quits their hand from boyhood till death; and they become accustomed to witness, endure, and practise the greatest outrages. The personal qualities of some members of this peculiar race are superior to the condition in which they live; a few of them are remarkable for symmetry of form and countenance, and the natural intelligence of many appears to be in the highest degree acute. The greater part, however, are, from hardship of life, and bodily injuries, disgusting specimens of the human race; and the deformity of old age, whether in the men or women, is usually accompanied by a concentration of all the vicious propensities to which their usages give rise. In their intercourse with the whites, they accommodate themselves with astonishing readiness to the language, the habits, and even the weaknesses of their new friends. They are remarkably cheerful, and make themselves useful in many employments; but they are not to be relied upon, for in a great many instances it has been found, that after living for months in the house of a settler, they have been all along employed by the rest of the tribe as spies, for the purpose of conveying intelligence as to the best point of attack on life or property. Living in a constant state of warfare, they are bold, crafty, and persevering, and lay their plans with judgment, equal to the vigour with which they put them into execution. With such qualities as these, they would be too powerful for the present number of colonists, if it were not for their mistrust of each other; they cannot combine their efforts, nor act on a concentrated plan; for if they were to do so, there are many of them who would readily betray the rest, and voluntarily lead the whites to their retreats for the sake of seem to confine it to the female alone: "Of these, the most remarkable, and that which most directly bears upon the question, is the result of a union between an aboriginal female and an European male. Whenever this takes place, the native female is found to lose the power of conception on a renewal of intercourse with the male of her own race, retaining only that of procreating with the white men. Hundreds of instances of this extraordinary fact are on record in the writer's memoranda, all tending to prove that the sterility of the female being relative only to one, and not to another male, is not accidental, but follows laws as cogent, though as mysterious, as the rest of those connected with generation. To this direct, though occult case, of the decrease of the aboriginal race may be added others. Of these, the leucorrhœa, a general complaint, raging with unusual severity and aggravated symptoms, affords a most extraordinary instance; not only because its character might be mistaken by many observers for a modified elephantiasis, but because its origin amongst the native women dates precisely from the arrival of the European females amongst them." Colonization.] We are not aware, that, from the days of De Brosses, the colonization of A. occupied the notice of Europeans, until necessity compelled the British government to look out for some distant establishment. It had been the practice of Great Britain, during many years, to transport felons to the American plantations; but the transactions which ended in the separation of the N American colonies from the mother-country, rendered it necessary to provide another place for a similar purpose. Most of the southern navigators had approached the coast of New Holland where it is bleak and barren, and had drawn disadvantageous conclusions concerning it. The discoveries of Cook, however, showed that its eastern parts were rich in vegetable productions, that the soil was good, and the climate agreeable; and government, after having sought in vain for a suitable place on the coast of Africa to receive transported criminals, determined in 1785 and 1786, that part of Australia should be chosen as a settlement. In the subsequent year, several vessels laden with convicts, and also carrying out the members of a civil government, sailed for Botany bay. They reached it in safety; and having founded a town in the vicinity, began to cultivate the country. The outlines of a scheme for the future government of our Australian colonies has recently been promulgated by the home-government. One of the governors of these colonies, it is proposed, shall be constituted governor-general of A., with authority to convene a body of representatives to be called 'the General Assembly of A.' This assembly is to be a single house of delegates, consisting of not less than 20, nor more than 30 members, who are to be rateably chosen by the different Australian legislatures; but the first convocation of the body is not to take place until the governor-general shall have been requested, by addresses from at least two of the legislatures, to exercise that power. It is proposed to leave the church of England undisturbed in its present endowment; but to raise the grants to other religious communities in proportion to their numbers; and thereafter to leave the further endowment of religion to the colonial legislatures. The salaries of the judges, and some other public servants, are to be reserved under the protection of the Crown. Steam communication.] A charter has recently been granted to the 'India and Australian Steam-packet company' by the establishment of which these sections of the British empire will be brought within 30 days' distance of each other; and the whole of our settlements in the E hemisphere and the S Pacific will be united by steam-navigation with the parent-state. The route proposed by the company is from one of the southern English ports by Gibraltar to Alexandria, with—on the completion of the railways to Trieste—a short cut over the continent for those who prefer time to money. From Alexandria the route will be up the Red sea to Aden, turning off one branch to the Mauritius and another to Bombay; but the principal route will be onward to Ceylon, from thence throwing off branch-steamers to Madras and Calcutta, which, coasting the bay of Bengal, will again join the principal line at Singapore, in 42 days from England, a distance of 8,390 m., or according to another calculation of 8,488 m., and go on to Java. I.—EASTERN AUSTRALIA.—Eastern Australia, or New South Wales, embraces, in its widest acceptation, the whole eastern side of the island of New Holland, from Wide-bay, under the parallel of 26° S lat., to Cape Howe, the SE extremity of the continent, a distance of 1,000 m., and thence along the coast for 500 m. to the 141st meridian, which separates it from the new colony of South Australia. The Pacific, or Great Southern ocean, separates it from Peru and Chili, distant about 5,000 m. It is divided into three districts: 1st, New South Wales Proper, or the Middle district, the capital of which is Sydney, the seat also of the general government; 2d, Port Phil-

LIP, or the Southern district, the capital of which is Melbourne; and 3d, Moreton Bay, or the Northern district, the principal town in which is Brisbane. Progress of discovery.] A period of 25 years passed away before the colonists of New South Wales succeeded in their attempts to penetrate beyond the Blue mountains, which were considered an impregnable barrier betwixt them and the interior districts. In 1813, stimulated by the entire failure of the pasturage throughout the colony, an exploring party succeeded in penetrating to a distance of 25 geog. m. due W from the Nepean river, by a route exceeding 50 m. in length, whence from the rugged brow of a dreary precipitous height they looked down upon a grassy wellwatered vale extending to the W. The downs of Bathurst, and the rivers Macquarie and Lachlan were shortly afterwards discovered; and in the following year, a practicable line of road, 148 m. in length from Sydney, was constructed over the mountain-ridge, in a direct line nearly WNW, and in some parts rising 3,400 ft. above sea-level, by which the illimitable range of sheep and cattle pasturage on the W side of the Blue mountains was made available to the colony. In 1817-18, Mr. Oxley explored 400 m. inland, to the meridian of 1441° E, beyond the westernmost range of hills to an interior steppe-like country, presenting a dead level, bounded by the horizon, and only 250 ft. above the sea. It was expected that the Lachlan river would be easily traced to its mouth on the coast; and that the Macquarie would be found to unite with it; but both rivers long baffled all attempts to follow their further progress through the reedy morasses into which they had been traced by Mr. Oxley's exploring parties. In 1819, the Wombat-brush in Argyle was passed, and a third river, also flowing inland, and called by the aborigines Morrumbidgee, was discovered, and soon afterwards the Brisbane downs, to the E of the meridian of 149°, on the W side of the coast-range of hills, and at an alt. of at least 2,000 ft. above sea-level. In 1824, an exploring party passed to the E of the Wurragong chain, and holding a course to the SW, discovered the Hume and Goulburn rivers, and at last struck the S coast on the NE side of the large bay of Port Phillip. In 1827, the tract of discovery turned to the N, and a well-appointed expedition, under Mr. Cunningham, set out to explore the unknown country on the W side of the dividing range, between Hunter's river, in S lat. 32°, and Moreton bay, in 27°. After traversing a waste of forbiddingly arid character, the expedition discovered an extensive tract of clear pastoral country, subsequently named Darling downs, in about the mean parallel of 28° S, along which they stretched 18 m. to the meridian of 152°. To the N of these, long patches of timberless land, were named Peel's plains, and others to the S and SE Canning's plains. In 1826-7, Captain Sturt, after a season of severe drought, succeeded in exploring the dried-up surfaces of the Macquarie marshes, and ascertained that below Mount Harris the Macquarie ceases to be a river, having no banks or continued channel, but diffusing itself over a succession of levels and inclinations, until in its highest floods it throws its waters into the channel of the Castlereagh river. Captain Sturt also traced the Darling river to a point in S lat 30° 16', E long. 144° 50'. In 1829-30, Captain Sturt traced the course of the Morrumbidgee, through a country of level and monotonous aspect, till it delivered its waters into a broad and noble river of a medium width of from 300 to 400 ft., and setting to the W at the rate of 2½ m. an hour. After nine days' voyage down this new river, which they called the Murray, without observing the least rise in the level of the country throughout about 100 m. of westing, they passed the mouth of a stream flowing from N by S, in E long. 141°, S lat. 34°, which they ascended some distance, and which they conjectured might be the Darling river. Continuing the descent of the Murray, on the 32d day of their voyage they entered Alexandrina lake. [Cunningham in Geog. Journ., vol. ii.] In 1836, Major Mitchell succeeded in tracing the Darling into the Murray; and the Lochlan's channel into the Morrumbidgee. The following notices of the recent progress of discovery in A. chiefly relate to the country lying between the N extremity of New South Wales and the northern coast of A., and may be most conveniently inserted here. The Clarence river district.] The Clarence river was discovered in 1838, by some persons engaged in cutting cedar. It falls into the sea in about 29° 20' S lat., at Shoal bay. Its mouth is 340 m. to the N of Sydney, and 90 m. to the S of Moreton bay. For about 15 m. up the river, writes Captain Perry, the soundings varied from 3 to 5 fath., the average breadth being nearly a quarter of a mile; owing to the denseness of the brush on the banks, AUSTRALIA. no part of the country could be seen from the deck of the vessel which was not completely screened by a mass of the most luxuriant vegetation. The stems of gigantic trees, covered with climbing plants of various descriptions which fell down in graceful festoons from the upper branches, produced an effect observable only in a region fresh from the hand of nature. In further navigating the river several estuaries were observed, and the river was found to be divided into two branches by an island of about 120 sq. m. in extent. The steamer's further progress was put an end to by a rapid, having made about 85 m. by the course of the river, which for boats is navigable some 15 m. higher up. The probable results of the discovery of the Clarence river are thus collected by the deputy surveyor-general:-"As a field for the employment of capital in agricultural and commercial pursuits, the opening of a tract of fine country so situated, with respect to the stations to the westward, is contemplated with satisfaction by those who may be considered competent judges. In the lower part of the district alone there is room for a large body of industrious emigrants; and such is the nature of the soil, that little apprehension is entertained of its affording abundant compensation for the labour that may be bestowed on the cultivation of wheat, maize, the vine, tobacco, sugar, indigo, and many other articles of consumption, and even of export. The height of the neighbouring mountains so near the coast insures to this quarter some protection from the hot winds that prevail to a great extent in more exposed parts, and to their proximity to the coast may be attributed the frequency of showers in times when other districts are parched with drought. The connexion with Moreton bay, where the survey is steadily progressing, is already established by the discovery of a practicable route between the coast-range and the sea. The survey of the N bank of the M'Leay river is also in progress; so that the whole country, extending on the N from Moreton bay to the coast-range, on the W from Cunningham's gap to the crest of the Liverpool range, on the S from the sources of the Peel to the coast in lat. 32°, and on the E to lat. 28° 5' (Moreton bay), comprising an area of about 40,000 sq. m. (25,600,000 acres), may be said to be in a state of preparation, as regards the survey, for immediate location." Route from Moreton bay to Victoria.] Dr. Leichhardt, on the 17th December, 1845, accomplished an overland journey from Moreton bay to Port Essington in North A. He describes the whole coast as being backed by ranges of mountains consisting, nearest the sea, generally of granite and basaltic rocks, which he calls the granite range; behind this is a second range consisting of sandstone, which he calls the peak-range. Descending from this and again rising, they entered upon the table-land, which he could nowhere penetrate so as to determine the character of the central country. It was covered with a dense scrub, and had no water; and frequently there was difficulty in descending from it, being generally bounded by perpendicular cliffs passable only where there are ravines which have been probably cut by the water in periods of rain. The courses of the rivers were at first from S to N to about S lat. 21°, where an extensive stream, the Burdekin, coming from NNW, was struck. The travellers ascended a section of tableland in about S lat. 18° 20', E long. 143° 30', but found a dip northwards. The course of another river, which they called the Lynd, was struck and followed to lat. 16° 45', where a larger stream from the eastward joins it, which they named the Mitchell; and now a broad expanse of water was followed down to 16° 15' lat., where it appeared to trend gradually to the NW. They were now in about the meridian of Cape York, and moving directly westward they reached the shores of the great gulf of Carpentaria, and then turning to the southward followed the coast, as nearly as circumstances would admit, until they reached 139° long., having crossed or headed five rivers, all running nearly perpendicular to the coast. In about 18° lat. they found themselves again on the table-land, and found no descent until, in 135° long., and 16° 30' lat., they came upon the head of a river, and followed it down to the coast, to the shores of Limmen bight. In this vicinity extensive salt-basins were discovered, with a store of superior salt naturally formed. They now made an attempt to travel westward; and having reached the table-land-which at this spot appears to approach nearer to the sea-the party boldly shaped a course for Port Essington. They suffered more here than in all their former experience. There was no water, and no descending again; but they passed along the verge of stupendous cliffs, with a delightful looking country below them. At length, in a state of great exhaustion, they opportunely reached the dip towards the Alligator river, having been necessarily thrown out of their direct route. The country at the head of and along the Alligator, surpassed in fertility and beauty every thing they had seen. Dr. Leichhardt pronounces a decided opinion that no line of road can be effected direct from Fort Bourke to this settlement; he believes the central land to be elevated, flat, and dry; and that the continuous basin, which many theorists have supposed, between the gulfs of Carpentaria and Spencer, has no existence. It appears from the discoveries made in this expedition, that a route from Moreton-bay to the gulf of Carpentaria will be attainable. The accessibility of the country below the table-land along the N coast, however, remains problematical; and it is not known what may be the state of the table-land during the rainy season. It was traversed at the worst period, just before the rains commence. The country through which the expedition passed, in a journey of 3,000 m., contains much available land for the stockholders of New South Wales. Partly, perhaps, owing to the peculiar temperature of the southern hemisphere; partly, also, to the elevation of the land above the ocean-level, it is, - though principally within the tropics, -wonderfully adapted to the European constitution. A large and valuable portion of this region is occupied by the valleys of the Belyando, flowing from the S, and the Burdekin from the N, wnich uniting on the 147th meridian, and between the parallels of 20 and 21 south, at the distance of a degree and a half of longitude from the coast, assume an easterly course, and join the sea near Point Upstart. This accessible and available country cannot fail to be soon occupied by our enterprising countrymen in the southern hemisphere. There are scarcely any natives to be troubled by their presence, or to trouble them in return. The distance from Sydney, the homogeneous character of the district, point it out as a fitting locality for an independent settlement. Discoveries in the Interior.] Sir T. Mitchell, surveyor-general of New South Wales, started in the beginning of 1846, with a view to explore an overland route from Sydney to Port-Essington. Having reached the junction of the Macquarie with the Darling in E. long. 147° 33', S lat. 30° 6', a few miles higher up he found a good ford across the Darling, and advancing over a fine open country reached the Narran swamp at 26 m. from the Darling. This swamp appears to be almost an exact counterpart of the marshes of the Macquarie. The Balonne, which Sir Thomas describes as in E long. 148° 21', and S lat. 28° 31', only inferior to the Murray in breadth, separates to the south of that point into various channels. The most westerly and main branch is the Culgoa, which joins the Darling about 30 m. above Fort Bourke. The minor branches, in their order from W to E, are the Bokhara, the Ballandoola, the Birree, and the Narran. The three first-named, there seems reason to believe, re-unite and join the Darling, about 40 or 50 m. above Fort Bourke. The Narran terminates in the swamp of the same name. Sir Thomas remarks: "The Narran seems a wonderful provision of nature for the supply and retention of water in a dry and parched country. The division of the main river into others already mentioned, is no less so-irrigating thus from one principal channel extensive regions of rich earth beyond the Darling, while the surplus, or overflow, instead of passing, as in common cases, to the sea, is received in the deep channel of the Narran, and thereby conducted to that extensive reservoir where, on rock or stiff clay, and under ever-verdant polygonum, it furnishes an inexhaustible supply for the support of animal life." The expedition ascended the Narran and the Balonne till it reached a natural bridge of rocks, in E long. 148° 46', and S lat. 28° 2'. Here it halted to form a depot, while a party examined the country to the NW, which proved poor and sandy. On the 23d of April, Sir Thomas resumed his course up the banks of the river. From the abundance of water in the river, it was inferred that it must have other tributaries besides the Condamine. The Cogoon, a small tributary of the Balonne, was traced upwards to an isolated hill range, the centre of which is in E long. 149° 2′, and S lat. 26° 23'. The NW summit of these elevations—Bindango—is connected, by a low neck of grassy downs, with small knolls of traprock belonging to one of the masses of the coast-range, in which the Balonne appears to have its source: "Northward from Bindango," says Sir Thomas, "other waters fall to the NW, and I perceived in the remote distance one gap in a tabular sort of rocky country, through which I hoped the water-course would lead; but I was disappointed in following it down, for this promising little river—the Amby of the natives—turned to the S of W, and I found in the gap only a convenient pass for our carts to the interior country; it was, however, a very remarkable opening, in which were several conical hills, on which grew many strange shrubs, and one of the hills consisted of basalt. I named this St. George's pass, in hopes it may yet become a point on an important line of route. The country through which this pass led, consisted in general of sandstone."-Next day a river flowing to the SW, and fully as large as the Darling, was discovered. It is the Maranoa of the natives. Extensive reconnaissances convinced Sir Thomas that the course of this river was not favourable to the direction of his journey: forcing its way through the rocks, it flowed steadily to the SW. It was resolved, however, to trace it upwards by the r. bank, and for that purpose Sir Thomas, with a part of his men and four months' provisions, started on the 4th. Passing several tributaries they reached a chain of volcanic summits connected with a mass of table-land, which they called Hope's table-land. A pass between this and a higher range towards the coast was penetrated along a stream which, after flowing some space to the NW, turned like the rest to the SW. Hence a long ridge to the N brought Sir Thomas to another chain of mountains extending W about the 25th parallel. The surrounding country is thus described: "Beyond that range, whose summits are all of trap-rock, I found deep sandstone gullies, and in following down one of these I reached an extensive grassy valley, which terminated in a reedy lake, in a more open country. The lake was supplied by springs arising in a swamp at the gorge of the valley which supported a flowing stream of the purest water. This stream spread into the extensive reedy lake, and, to my surprise, was absorbed by it, at least so as to escape through some subterraneous outlet, for the channel of the river in which the lake terminated was dry. The country is adorned by hills of the most romantic form, presenting outlines which surpass in picturesque beauty the fairest creations of the painter. Several pyramids mark the spot where the springs were first discovered. Lower down appear, over the woods, isolated rocks resembling ruined castles, temples, and Gothic cathedrals: others have apertures through them, and the trees being also very varied, and graceful in form, and rich in colour, contribute so much to the beauty of the scenery, that I have been induced to distinguish AUSTRALIA the river and lake by the name of a painter. Returning to the party, we soon brought the carts and dray down the sandstone cliffs to the banks of the Salvator, and pursued that river downwards until I discovered-which was soon obvious-that its course turned to the E of N, consequently that we were upon a river falling to the eastern coast." To another river of a milder character was given the name of Claude. The Salvator was crossed in lat. 24° 31' S; the Claude about 10 m. further on. The Nogoa, the river formed by their union, flows to the NE, and is conjectured to reach the sea about Broad sound. A difficult sandstone country succeeded. On emerging from its ravines, a river-the Belyando-was struck, flowing where first seen to the NW. The expedition encamped on it in E long. 147° 17', and S lat. 24°. After following its channel as far N as 21° 30' S, it turned to the NE, and was recognised to be the Cape river of Leichhardt. "I have since ascertained," says Sir Thomas, "that we were still on the seaward side of the division of the interior waters; or rather that the eastern coast range, hitherto supposed to extend from Wilson's promontory to Cape York, is only imaginary; while the estuaries of two important rivers, affording easy access from the eastern coast to the rich plains of the interior, are realities which have remained undiscovered. That there was no feature deserving the name of a coast range to the westward of the Belyando, was evident from the absence of any tributaries of importance; the sandy-channels of water-courses from that quarter having had no effect in changing the course, or character of the river, which last was very peculiar and remarkable, especially in its habit of spreading into several chains of ponds surrounded by brigalow scrub, apparently a provision of nature for the preservation of surface-water, resembling the net-work of rivers in the south. On the banks of one of these tributaries we found some trees seen by us nowhere else. One was a true fig-tree, having small leaves, and with the fruit fully developed and ripening. The water abounded with the harlequin fish, identical with those in the Maranoa." No time was lost by the party in retracing their steps to the camp on the Salvator, to resume their search for waters flowing to the gulf of Carpentaria. Sir Thomas ascertained, when far up the Balonne, that they were but little higher than the bed of the Darling; that the Narran has scarcely any inclination at all; that the Belyando, at the lowest point attained, was not 600 ft. above the level of the sea; and that the range, under the parallel of 25°S, was the highest they had crossed. Their route across it was in E long. 147° 23', where the mean height above the sea exceeds 2,000 ft.; yet this they were only made aware of by the extreme cold, or by the barometer, for there was nothing in the appearance of the country to lead to such a conclusion. On almost every clear night, Fahrenheit's therm. fell to 9°, and occasionally at 4 A. M. the mercury was as low as 7°. Resuming his journey with a small party, the surveyorgeneral reached a gap in a westerly range, connected with hills to the northward, in E long. 146° 42', S lat. 24° 50': "On ascending the range early next morning, I saw," he says, " open downs and plains, with a line of river in the midst, the whole extending to the NNW as far as the horizon. Following down the little stream from the valley in which I had passed the night I soon reached the open country, and during ten successive days I pursued the course of that river, through the same sort of country, each day as far as my horse could carry me, and in the same direction, again approaching the tropic of Capricorn. In some parts the river formed splendid reaches, as broad and important as the river Murray; in others it spread into four or five channels, some of them several miles apart; but the whole country is better watered than any other portion of A. I have seen, by numerous tributaries arising in the downs. The soil consists of rich clay, and the hollows give birth to water-courses in most of which water was abundant. I found at length, that I might travel in any direction and find water at hand, without having to seek the river, except when I wished to ascertain its general course, and observe its character. The grass consists of panicum and several new sorts, one of which springs green from the old stem. The plains were verdant; indeed, the luxuriant pasturage surpassed in quality, as it did in extent, anything of the kind I had ever seen. The myall tree and the salt-bush-acacia pendula, and salsolæ—are also there. New birds and new plants mark this out as an essentially different region from any I had previously ex plored; and although I could not follow the river throughout its long course at that advanced season, I was convinced that its estuary was in the gulf of Carpentaria: at all events, the country is open and well-watered for a direct route thereto. That the river is the most important of A., increasing as it does, by successive tributaries, and not a mere product of distant ranges, admits of no dispute; and the downs and plains of Central A., through which it flows, seem sufficient to supply the whole world with animal food. The natives are few and inoffensive. I crossed the river at the lowest point I reached, in a great southern bend, in long. 144° 34' E, lat. 24° 14' S, and from rising ground behind the N bank, I could trace its downward course far to the northward. I saw no calltris-the pine of the colonists-in all the country; but a range showing sandstone cliffs appeared to the S, in about 145° E long., S lat. 24° 30'." From this point Sir Thomas hastened back to rejoin his comrades. Mr Kennedy's inquiries amongst the natives led to a very important discovery, namely: that the Maranoa turns S about 30 m. below where he had his camp, and joins the Balonne. Gipps' Land.] To the southward of Sydney there exists a range of hills named the A. alps, but, in the colony, sometimes called the Snowy mountains. Beyond these a cattle-station had been pushed; but, with the exception of the few miles of distance along which the persons connected with that station had necessarily travelled, the country between the mountains and the sea was, until the month of March 1840, utterly unknown. The Count Streleski, a Polish exile, and his two friends, Messrs. Riley and Macarthur, have made a most important addition to the general stock of knowledge respecting A., by the discovery and description of a rich pastoral and agricultural country in this quarter, previously untrodden by any white man, and which in honour of the governor, the Count named Gipps' Land. "Scarcely any spot I know," says the Count, "either within or without the boundaries of New South Wales, on a large or small scale, can boast more advantages than Gipps' land. On an extent of 5,600 sq. m. it has upwards of 250 m. of sea-coast; two already known harbours, that of Corner inlet, and Western port,-besides those for small craft which more than probably exist where her rivers disembogue; eight rivers, in addition to a navigable lake and lagunes which bisect 100 m. of its length; 3,600 sq. m. of forest, plains, valleys, which in richness of soil, pasturage, and situation cannot be surpassed; and 2,000 sq. m. of a coast range, with the most excellent quality of blue gum-tree and black butt, embracing valleys large and deep, and holding out high expectations for the cattle-breeder. The most distant of its NE points is but 120 m. from Corner-inlet, and 160 m. from Western port. One-half of the communication for 70 m, requires only the construction of bridges across the rivers; the other half, across the hilly country to Western port, through the ridges of which I wound my course, requires only the occasional clearing of the bogs, and in some parts the brush." Topography.] The Moreton bay district, stretching along the coast from the parallel of 26° to Trial bay the N point of Macquarie co. in the Middle district, or New South Wales Proper, is divided into the following counties, all lying along the coast, viz., Stanley intersected by the Brisbane; Ward, divided on the N from Stanley by the Teviot, and watered by the Logan and the Albert, both flowing in a NE course to the Teviot; Rous watered by the Tweed flowing NE to Port Danger, and by the North Branch flowing SE into the Richmond; Richmond divided from Rous by the Richmond river; Clarence watered by the Clarence flowing into Shoal bay; Raleigh, divided from Dudley by the Bellingen river; and Dudley watered by the Nambucca, and separated on the S from Vernon and Macquarie counties by the Macleay river. To the W of the counties now enumerated are the districts of Cavendish, Churchill, and Buller, on the W frontiers of which the waters flow through Darling downs towards the Condamine. The districts of Gresham and Sandon adjoin Raleigh and Dudley on the W. Assigning the above districts to Moreton bay, or the northern division of the colony of New South Wales, the counties to the S of the Macleay are as follows: Macquarie, with the yet unproclaimed counties of Vernon, Hawes, Parry, Buckland, and Pottinger, stretching in succession towards the W over the country originally called by its discoverers the Liverpool plains. From the Manning river which forms the S boundary of Macquarie co. to Cape Howe the counties forming the coast-land of the Middle district, are Gloucester watered by the Kamah and Port Stephens; Northumberland, separated on the S by the Hawkesbury river and Broken bay from Cumberland; Cumberland, in which are the important towns of Port Jackson and Sydney, stretching S to Cataract river; Camden lying between the last-mentioned river and Shoalhaven; St. Vincent stretching from the Shoalhaven to the Moruya; Dampier and Auckland, two yet unproclaimed counties. The interior counties immediately to the W of the coast-counties now enumerated are Durham, Hunter, Cook, Westmoreland, Argyle, Murray, Beresford, and Wellesley, the two last not yet proclaimed; the more interior counties, lying to the W of these last enumerated, are Brisbane and Bligh, both immediately to the S of the Liverpool range of mountains, Phillip, Wellington, Roxburgh, Bathurst, Georgiana, King, Cowley, Wallace, Buccleugh, the three last unproclaimed. In the Port Phillip district, it is proposed to divide I. AUSTRALIA. that portion stretching from Cape Howe to the meridian of 146°, or Gipps' land, into the counties of Howe, Combermere, Abinger, Bruce, Douro, Haddington, and Bass. All these counties, with the exception of Haddington, are washed on the S by the ocean. Their interior frontier is formed by the SW prolongation of the Australian Alps; to the N of which lies the Murray territory watered by numerous rivers flowing N to the Murray, and NW to its great tributary the Goulburn. The western division of the Port Phillip district has the following counties on the coast proceeding westwards: Mornington, embracing the great bays of Port Western and Port Phillip; Grant, Polwarth, Heytesbury, Villiers, and Normanby. Immediately to the N of these lie Evelyn, Bourke, Grenville, Hampden, Ripon, Dundas, and Follet; and to the N of these Anglesey and Dalhousie stretching the one on the E, the other on the W of the Goulburn, and Talbot separated on the E by the Colliban, a head-branch of the Campaspe, from Dalhousie. If the Glenelg should be adopted as the western boundary of the Port Phillip district, then the proposed co. of Follet, which lies between it and the 141st meridian, will be excluded from this division of Australia. Lands beyond the boundaries.] The whole of the lands beyond the boundaries of New South Wales were, up to the granting of a charter to the colony of South Australia, occupied merely on license from Government. Certain boundaries are established by the name of limits of location. Within those limits, land is either sold or let upon lease; beyond them it is not either sold or let, but licenses are granted for the occupation of such tracts as may be desired for pasture by proprietors of stock, on each of which licenses a fee of £10 is payable annually, and an assessment under a local ordinance is levied on the stock depastured thereon. Each of the tracts thus occupied is called a station, and the size varies from 3,000 to 5,000 acres. This system has been acted on very largely indeed, and forms one of the characteristic features in the settlement of New South Wales. The number of stations in 1842 was stated at 756, embracing 8,963 acres under cultivation, with a resident pop. of 7,593. From Harvey's bay on the N, to Wilson's promontory, the country occupied on these terms, extends through 14 degrees of latitude; or from the same bay to South Australia, the diagonal line is 1,100 m. This territory comprised according to the latest information; Population, nearly . . . 10,000 . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 15,000 Horses, . . Cattle, . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 570,000 3,000,000 So rapid has been the progress of this species of settlement as to outrun all provision for the general or spiritual welfare of the people: and a community has thus sprung up, which, if circumstances remain the same as at present, is exposed to lose some of the chief advantages or even characteristics of civilization. Yet it is worthy of remark, and is one satisfactory feature in the case, that among those who have gone forth into this wilderness are young men not only of good family, but of high education received in Europe. [Parl. paper.] Climate. The climate of New South Wales is salubrious, although the heat of summer is often excessive. At Port Jackson the mean heat during the three summer months, - December, January, and February,—is about 80° at noon; but is moderated by the sea-breeze which blows from 9 A. M. till 6 P. M. The hot winds here blow from the NW, but seldom for more than twelve hours at a time. While they prevail, the sea-breeze ceases; and they are generally succeeded by a cold southerly gale which gives way 2 F in 12 or 24 hours to the regular sea and land breeze. In March, April, and May the therm. varies during the day about 15°; but seldom exceeds 75°. In June, July, and August, the mean temp. at daylight is from 40° to 45°; at noon, from 55° to 60°. In the beginning of September, the heat seldom exceeds 60° at noon; but frequently reaches 80° in October; and 85° in November. In the inland districts, but to the E of the mountains, the therm. t roughout the winter season is on an average 5° lower in the morning, and as many degrees higher at noon; during summer, it is 5° higher at all hours. [Wentworth.] The mean variation of barometrical oscillation is with those from the equator. Macquarie, Launceston, Phillip. Annual mean The fertility and salubrity of New South Wales are proved by the healthiness of the English settlers who have taken root in the soil. "No epidemic disease, and seldom any endemic of grave character, prevails; and if individual indisposition, or even partial deterioration of the progeny, is sometimes seen, it is to be traced to the pertinacity with which the English race cling to their original modes of living wherever they settle, and however different may be their adopted to their native climate; it is to the abuse of strong wines, malt liquors, and spirits, and particularly to the excessive consumption of animal food of the richest description, and even to the mode of clothing and housing, that individual diseases, such as dyspepsia, premature decay of teeth, and affection of the brain may be attributed." [Strzelecki.] Soil and productions.] The soil of this part of A. is productive, yielding every grain and vegetable useful to man, with fruit in the highest perfection, and of all varieties, from the current and gooseberry of colder climes to the banana and the pine-apple of the tropics. Even in the immediate vicinity of Sydney, apples, pears, plums, strawberries, cherries, raspberries, mulberries, medlars, apricots, peaches, nectarines, figs, grapes, melons, oranges, lemons, citrons, loquots, olives, pomegranates—and in sheltered spots, the guava and the banana-will be found growing intermingled, and producing fruit abundantly, and of the richest flavour. Near the coast, green pease are gathered in winter as well as summer, and the potato produces two crops in the year; in the interior, the winters are more severe. Wheat on good soils, with proper cultivation, averages from AUSTRALIA. 20 to 30 bushels an acre, weighing from 60 to 65 lbs. the bushel. In the county of Argyle, one of the colder districts, 40 bushels per acre have frequently been obtained, and, in some instances, it is said, even more; but, under the improvident, slovenly system too prevalent on the farms of the smaller settlershitherto the chief wheat-growers—the average crop is probably not more than 15 bushels per acre. The quantity of land in cultivation in the colony, including the district of Port Phillip, in 1836 and in 1845, was as follows: 1836. 1845. 87,894 acres. Wheat 51,616 acres. Maize The seed-time is from March to June; the harvest in November and December. Maize, the most luxuriant of grain-crops, is sown in October and November, and ripens from March to June, producing from 20 to 50, and even 70 bushels per acre, according to the been known to fail. The hop-plant has been cultivated successfully in different districts. The climate and soil are well-suited to the vine, the olive, and the mulberry. Vineyards and olive-grounds have been planted in several districts, and very palatable wine produced. Tobacco of good quality is grown. Silk and dried fruits, with other useful and valuable articles for the growth of which the climate is favourable, will doubtless by degrees be produced; and Batavia and Manilla; and an occasional supply is also received from several of the South sea islands. The prevailing trees are different species of Eucalyptus, some of which bear large and handsome flowers, but their leaves are mostly of a dull green. They are, however, evergreens. Several species of Leptospermum or tea-tree are cultivated, and many acacias. Hedges are often formed of geraniums, and sometimes of the fruit-bearing cactus, or prickly pear, C. opuntia. Among the more magnificent flowers is the waratah or Telopea speciosissima bearing a large flower of the richest crimson-scarlet; the gorgeous colossal crimson lily, the Doryanthus excelsa; the delicate oleander, and the handsome bushy pomegranate. [Meredith.] Mineralogy.] Coal has been found both to the east and west of the Blue mountains. On the eastern coast it has been traced over an immense tract cropping out very near the surface. The town of Newcastle, in the district of Northumberland, is so called from the abundant supply of coal which it affords, the whole surrounding country abounding with that valuable mineral. A considerable trade in this article is carried on between this port and Sydney, where it is sold at the rate of 20s. to 21s. per ton, the price at the pit-mouth is 5s. to 6s., the freight 15s., the distance being nearly 100 miles. Near the mouth of the Hunter river, in a depth of 204 ft., there are five beds of coal, two of them 5 ft. in thickness. [Strzelecki.] The coal-mines at Newcastle are in the hands of government, and are worked by 'second sentence men,' or those who have committed offences after reaching their first destination. The New South Wales coal is said to ·AUSTRALIA. be of good quality, but generally small and dirty; but it is not generally used, as wood is still very abundant and cheaper. Iron seems to be plentifully diffused, particularly on the shores of the gulf of Carpentaria; but English iron can still be furnished so cheap as to preclude the necessity of working the native stores of this metal. Copper, tin, and lead have been also traced. Limestone exists, but has not yet been found in any great quantity. Rock Potatoes, "With respect to agriculture," says the governor, in one of his despatches, "it is already known and ascertained that the frequent recurrence of dry seasons renders it a pursuit of too hazardous a nature to depend upon; and the very abundant harvests which do at intervals occur are but exceptions to the general course of events, and insufficient to alter the unfavourable impression entertained of it. The colony, therefore, can never depend on raising her own grain, nor is it likely that much exertion will be devoted to that object, since the necessary supplies can now be obtained with facility and advantage from the neighbouring colonies of Van Diemen's Land and Southern A., independently of Valparaiso, from whence much wheat has already been imported, of excellent quality, and at a very low price. Several shipments have already been made with very encouraging results, and others since followed, and are now in progress. Wool.] The pastoral department of this colony is more important and characteristic. The grand staple of New South Wales at present is wool; and there is no instance on record of such rapid progress of a country in wealth as that which has resulted to New South Wales from the produce of its flocks. It was in 1812, that Mr. John M'Arthur had consigned to him a number of merino sheep. By a singular coincidence these were conveyed out by the ship 'Argo,' and the consequences that have followed seem to have realized the fable of the golden fleece; for such has been the rapid increase in the exports of fine wool, the produce of these flocks, that from 245 lbs. exported in 1807, and 99,415 lbs. exported in 1820, the quantity had increased to 17,364,734 lbs. in 1845, valued at £1,009,242. "It may be interesting to mention," say the Emigration commissioners in their report for 1849, "that on comparing the increase of population and cultivated land in Canada and New South Wales, we find that while the population of Upper Canada has increased 76 per cent. in nine years, that of New South Wales has increased 79 per cent in eight years, and that while the increase of cultivated land since 1839 is in Upper Canada 69 per cent., that in New South Wales is 72 per cent. during the same period. While, however, the rate of increase is so nearly the same, the proportion of the cultivated land to the population is widely different, being in Canada about 3.8 acres, but in New South Wales no more than 4-5ths of an acre to each inhabitant. New South Wales seems to offer at least three native varieties of inhabitants, all belonging to the race of Oceanic Negroes. In the neighbourhood of Glasshouse-bay, the savages have large heads which in shape resemble those of the ourang-outang, while their very limited intellects, their hairy bodies, and habitual agility in climbing trees, seem to bring them near the monkeycharacter. To the south-west of the English colony, tribes have been found which speak a distinct language, and have hardier constitutions than those in the immediate vicinity of the colony. Education.] No system of general education for the lower classes has yet been established; the attempt made in 1837 to introduce the Irish, and subsequently in 1839, the British and Foreign system, has not proved successful. At present the schools for children of the lower classes, are for the most part under the management of the clergy of the different denominations, assistance being rendered to all equally by government. There are three establishments for orphans, or destitute children, maintained entirely at the expense of government, -one for males, one for female Protestant children, and a third for Roman Catholics of both sexes. There is also a school for the children of convicts in the female factory at Paramatta. For children of the higher classes there is no deficiency of places of education. A college has recently been founded at Sydney under episcopal auspices. The Australian college, founded by Dr. Lang, is on the Scotch model. Government.] The internal polity of New South Wales, the earliest colonized portion of New Holland, was, until recently, conducted by a governor, and a council composed of military officers, the governor himself always being of that profession. class of functionaries called police-magistrates, distributed throughout the colony, with salaries of £400 per annum each. These take special cognizance of all offences committed by convicts, whom they have the power to punish by flogging or condemning to work in irons.
