Expenditure. II.—AUSTRALIA FELIX.—The district of Port Philip, which Dr. Lang proposes to call PHILIPSLAND, and which received the name of A. Felix from its first explorer, lies between New South Wales and Southern A., and properly belongs to the government of the former. As at present defined it forms the southern portion of the territory of New South Wales; and comprises all land lying to the S of the southern boundaries of the counties of Auckland, Wellesley, and Wallace, and from thence by the rivers Murrumbidgee and Murray, up to the eastern boundary of South A., or the meridian of 141° E long.,-"a compact and well-shaped territory" [Parl. paper],—though smaller than that of any other colony on the main land of Australia. Its territory, however, is so extensive as to require that it should be placed under a colonial government of its own. Port Philip, the principal point of the settlement, is nearly opposite to Van Diemen's Land. It is an immense bay, measuring about 40 m. by 35 m. The capital, Melbourne, already a thriving town, contained upwards of 11,000 inhabitants in 1845; while that of the entire district was 32,879. Melbourne is built close upon the falls of the river Yarra-Yarra. Immediately below the town, vessels anchor in salt water. Rivers and Mountains.] The Glenelg takes its rise in the Grampians, and after pursuing a general S by W direction for 300 m., discharges its waters into the sea in E long. 141° 20'. [Arrowsmith.] Its stream is described by Major Mitchell, as deep, placid, and free of all obstructions; its banks are wild and picturesque, and its waters fresh within sight of the sea; it has a slight tide of from 6 to 12 inches at its confluence with the sea, but its navigation is impeded by a bar of sand through which only a boat-channel has yet been discovered .- Following the coast in an easterly direction, we pass the Fitzroy, Surrey, and Shaw, -three small streams in the neighbourhood of Portland bay, which fall into the sea between the longitude of 142° and 143° E, and arrive at the mouth of the Barwen, about 3 m. to the W of Port Philip. The Barwen takes its rise in the hilly country of the interior, and passing eastward through the district of Glenelg, close to the town of the same name, gradually loses itself in a large shallow and reedy outlet upon its approach to the sea, to the S of Inner Geelong bay, the SW corner of Port Phillip. The rivers flowing into the harbour of Port Phillip are the Yarra- Yarra, the Marriburnong or Saltwater, sometimes called the Arundel, and the Weirabee. The Yarra- Yarra springs from the Australian Alps, and flowing in the direction of S by W, empties itself into Hobson's bay, the N extremity of Port Phillip. This river is navigable for 8 m. by small vessels of 200 tons. Within 2 m. of its mouth it is joined by the Saltwater river. The Weirabee, or Ex, which flows N and S, and likewise empties its waters into the bay, is navigable for boats of 10 to 20 tons. The country between these rivers, extending northwards 40 or 50 m., and to the E about 25 m. to a chain of mountains running from the back of Western Port in a northern direction, undulated with valleys between, is moderately wooded except towards the N, where open plains stretch along. The soil is a sandy loam of good quality, occasionally in the lower parts very rich. The country between the rivers coming from the N and the W extremity of the port, extending about 25 m. inland, is open, and partakes of the nature of downs, the whole being covered with a slender grass growing on a stiff and shallow soil. Along the course of the Weirabee, and along the shore of the port, the plains are open, affording in all places valuable sheepstations for breeding flocks. It is not improbable they may be sometimes, like other districts of A., visited with drought in dry summers; but the prevailing winds are from the W and S, which usually bring rain with them. One river, the Wimmera, rises in the interior, flows inland, and empties itself into a lake. It has its source, in all probability, in the Grampians, pursues a NNW course for 150 m., and don follow courses parallel to the Bayungun, and are, like that river, tributaries of the Hume; the one rises at the foot of Mount Macedon, the other in the Pyrenees. The Wando, the Wannon, the Grange, and the Crowford, all join the Glenelg, watering a large and fertile tract of country westward of the Grampians.—The mountains of this prov. are neither numerous nor remarkable for their height, but as features in the natural panorama of the country have been much admired. The highest range is the Grampians, the most prominent point of which is thought to rise 5,000 ft. above sea-level; the highest isolated mountain is Mount Macedon, 3,500 ft., besides which are Mount-Byng, Mount-Campbell, Mount-Blackwood, and the Villamanata or Station-peak, remarkable for its picturesque appearance on whichever side it may be viewed. To the westward are Mount-Napier, an extinct volcano, Mount-Eckerlsy, Mount- Shadwell, and Mount-Clay; to the N the Pyramidhill, and Mount-Hope, from the summit of which the rich fertile plains of this territory first broke upon the discoverer's view. Animals.] Kangaroos are not numerous about Port Phillip, for which, in the midst of so much good pasturage, it seems difficult to account; except that being without that refuge in the woods which is afforded everywhere in Van Diemen's Land, they are more successfully hunted down by the natives, and I considerable plains in directions nearly parallel to perhaps the young ones occasionally by the dingo or wild dog. A crow of a large species, wild geese, quails, black swans, wild ducks, and teal, are very abundant; but are all, with the exception of the quails, wild and difficult to get. There is also an animal which appears to be a species of wombat. The holes which it inhabits are large enough for a moderately sized person to creep into, and the creature itself must weigh 150 lbs. and upwards. The natives call it ouringore, and prize its flesh very highly. Besides these, a very beautiful species of squirrel has been found here: it is small, with fur exquisitely fine. Of the winged race the genus Psittacus appears to be, as in Van Diemen's Land, the most numerous. The bays and rivers abound with excellent fish. Soil and capabilities.] This district was first explored in 1835 by Lieut.-colonel Sir T. L. Mitchell, and found in a state of unproductiveness; but it possesses great capabilities. With regard to the land, it has been remarked, "The good land in New South Wales is only in patches; but, in Port Phillip, it is the bad land that is in patches." There is a tract extending W from the western arm of Port Phillip, about 200 m. in length, and 25 m. in breadth, which is generally considered equal to any land in Great Britain, and is capable of sustaining as dense a population. It comprises about 3,200,000 acres in one continuous stretch, and is at present occupied merely as squatting stations. It is used by the squatters for pastoral purposes alone, except as regards the production of grain for their own wants. The average produce of the district is 35 bushels to the acre, without the application of manure, which is never thought of. Revenue and Commerce.] The following table exhibits the value of Imports and Exports, the quantity of wool exported, and the customs revenue for the district of Port Phillip, from 1837 to 1846: Exports. Customs. Wool. Imports. lbs. £ £ The revenue of the district in 1846 was £89,118; expenditure, £69,748.—The imports amounted to £205,390; the exports to £342,624. II.—SOUTHERN AUSTRALIA.—By Act of 4° and 5° William IV., c. 95, that part of Australia which lies between 132° and 141° E long., and between the Southern ocean and 26° S lat., with the islands adjacent to the coast, was erected into a British province and colony. The area within these limits is nearly 300,000 sq. m., or 192,000,000 acres; being twice as large as that of the British isles. Physical features.] All accounts concur in representing South A. to be a country of great beauty and richness. There are few arid or desert wastes which defy the industry of man; and man; spots occur which, while well-timbered, are in the disposition of the trees more like an English park than an untrodden wild. It is generally well-suited for depasturing sheep, and in many places quite open enough for the plough. Major Mitchell says: "Every variety of feature may be seen in those southern parts, from the lofty Alpine region on the E, to the low grassy plains in which it terminates on the W. The Murray-perhaps the largest river in all A .- rises amongst those mountains, and receives in its course various other rivers of considerable magnitude. These flow over ~!* the main stream, and thus irrigate and fertilize a great extent of rich country. Falling from mountains of great height, the current of these rivers is perpetual; whereas in other parts of A., the rivers are too often dried up, and seldom deserve any other name than chains or ponds. Hills of moderate elevation occupy the central country between the Murray and the sea, being thin or partially wooded, and covered with the richest pasturage. The lower country, both on the northern and southern skirts of these hills, is chiefly open, slightly undulating towards the coast on the south, and in general well-watered. The grassy plains which extend northward from these thinly-wooded hills to the banks of the Murray, are chequered by the channels of many streams falling from them, and by the more permanent and extensive waters of deep lagunes, which are numerous on the face of these plains, as if intended, by a bounteous Providence, to correct the deficiencies of a climate otherwise too dry for an industrious and increasing people, by preserving in these abundant reservoirs the surplus waters of the large river; indeed, a finer country for cattle-stations than this, can scarcely be imagined. In the western portion, small rivers radiate from the Grampians, an elevated and isolated mass, presenting no impediment to a free communication through the fine country around its base. The practicability of an overland journey from Sydney to Adelaide has been established by Messrs. Hawdon, Eyre, and other gentlemen, conducting large flocks of cattle from the former to the latter place. They speak of the country through which they passed, in terms of praise. On the journey they came into the neighbourhood of several large lakes. On the third day after leaving the Darling, following a flooded branch of the Murray, they found it joining the Rufus within a mile of a beautiful lake, about 40 m. in circumference, out of which the Rufus takes its rise. The large body of water which flows down this river, appears to be supplied entirely by springs rising in the lake, the bed of which is white clay, and discolours the water. They named this lake Victoria; and afterwards passed another lake, about 20 m. in circumference, the water of which is impregnated with nitre, which was named Bonny. Port Lincoln, situated in Spencer's gulf, seems destined, at no distant period, to be the seat of a flourishing and important settlement. The harbour is of vast extent, capable of containing the whole navies of Europe; and the depth of water is generally not less than 12 fathoms, with a bottom of muddy sand. This, taken in connexion with the richness of the land on every side, will make it a most desirable locality for a settlement. Rivers.] South A. does not possess numerous large rivers. In the vicinity of the mountains there are, as already remarked, a number of streams. The Mount-Lofty range, running 40 or 50 m., and extending in breadth 20 or 30 m., contains about half-amillion of acres of a very well watered mountainpasturage. But, with the exception of the great river Murray already noticed, which is as broad as the Thames at London for the last 200 m. of its course, there are no large rivers. The other rivers are the Glenelg, a very useful river, but dry for a part of the year at its mouth; the Rufus, which is only a few miles in length; the Torrens, and the Hindmarsh. Productions and Climate. Timber forms one of the most important natural productions of this division of A. It principally consists of various species of Eucalyptus, or gum-tree, -heavy, hard, and difficult to work, but affording excellent roofing and flooring. The blue-gum is often found of 14 or 16 ft. in girth. The oak, the pine, the tea-tree—a kind of myrtle-and the yellow-wattle, abound. The grasses and herbage are not the least valuable productions of the colony. The kangaroo-grass grows luxuriantly, and sheep fatten on it rapidly; there are also several varieties of rib-grass, besides chicory, trefoil, and some other herbs which stand the winter and afford good feed for sheep.—The kangaroo is the principal native animal. The native dog has been nearly extirpated. The feathered tribes are numerous, and many of them remarkable for their singular character and beautiful plumage. The black swan is found upon the lakes and rivers. Pigeons, snipes, plovers, and quails are numerous. Locusts are plentiful in summer, but not injurious. Flies are abundant and troublesome, and mosquitoes. The white ant is the most injurious insect; and the brown snake the most deadly reptile. The inlets, bays, and waters abound with fish. The best kinds are snappers, rock-cod, bream, mullet, whiting, rock and bed oysters, and prawns. The Torrens abounds in craw-fish. Mr. Horton James says: "There can be no doubt that South A. will in time be a very abundant country. If the settlers will economise water, and find some means of keeping it when it falls, they may in a few years luxuriate in all the good things of this life. Whatever is to be seen of vegetable produce in the markets of Lisbon or Cadiz, Sicily or Algiers, the settlers in this new colony may equally command. It is peculiarly the country of the grape and melon, oranges and lemons, figs, olives, pomegranates, and loquets; and even in its present infant state produces as fine melons as the Levant. But the character which stamps the South A. climate as most valuable in the eyes of the settler, is its peculiar adaptation to sheep-farming, and the growth of wool. By a register kept at Government-house, during the year 1837, it rained 115 days, and was fine and clear 250; and this may be reckoned upon as a fair average of a series of years. It is this peculiar character of the climate, which ought to recommend to the cautious settler sheep-husbandry over every other sort of rural industry." It has also a most favourable influence on the general health of the colony; for, except ophthalmia, the writer saw nothing indicative of disease. The inhabitants retain for a long time their English ruddiness of complexion, and even the children look plump and chubby. A regular demand exists for agricultural and pastoral as well as mining labour. The commissioners send out regularly one vessel a-month, averaging 250 emigrants in each vessel. There is no great disproportion between the sexes, the numbers being as 30 to 25. Until lately there were no Irish in the colony. There is a considerable German emigration from the Hartz district and Silesia, the number of Germans at present in the colony being from 3,000 to 4,000. They are very quiet and sober settlers, and generally congregate together, although they agree well with the others. They are not, however, so industrious as Englishmen or Scotchmen. "The women do most of the hard work, while the men are apt to go about smoking." A few years ago the province of South A. was tenanted only by kangaroos and a few straggling natives, physically and mentally the lowest of the human race; it is at present the scene of the enterprise and energy of more than 35,000 individuals of English race, has an active and extending mining industry, a great breadth of land under cultivation, and a greater covered with flocks. In 1840 only 2,503 acres were in cultivation, but in 1844 it had extended to 26,918 acres. In the year 1843, however, 28,690 acres were under cultivation. During that year 23,000 acres were devoted to wheat, 3,300 acres to barley, 790 acres to oats, 290 acres to maize, 470 acres to potatoes, and 840 acres to gardens. The decrease in the number of acres under cultivation in 1844 as compared with 1843, was owing to the extremely low price of wheat in the colony. In 1848, the land in cultivation was 48,912 acres, of which 29,737 acres were wheat. The agricultural produce is decidedly good, and its wheat realizes the highest price. About 25 bushels to the acre is considered a good crop. No regular rotation is observed, the fertility of the soil enabling to go on growing wheat year after year. Lime for manure is to be had in considerable quantities. The number of sheep in the colony is 1,000,000. There is communication by land from Adelaide to Sydney, a distance of 1,000 miles, chiefly used for the transmission of cattle. There are very few aborigines, and these perfectly harmless. The distinguishing and cardinal principles of this colony are that all public lands shall be sold, and the proceeds employed in conveying labourers to the colony. The first vessel with emigrants sailed from London on the 22d of February 1836; and in the month of March the surveying staff sailed, and were soon after followed by the governor. The quantity of land possessed by the company is about 60,000 acres; and their plan has been to let it on leases for 7, 14, and 21 years, at a rent of 4s. or 5s. per acre for the first seven years, and of 6s. or 7s. for the next two periods. These leases contain no engagement for renewal, but they have a clause authorizing the tenant to purchase the freehold at a fixed price,—generally 20 years' purchase. Up to 1847 the quantity leased was 9,612 acres. The company originally had several ships in the whalingtrade, and they also established a bank, and intended besides to enter into commercial operations. All these schemes have been discontinued, and they now confine themselves to sheep-flocks and herds, and the cultivation of the land, with the exception that they have recently purchased a very extensive district of mineral lands, which they contemplate working in part themselves. Minerals.] Regarding mineral productions generally this colony abounds with copper. Lead has also been found of good quality. Tin likewise has been reported, but it is believed inaccurately. No coal has yet been discovered. The Burra Burra copper-mine is about 96 m. due N from Adelaide, the cap. of the colony, and is one of the most extensively producing mines in the world. The average produce in the first eighteen months' operations in this mine was 126 tons of copper per week; in March 1847, it amounted to nearly 300 tons per week. The Greenock-creek copper-mine, the first established, commenced working in 1836, on a piece of land 34 m. from Adelaide. The Glen-Osmond mine, 31 m. from Adelaide, has produced rich silver-lead ore. The ore of the Kaumantu mines, distant 25 m. from Adelaide, has yielded from 40 to 50 per cent. of metal. Native gold of great purity has been discovered within 10 m. of Adelaide. Sulphate of copper abounds in Mount-Remarkable district, near the head of Spencer's gulf. Rapid-bay mine, 65 m. from Adelaide, has produced copper, lead, and zinc; and the Rivers-edge mine, 13 m. from Adelaide, on the banks of the Torrens, has a lode of lead containing 1.75 per cent. of silver. The total export of metallic ore from South A. in 1845, was £40,000; in 1846, £150,000; in 1847, £181,736. Imports and Exports.] In 1843, the exports, chiefly colonial produce or manufactures, amounted in value to £62,645. Previously to that year they consisted in a great measure of imported goods re-exported. The value of the wool, estimated at 9d. per lb., and the average weight of fleeces at 21 lbs., was £32,055; but the average realized was 13d. per lb. The imports in 1843 amounted in value to £93,143; in 1844, to £119,648 18s. 3d.; in 1845, to £163,898 1s. AUSTRALIA. 5d. The total exports in 1844 were, £95,199 7s. 6d.; in 1845, £148,459 4s. 7d. In 1844, the imports reexported were, £12,921 13s. 10d.; in 1845, £16,658 18s. 7d. The imports consumed in the colony were, in 1844, £106,727 4s. 5d.; in 1845, £147,239 2s. 10d., The exports of colonial produce were, in 1844, £82,268 13s. 4d.; in 1845, £131,800 6s. In 1846, the exports amounted to £313,000; the imports to £330,000. The greatest augmentation of exports was on bread, barley, copper-ore, oil, whale-bone, and wool. [Parl. Papers.] Shipping.] The ships inwards in 1844 were 59, the tonnage 8,592, and the men 550. In 1845, the ships inwards were 109, the tonnage 13,795, and the men 1,012. Revenue.] In 1844, the ordinary revenue of the province produced £27,881 12s. 10d.; in 1845, £37,175 9s. 10d. Ordinary expenditure in 1844, £27,720 13s. 10d.; in 1845, £30,100 9s. 6d. The revenue for 1846 was estimated at £42,970; the expenditure at £32,677. III.—WESTERN AUSTRALIA.—The colony of Western Australia, as defined by his late Majesty's commission, includes all that portion of New Holland which is situated to the westward of the 129th degree of longitude: its greatest length therefore is 1,280 m. from N to S, and 800 m. from E to W. The voyage from England to Western A. may be performed in about a month less than the voyage to New South Wales; and the homeward route, instead of being by the way of Cape Horn, is in the opposite direction, by the cape of Good Hope. The nearest islands in the Malay archipelago, are at the distance of two days' sail from the northern ports of this colony. Passages to Madras have been effected in 25 days; and to the Isle of France and China in proportionately short periods. For the purposes of commercial intercourse with the countries above-mentioned, whether by steam or by sailing vessels, the position of Western A. is highly advantageous. In maritime warfare, the importance of a coast which bounds the Indian ocean for 2,000 m., may be easily estimated; nor is its value in peace unimportant, when it is considered how much the position which it occupies may influence the navigation of the neighbouring seas. Coasts and Rivers.] It appears from King's surveys, that the coast of the colony, within the tropic, is fronted by indentations, bays, straits, and islands, and abounds in the finest harbours imaginable. The rise and fall of tide in some places amounts to 35 ft. The existence of an interior sea, or of great rivers, connected with some of the inlets which were not fully explored by King, remains a problem as yet undetermined. It possesses one peculiarity in point of seasons in the occurrence of boisterous westerly monsoons, accompanied by clouds and rain, at the hottest period of the year, from November to March. The principal ports in the SW parts of the colony are those in Cockburn-sound and in King George's sound. The first of these is an excellent port, but its entrance is encumbered by rocks, and it is not accessible with safety to large vessels, while there is not an effective establishment of pilots and beacons. The open anchorage at the mouth of the Swan-river is, therefore, in general use at present. King George's sound possesses all the qualities which constitute a good harbour; its position being, however, to the eastward, and to leeward of Cape Leuwin, in the vicinity of which strong westerly gales prevail, this circumstance detracts from the value of its other qualifications. Shark's bay abounds in safe anchorages, and affords, as well as Doubtful Island bay, secure access to the districts in their immediate vicinity. Harbours for boats and small coasting-vessels exist near the entrance of Peel's inlet, Port Leshenault, Augusta, Nornaleep, Torbay, Collinwood bay, and Cape Riche.—"The rivers in South-Western A.," remarks Captain Grey, "much resemble in character those of the south-eastern parts of the continent. They rise at no very great distance from the sea. Near their sources they are mountain torrents, but valleys or plains, which are liable to sudden and terrific inundations, caused, I conceive, by the rain which falls in that part of the mountains where the rivers take their rise." Climate.] The wet season commences with light showers in April, which continue to increase in number and force throughout May, June, and July, and from that period to decrease, until they cease altogether in the month of November, when the dry weather begins. These two seasons, with an intermediate spring following the conclusion of each, embrace the circle of the year. It is usual to call the wet season the winter, and the dry season the summer; but neither of them has the character of the corresponding season in Europe. The extreme drought and heat of summer renders it the least agreeable portion of the year: while the winter, with the exception of intervals of stormy weather, is only sufficiently cold to be pleasant. The prevailing wind, in the seas adjacent to Cape Leuwin, is from the westward throughout the year; on the coasts, however, land and sea-breezes take place with great regularity in the summer. In the winter season, gales of wind from the NW and SW are very frequent, and are usually accompanied by heavy falls of rain. Surface.] The surface of the country, generally, is covered with those substances which are technically called earths, in contradistinction to soils. Of the latter—as far at least as relates to those of a vegetable origin—a very small portion exists, and that only on moist grounds. The extreme drought of the climate, and the summer-conflagrations, appear to prevent the growth of succulent plants, as well as any great accumulation of soil from decayed vegetation. But although the country is not remarkable for richness of soil, it is favourable in other respects to farming-purposes. In its natural state there is scarcely any part which does not produce some description of plant; and its defects appear to be of that class which art, aided by climate, will be enabled hereafter to overcome. A very large portion of its surface, extending probably to three-fifths of the whole, is poor, comparatively unprofitable, and unlikely to be cultivated, or to yield any return except in timber, until a dense population, and low wages, aided by abundance of cattle, shall enable the farmer to bring it into use. The best districts at present known are those on the Avon, the Hotham, the Williams, Arthur, Beaufort, and South-east river, together with the portions of country adjacent to the Swan, the Murray, the Harvey, Brunswick, Preston, Capel, and Vasse. Minerals.] Coal has recently been discovered under favourable circumstances. Dr. Von Saumer reports that he has traced the great coal formation of Western A., on its most western boundary, from the place where coal and shale were first found by accident, in 29° 3' S lat. down to 29° 45,' bearing from the first place to the second S 11° 30' true, or 173½° magnetic. He was prevented from following that line farther down on account of the boggy nature of that formation in its lower parts at this time of the year, but traversed on that tract more than 200 sq. m. of very good, well-watered, and wooded pastoral land. At the Canning river good copper-ore has been discovered, and a company has been established for working the mines. Quicksilver has also been discovered: not only the red sulphuret of cinnabar but also the oxides of that metal have been obtained. Colonists.] The number and location of the Colonists were as follows in 1838:- Augusta, and the second second second second second second second second second second second second second second second second second second second second second second second second second second second second second second seco Vasse, 21 Military, including women and children, . . 185 2,032 Total, . . From this return, as compared with a similar document drawn up in 1832, it appears that the population increased between the latter period and 1838 from 1,510 to 2,032. In 1847, the population had reached 4,530. On the formation of this colony enormous grants of lands were made to individuals, to which cause its comparative want of progress is attributed. Mr. Peel had a block of 250,000 acres, and there were other grants of a similar nature, so that not less than 1,500,000 acres had been appropriated up to 1841, the population being proportionably dispersed. Labour is extremely scarce, and wages so ruinously high, that it was proposed to secure Chinese immigrants from Singapore, but the plan was discouraged by the government. In 1841-42, about 920 settlers landed, but since that time there has been no addition, except 38 Parkhurst boys, who have turned out badly. The community, however, being small, they would not be able, from want of capital, to receive and employ any great number of immigrants at once; but it is calculated that about 1,500 or 1,600 labourers annually might be provided for with as much advantage as in any other colony. [Lords' Report.] The number of aborigines is about 1,500. Agriculture.] Agricultural and pastoral pursuits are the leading occupations in this country. The culture of the vine, fig, peach, and melon tribe, has been carried to a considerable extent; and if ever it should be desirable for the mother-country to possess a wine-growing colony, the soils and seasons of this country afford reasonable ground for anticipating a successful issue to such a speculation. There is every reason to believe that Western A. will one day become a great wine-country. Its vineyards are becoming more numerous and extensive every year; and the wine produced in them is of a quality to lead us to believe that when the art of preparing it is better understood, it will be found of very superior quality. It will, however, be a new kind of wine; and, therefore, before it will be prized in Europe, prejudices in favour of older wines have to be overcome. Every farmsettler is now adding a vineyard to his estate. [Landor. A vine and olive company has been established at Perth, to include the cultivation of the vine and olive, and to carry on an export trade of raisins, wine, and oil. The capital is to be £5,000, in 1,000 shares of £5 each; the probable expenditure for three years is estimated at £2,392, the returns in six years at £5,324. The amount of stock in 1844 was 98,018 head. In 1848, there were 2,095 horses, 10,919 horned cattle, 141,123 sheep, 2,287 swine, and 1,431 goats, within the colony. The number of acres in cultivation in 1844 was 4,867. In 1848, there were 3,316 acres under wheat; 672 under barley; 133 in oats; 100 in rye; 38 in maize; 120 in potatoes; 114 laid out in vineyards; 10 on olive-yards; 244 on kitchen gardens; and 2,320 under green crops. There is a greater quantity of bad land in this than in the other colonies, but the soil would produce very good grain with manure. The weight of the wheat produced has been more than 70 lb. per bushel, and its quality generally has been as good as that in South A. Soil upon which sheep have been folded produces on an average about 20 bushels per acre, but it might be much increased. A great deal of barley is grown. Oats will not grow well. The climate is unsuited to flax. The herbage is scanty, and the average soil would keep about one sheep to five acres, but there is the advantage of a supply of water throughout the year remaining in pools or lakes sufficient for the pastoral system and for the climate generally. The present stock of sheep and cattle is 116,570 head. Originally, the colony was heavily pressed by the expense of importing stock from Sydney, which experienced a serious mortality. The average passage from Sydney by sea is four or five weeks. [Lords' Report.] Fisheries.] Since the earliest discovery of this coast, it has been known to abound in various descriptions of fish. The Malays have carried on, for at least 200 years, an extensive and profitable trepang and tortoise-shell fishery, on the NW coast. Dampier, Baudin, and King, at different periods, have reported the existence of astonishing numbers of whales in the adjacent seas; and our own experience, since the establishment of the colony, and still more recently since whale-fishing commenced in its bays, confirms these reports. Trade.] The value of exports in 1843 was £7,088; in 1844, £13,343. The imports in 1843 amounted to £37,486; in 1844 to £36,440. "The difference in value," says the governor, "of the exports of 1844 over those of 1843, is £6,255, being an increase of 95 per cent. The great bulk of these exports will be sent to England; but a trade is rising up with the Isle of France and other places in the Indian ocean, and efforts are making to carry it on in colonial vessels. The extremely favourable nature of the climate has led to an extensive cultivation of the vine, and that of the olive is following close in its train. It is reckoned that upwards of 5,000 gallons of wine will be made this year, besides some cwts. of raisins, and tended for private consumption; but dried fruits will, in the course of the next twelve months, appear among the exports, and with a little more time and experience, wine and olive-oil will be added to the list." In 1845 there was no improvement in the amount of exports, the total remaining exactly the same; the imports were reduced to £20,350. In 1847, however, a rapid stride is observable. The exports reached £24,535, the imports £25,463. The chief feature in connexion with these changes is the evidence they afford of energy on the part of the settlersthe result probably of necessity—in opening up new markets, since almost the whole of the great increase in the exports has arisen, not in the trade with England, but in the transactions of the place with other colonies. This branch of commerce, it appears, had increased from £997, its amount in 1844, to £7,288 in 1846, being a nearly eightfold growth in three years. Of the various articles of produce shipped from the colony, oil seems to be the only one that has fallen-off. The quantity of wool has nearly doubled, and it now stands at £13,360. Whalebone, cattle, potatoes, and sandal-wood are the items next in order. The latter article finds an excellent market at Singapore or in China; but the eagerness of the colonists to embark in sandal-wood speculations has had ill effects, by causing them to neglect their flocks and homesteads. The wheat harvest of 1848 was expected to be scanty, from the sanguine expectations created by sandal-wood cutting; the land which used to be laid out for wheat is now used for the purpose of raising hay for the horses and ern coast of A., from Moreton bay on the E, to Swan bullocks employed in transporting sandal-wood to the sea; and the 'hewing mania' is represented to have seized nearly all the colonists; while the value of labour for hewing, dressing, and carting, has augmented fully 60 per cent. The amount of this wood exported in 1846 was only 32 tons; in 1847, it was 379 tons; and in 1848, 1,310 tons. It costs £8 per ton at shipment, and fetches about £24 in Singapore and £30 in China. There is likewise an available supply of ship-building timber, which grows quite close to the sea, is analogous to Honduras mahogany, is of a great size, possesses the peculiar property of resisting the sea-worms, and is not apt to split or warp, while at the same time it is more easily worked than any other wood in A. It has been used in the colony for building small vessels, and also for rafters, &c., in house-building. A shipload supplied to the Admiralty, under contract, was very highly approved of. Government.] The local administration is provided for by royal commission and acts of parliament. The legislative power conferred on the governor and the members of the legislative council has been hitherto exercised only in the adoption of certain recent acts of parliament of a general tendency, and in the passing of a few ordinances connected with matters of local interest. The revenue in 1843 amounted to £10,311; in 1847 it was only £8,944. A parliamentary grant averaging £7,000 per annum is necessary to meet the expenditure. The public are more generally acquainted with Western A. by the name of the Swan river, than by its present designation. This, however, is but the designation of a portion of an extensive province. The Swan river bounds the northern limit of the great plain of Quartania. The name Zwaanen Riviere was given to it by Vlaming, a Dutch navigator, in 1697. The sailing distance from the meridian of Greenwich is 13,000 miles; and from Swan river to— Distance. Sailing time. Cape of Good Hope . 5,000 miles. Sydney . . . "Western A.," says Mr. Ogle, "was, like other colonies, founded without principles or system; and the hardships endured, and the losses incurred, were enough to have shaken the stoutest hearts: but the high and undaunted spirit displayed by the greater number, and the conduct of Sir James Stirling, the first governor, met and conquered the difficulties, and prepared a path in the wilderness, which those who now follow will find leading to a promised land, -not to a land of idleness, but of uniform labour; not to a place of varied and continuous bustle and excitement, but where the peaceful occupations of the shepherd, the herdsman, and the tiller of the soil, consume the year. Had the principle of keeping capital, land, and labour in due proportions, been adopted in all the A. colonies lately settled, and the system of local taxation for local purposes been instituted, many of the great desiderata of colonization would have been attained. It would have been useless for any one to have obtained a great extent of land, as it would have subjected him to local assessments for roads, bridges, and other district improvements, making his land a heavy and unceasing loss unless he cultivated it, so as to render the improvements through and contiguous to the property profitable to himself." The errors of the first settlement of the Swan river colony may be easily understood, from the fact that about 130 individuals got upwards of a million and a half of acres amongst them! IV.—NORTHERN AUSTRALIA.—The northriver on the W, a distance exceeding 3,000 m., and | including the vast gulf of Carpentaria, is to a great extent unknown. The eastern part of it, where Port-Essington is situated, abounds in trepang, pearlshell, tortoise-shell, and other products which bear a high value in the Chinese market; while its more westerly portion, from the currents prevailing along the shore, the high and rapid tides found near it, and the general character of its coast, is believed, on strong grounds, to contain the mouth of a considerable river, by which it is probable intercourse might be maintained with the interior, and many valuable districts of A. In 1824, British settlements were established on this coast, but they were withdrawn in 1829. These settlements, -which were placed, one on Melville island, in lat. 11° 30' S, long. 131° E, and about 15 m. from the main land of Australia, the other in Raffles bay, on the E side of Cobourg peninsula, which projects from the main land in about 132° E, - were originally suggested by our merchants trading with the islands of the Indian archipelago; and the plan of their establishment seems to have been well devised, although it failed from the injudicious selection made of the localities. The entrance to the harbour in Melville island was so extremely difficult, that in the four years during which it was occupied, not a single vessel touched at it, excepting such as were sent expressly with supplies; the country about it was also so arid and difficult of cultivation, that the settlement remained to the last entirely dependent on foreign supplies, even for fresh meat: the discouraged settlers were thus kept struggling from beginning to end with the elementary difficulties of colonization, and never had leisure to look abroad in search of means of turning their situation to account. Port-Raffles was also an inferior harbour, selected under an impression which subsequently proved erroneous, that Port-Essington, a noble port some miles farther W in the same peninsula, was without fresh water. Climate.] There is of course neither summer nor winter,—as these terms are understood in Europe,—in this quarter of A. The heat appears to be greater throughout the year at Port-Essington, than in Java, or even Singapore. June "Port-Essington," says Mr. Crawfurd, "is within the range of the NW and SE monsoon, that is, of the monsoon which prevails to the S of the equator, and within which is Java, and the whole chain of islands extending E of it for 1,500 m., to New Guinea; but I suspect that it is towards its extreme limit S, and the seasons are consequently less certain than in the countries which come within its full force. I come to this conclusion from the uncertainty which seems to have been experienced, both with regard to the rainy and dry seasons. Drought will, I suspect, be found the prevailing character of this, as it has been found to be of every other part of the continent of A. When the thermometer, in the coldest month, never falls below 63° in the shade, and is as high as 89°, while it must be at least 20° more in the sun, it is clear that Europeans cannot perform field, or indeed any other active bodily labour, efficiently; the salubrity of the climate will enable them to live, and even to multiply, although at the slowest rate, but they will be feeble and languishing exotics transferred to an uncongenial climate; they will be pale, with a tinge of yellow, labour under ophthalmia, and, as if they dwelt in the land of Egypt or Arabia Petrea, losing the energy of the Anglo-Saxon breed, be in time very indolent." Melville and Bathurst islands.] Melville island, between the parallels of 11° 8' and 11° 56' S, and the meridians of 130° 20' and 131° 34' E, is separated from another considerable island called Bathurst's island, by a narrow winding channel called Apsley's straits, within which the settlement was placed, and the difficulty in navigating which proved one of its greatest disadvantages. Both islands are low, and well-covered with timber; but both are alternately rocky and swampy, with few intervals of rocky soil; their coast is also thickly set with mangroves growing chiefly on salt marshes. The average height of Melville island is 70 ft., but some points rise to 200. Its aspect is at first promising, the vegetation being luxuriant; but some noxious quality seems to exist in the native grasses, for, unless when converted into hay, cattle set to them almost invariably died in a short period. The timber, though slender in the stem, is generally of a useful quality; the prevailing kind is Eucalyptus; but there are many varieties also of palms, especially the sago, grass, and cabbage-palm. Only two kinds of fruit-tree,—a species of apple and a plum,—have been found upon them. There is little underwood, unless in the swamps; the ordinary crop of timber is from 120 to 150 trees per acre. It is not difficult to clear, but excessively difficult afterwards to break up ground here; especially without draught animals which cannot, it would seem, be long kept. The native animals on Melville island are the kangaroo, opossum, bandicoot, native dog, a small brown rat, a species of squirrel, and an animal with a sharp nose, the body covered with dark brown hair, a tail fourteen inches long, and bare, like that of a rat, excessively destructive to poultry. The ternate bat, or were the prevailing complaints. The natives continued to the last unfriendly; and though not sufficiently numerous to be really formidable, their enmity narrowed the range and otherwise curtailed the comforts of the settlers. Raffles bay.] Raffles bay is on the E side of Cobourg peninsula, an extensive projection from the main land of A., in about 132° E long. The bay is of a circular form, the diameter being about 3 m.; but it is very shallow, especially on the E side, and its entrance is inconvenient, though not positively difficult. The land in its immediate vicinity is of the same character with that on Melville island, but drier and more free from swamps. The climate was also found considerably more salubrious than on Melville island, and the difficulties encountered by the settlers were, proportionally to all these circumstances, greatly less. As a trading-station, however, its success, though partial, was not sufficiently striking to induce government to retain it when Melville island was given up. Cobourg peninsula, and Port-Essington.] Port-Essington, in 11° 7' S lat., and 132° 12' E long., is a noble port, extending 17 m. in a direction SSE into the interior of Cobourg peninsula, which itself projects about 50 m. from the main land of A., and is connected with it by a narrow neck of land, 5 m. long, and not more than 2 m. wide. The peninsula afterwards widens to about 15 m., and carries this breadth along a considerable portion of its length. Its elevation varies from 30 to 300 ft. above the level of the sea; its soil is generally good, and climate healthy. The entrance to Port-Essington is open and unobstructed, so that it may be entered with almost equal ease by night as by day. Its average width is about 5 m.; its depth of water 12, 9, and 5 fath.; the rise and fall of the tide is 13 ft. At its head it divides into three spacious harbours, each extending inwards about 3 m., with a breadth of about 2 m., and perfectly land-locked. The bottom is stiff mud and sand. A town named Victoria was founded here in 1838. The shores present a pleasing variety of little bays and sandy beaches, alternating with bold cliffs and steep clay-banks. Inland, the continuous forest of trees is occasionally relieved by small round hills, rising perhaps 100 ft. above the usual level of the country, which is otherwise pleasingly undulating, being in general about 150 ft. above the level of the sea, but occasionally dipping below 60 ft. The vegetation round the port is luxuriant; the forest lands are free from underwood, and the native grasses were not found injurious to stock, like those on Melville island. The climate is also superior; the soil deeper, and the resources in every respect more abundant. Fish are caught in quantities in the port; turtle are common, and the spermwhale is in the adjoining sea. A small establishment is kept up here; but no successful attempt at colonization has yet been made. Mr. Earl says: "Throughout the Cobourg peninsula, generally near the sea-shore are found patches of land varying in extent from two or three acres to fifty acres and upwards, on which the vegetation is perfectly in character with that of the islands of the Indian archipelago. These spots are covered with a thick jungle, difficult to penetrate, and the trees are often of an enormous size. The soil is here of the richest description, and I have no doubt that it would produce any articles that are grown at the Moluccas. I do not think, however, that more than 1,000 acres of this land will be found in the entire peninsula. A considerable proportion of the timber-trees which cover the face of the country are hollowed out in the centre by the white ant. On the S side of the peninsula, Lieut. Stewart, and others who have penetrated in that direction, saw numbers of cedar-trees many of which were 10 ft. in circumference, and 30 ft. in length from the root to the lower branches. The cypress was also met with, together with a tree from which the natives form their canoes, the trunks being 12 ft. in circumf., and from 50 to 60 ft. long. The Macassar trepang-fishers find that the timber, generally, is well-adapted for repairing their prahus, and also for the construction of masts and wooden anchors. The latter are made from a tough and heavy description of timber which has received the name of iron-wood, burden, are said to be employed in the fishery, the and these are so highly prized at Macassar that many are carried away by the prahus every year. The bark obtained from the roots of the Morinda citifolia, which yields a red dye, together with an inferior kind of sandal-wood found upon the coast, are also exported by the Macassars whenever their prahus are not too deeply laden with trepang; and these, as far as I have been able to discover, are at present the only vegetable productions of the coast that constitute articles of commerce. The various grasses closely resemble those of New South Wales. They do not cover the surface of the ground with their roots, as is the case in the meadows of England, but grow in tufts or tussocks, the arrangement being very similar to the tufts of hair in a shoe-brush. During the rainy season the grass shoots up with great rapidity, the seed-stems of some of the larger kinds attaining a height of from 6 to 8 ft." The natives in Cobourg peninsula resemble those on Melville island, but were not found by any means so intractable. Their countenances are intelligent, and perhaps fierce; but after intercourse with them was fairly established, they behaved well, and with less caprice than is usually found among savages. They were found to be, in all respects, indeed, much superior to their brethren near Port Jackson, being divided into three castes, or classes, between whom a distinct difference of rank was observable, but not any separation of occupations. The first were superior to the others in form and stature, as well as in rank. Their women were seldom seen, and the entire number of natives within the peninsula was not believed to be considerable. They are great turtle hunters; but do not use the trepang, which bears so high a value in the Chinese markets, and which abounds along their shores. A few Bugis visit the neighbouring coast occasionally in search of trepang, or the sea-slug. In point of size and appearance it resembles a prickly cucumber, except that the colour is generally a whitish brown; but there are several varieties, one of which is perfectly black. The trepang is found in all the sheltered harbours, where it gropes about the bottom, and feeds upon weeds and mollusca. It is taken at low-water, upon the shoals or mud-banks, over which the fishermen wade, dragging their boats after them; and when the feet come in contact with a slug, it is picked up and thrown into the boat. They occasionally search in deeper water, when the fishermen avail themselves of the services of the natives, who are expert divers; or if they cannot obtain such assistance, they prick for them with barbed iron darts, provided with long bamboo handles. The process of curing is very simple. The slug, on being taken from the boat, is simmered over a fire in an iron cauldron for about half-an-hour; after which it is thrown out upon the ground, and the operation of opening commences, this being effected by a longitudinal cut along the back with a sharp knife. It is then again placed in the cauldron and boiled in salt water, with which a quantity of the bark of the mangrove has been mixed, for about three hours, when the outer skin will begin to peel off. It is now sufficiently boiled; and after the water has been drained off, the slugs are arranged in the drying-houses, upon frames of split bamboo, spread out immediately under the roof. Each slug is carefully placed with the part that has been cut open facing downwards, and a fire is made underneath, the smoke of which soon dries the trepang sufficiently to permit its being packed in bags or baskets for exportation. The entire process is a simple operation, and the utensils required are far from being costly. It is difficult to form an estimate as to the amount of trepang annually exported from the coast. From 30 to 40 prahus, varying from 20 to 70 tons P crews amounting to about 1,200 men. The provisions and stores required for the voyage are advanced by Chinese or Dutch merchants at Macassar, who thus acquire a right to the entire proceeds at a certain price previously fixed upon. Perhaps the average amount of trepang obtained by each prahu may be estimated at 20 tons. The value at Macassar is 70 rupees, or somewhat less than £7 sterling, for the picul of 133 lbs. avoird. The price to the consumer in China is enhanced to the amount of about one-third. The value of this commodity as an article of export to China has long been known to the people of New South Wales, and small vessels have been from time to time sent out from Sydney to collect trepang on the northern coasts, but the success met with appears not to have been sufficiently great to afford much encouragement: indeed, it is an employment for which European seamen are by no means well adapted. [Earl.] Among the native fruits is a species of nutmeg of fine flavour; and there is no doubt that sugar, rice, and coffee, and perhaps cotton and indigo, might be produced in this quarter of A. Timber, gums, lichens, and mimosa bark, may be collected with a trifling degree of labour, if means of transport or flottage to the sea-coast could be found.-An attempt was recently made to found a settlement at Port Curtis, which is, however, rather on the E than the N coast of A., being between Sandy cape and Broad sound. It has not, however, proved successful. Mr. Jukes says, "Port Bowen, a little N of Port Curtis, is, at all events, hopelessly barren, and a great mass of sterile hills seemed to stretch along the coast to the S. North of Broad sound, however, and from it to Rockingham bay, the coast had a more promising appearance than any other part of the coast which I have seen. Good land was often found by us close to the sea, as soon as we had penetrated the belt of mangroves which always margin its bays and inlets. From the recently published accounts of Dr. Leichhardt's arduous journey, it appears that several important streams fall into this tract from the dividing range, among others the Burdekin, apparently the largest river he met with. Had the recently attempted settlement, under Col. Barney, only gone as far as West-hill or its neighbourhood, I think they would have been tempted to make a longer stay before abandoning the enterprise. The country about Cape Palmerston, with the many steep and woody islands fronting it, was often really charming in appearance, well-wooded, grassed, and watered, and the climate appeared to be delightful. I am very unwilling to use strong terms, for fear I might be the means of leading any person astray, but I certainly think the country between Broad sound and Cape Upstart deserving of exploration, with a view to its immediate occupation, when once a steamer shall regularly traverse its shores. Whether its staple shall be cotton, or wool, or any other commodity, is utterly out of my province to decide. The great range of the tide, however, near Cape Palmerston, amounting at the springs to 30 ft., and the deep and secure harbourage about Port Molle in Whitsunday-passage, together with the abundance and variety of the timber, point out this tract as the probable locality of the future shipyards of that part of the world."
AUSTREY, a parish in Warwickshire, 6 m. ENE of Tamsworth. Acres 2,280. Pop. 479.

AUSTRIA. An extensive empire formed by the union of several kingdoms and states, in Southern and Central Europe, with the original or hereditary domains of the prean Italianized modification of the German name Oesterreich, signifying 'the Eastern kingdom,' and indicating the geographical position of the archduchy of Austria, in relation to the rest of Germany. The cradle of the A. empire was the territory below the Ens. In the time of Charlemagne, about 800, the margraviate of A. was formed by a body of militia which protected the SE of Germany from the incursions of the Asiatic tribes. In 1156, it was united with the territory above the Ens, and made a duchy. In 1282 the state began to consolidate under the dominion of the house of Hapsburg. This dynasty soon added several new territories, which afterwards formed the A. circle, and in 1438 obtained the electoral crown of the German emperors. In 1453, A. was raised to an archduchy; and, having acquired Bohemia, and Hungary in 1526, with the consent of the inhabitants it attained the rank of a European monarchy. The Lorraine branch of the house of A. maintained this rank at the peace of Aix-lachapelle, signed in the year 1748. They confirmed the union of their territories by elevating the monarchy, in 1804, to a hereditary empire; and established its dignity as one of the chief powers in Europe, before, during, and after the congress of Vienna, in 1815. Boundaries.] The Austrian dominions are bounded on the NW and N, along a frontier-line of 570 m., by Saxony and Prussian Silesia; on the NE and E by the ci-devant free territory of Cracovia, and the Russian provinces of Podolia, Volhynia, and Bessarabia, for a distance of about 530 m. On the remaining E part, and the larger portion of the S frontier, the A. dominions, along a line of nearly 1,400 m., adjoin the Russo-Turkish provinces of Moldavia and Wallachia, and the Turkish provinces of Servia, Bosnia, and Croatia. The Adriatic then laves the A. shore for about 650 m.; and the remainder of the S boundary skirts the States of the Church, the duchies of Modena and Parma, and the Sardinian states. On the W, A. borders for 330 m. on the Swiss cantons of Tessino, the Valais, and St. Gall. The principality of Liechtenstein, and the lake of Constance, here interpose themselves; after which the frontier-line, in this direction, runs for 550 m. along Bavaria. This extensive frontier of above 4,000 m., though of irregular outline, is well-defined by strong natural boundaries, except on the NE; and forms a compact mass, with the exception of a narrow strip running along the coast of the Adriatic. Extent. The extent of the A. dominions from W to E, is 720 geographical, or 820 British m., taking it from the western extremity of the Voralberg on the Rhine, in 9° 30' E long., and 47° N lat., to the eastern confines of the Buckovine on the Sereth, parting it from Moldavia, in 27° E long.; but if we take it from the confluence of the Inn and Danube at Passau, in E long. 13° 45' and 48° 35' N lat., to the junction of the Podhorcze and Dniester, in 26° 20' E long. and 48° 45' N lat., the length will be only 520 geographical, or 623 British m. Measuring from the western boundary of the Austro-Italian states, in N lat. 46° and 8° 40' E. long., where the Ticino flows out of Lago-Maggiore, to the river Sereth, it will amount to 900 British m. The greatest breadth from the frontiers of Bohemia, in 51° N lat., to the southern point of the peninsula of Istria, in 45° N lat., is 415 British m.; and if we draw a line from Zawichost on the Vistula, 8 m. to the N of the confluence of that river with the San, to the northern bank of the Danube, and its most southerly winding in the banat of Temiswar, the breadth will be nearly the same. The kingdom of DALMATIA, with the ci-devant Venetian territory of Albania, the republic of Ragusa, and the islands in the Adriatic Next to Russia, the Austrian empire contains a greater variety of population than any other country in Europe. One-half of the whole population is of Slavonian extraction. The Slavonian provinces are Bohemia, Moravia, Silesia, Galicia, Illyria, Dalmatia, and the Military frontiers. The northern part of Hungary likewise is inhabited by Slavonians; and part of Styria. Above 7,000,000 are of German descent. The Magyars, who form the mass of the pop. in Hungary, are about 5,000,000 in number. The Italians may amount to 5,200,000; the Valaks or Rumuni, to 2,200,000; the Jews to 500,000; and the Gypsies to 120,000; a multitude of tribes, -all differing in their manners, languages, religion, and customs, -mutually strangers to each other, -and having opposite views, interests, and constitutions, holding or fancying themselves independent of the central state and of each other, except so far as the sway of the imperial sceptre is in question, -form the medley and ill-jointed pop. of A. It is this heterogeneous assemblage of tribes and nations, this diversity of language and manners, and consequent collision of interests and opinions, that so long prevented the A. empire—though physically strong—from exerting her whole collected strength, and becoming a fair match for the banded power of France; and that now threatens its very existence. And not only is there this want of adhesion in the pop. of the A. empire, but even its dominions were, until the changes effected in 1814 and 1815, geographically ill-connected, and disjointed. A long time was thus necessary for the march of levies from one extremity of the empire to the other. The A. territories are now more consolidated than before; and, if she can retain possession of the Venetian territories, A. has it in her power to become a maritime state, and to enjoy the advantages of a foreign commerce. The whole line of sea-coast, reaching from the mouth of the Po to the head of the gulf of Quarnaro, and from this last gulf, in a continuous line, to the borders of the gulf of Drino, in Upper Albania,-a tract of more than 500 m., -forms, from its political and commercial importance, as is justly remarked by Oppenheimer, "a golden seam or border to the monarchy." The A. empire, moreover, presents vast capabilities of improvement. The assertion that the pop. of England or France might be doubled, by adopting throughout these kingdoms at large the improved agriculture of certain districts, is perhaps a little too sanguine; but it is evidently not so to hazard such an assertion respecting A., whose provinces are as fertile as those of France or England, while their density of pop. is much inferior. Hungary and Bohemia, in particular, may vie with any countries in the world. of the same extent, in the abundance and excellence of their productions, their plentiful crops of grain, their numerous herds of cattle, and the variety of their metals and minerals. But the enigmatic situation in which this empire has been placed by recent and still evolving events,-by internal convulsions and complications, and external difficulties,-renders it hazardous to predict the future career of A., or of any of its component states. Physical features.] The physical features of A. are more fully described in the pages of this work under the head of the different countries and physical regions of which it is composed, as no general description can possibly apply to the whole. The Alps in the SW, and the Carpathians in the NE, are the principal mountain-chains of A. The varied ramifications of these great mountain-systems, besides numerous detached groups, confer on A. the general character of a mountainous country. The Tyrol, Styria, Carniola, Carinthia, and Dalmatia, are pre-eminently Alpine regions. The Carpathiansystem covers extensive portions of Hungary, Galicia, and Transylvania. The Hercynian-system comprises the Sudetes, and various branches intersecting Bohemia and Moravia.—Immense plains and extensive valleys also present themselves in A.; but they do not compose above a fourth part of the superficial area of the empire: those of Lombardy, Hungary, and Galicia, are the most extensive. Rivers. The rivers of A. which terminate their course in the North sea or Atlantic ocean, are: the Elbe, which takes its rise in the Riesengebirge, and making a curve through Bohemia, enters Saxony in its course to the sea. Within the A. territories it passes Josephstadt, Kolin, Koenigingraetz, and Leutmeritz. Its principal Austrian affluents are, on the r. the Iser; and on the l. the Adler, the Moldau, which waters Budweis and Prague, and receives on the l. the Beraun, and the Eger which flows past Egra and Theresienstadt.—The Rhine touches the southern extremity of the gov. of Tyrol. The Baltic sea receives the Oder, which rises in the mountains of Moravia, and after having traversed Austrian Silesia, enters Prussia. Its principal tributaries are, on the l. the Oppa, which bathes Troppau; and on the r. the Olsa or Elsa, which passes Teschen. —The Vistula, or Weichsel, which takes its rise in Austrian Silesia, and forms to a great extent the line of separation between Galicia and the kingdom of Poland. Its chief affluents—all of which are on the r.—are the Biala; the Dunajec, which passes Neumark and Neu-Sandec. It receives the Poprad, the Visloka, the San, which waters Sank and Przmysl, and the Burg, which passes Busk. All of these streams—with the exception of the last, which in its greater extent belongs to Russia-complete their course within the confines of Galicia. The Black sea receives the Danube, which is the greatest river in the A. dominions, and which intersects Upper and Lower Austria, runs through Hungary, separates that prov. on the S from Slavonia, and the Banat from Servia, and enters the Ottoman empire at Orsova, passing in its course Lintz, Klosterneuburg, Vienna, Presburg, Raab, Comorn, Grau, Buda, Pesth, Neusatz, Peterwardein, and Semlin. This magnificent river, out of a course of 2,000 m. has 850 m. in A., with an average breadth throughout of 600 ft. Until it reaches the plains of Hungary, the current of the Danube is very rapid, having through a great part of A. an average speed of 5 to 6 ft. per second, or about 4 m. per hour. The width of the river is unequal; near Vienna, for instance, it is 18,000 ft., or 3.4 m.; at Belgrade, only 3,160 ft.; at the 'Iron Port,' 500 ft.; and at Orsova, where the Danube leaves the A. territory, the width is 2,053 ft. The swiftness and changeableness of its current, its shallowness in many places, winding course, and cliffs, &c., offer great obstacles to navigation, especially up the stream. Nevertheless, thousands of boats sail down or up the river to Vienna, with provisions, and all kinds of agricultural and mineral products. Two able surveys of this river have been completed at different seasons of the year,—one very perfect, by the government, the other by the Danube steam company. It is not generally navigable higher than Gönyo (70 m. above Pest) to vessels of more draught than from 2 to 21 ft. The next difficulty is at a cluster of banks near Pacs, and afterwards acute windings in the course of the stream at Tolna. There may be estimated about 30 m. of difficult navigation in the whole course of the stream from Pest to Drenkova. It is the obstructions existing in the bed of the river between the latter place and Skela- Gladova, a distance of about 50 m., that are the real barriers to its thorough navigation. Its principal affluents are, on the r. the Inn, which traverses Northern Tyrol and passes Innsbruck and Schwatz; the Salza, which waters Hallein and Saltzburg; the Traun, which passes Wels; the Ens, or Ems, which bathes Steyer and Ens, and is joined by the Steyer; the Trasse and the Leitha in Austria; the Raab and Sarwitz in Hungary; the Drave, which passes Villach, Marburg, Eszeck, and receives the Mur, which waters Graetz, and traverses Styria, the kingdom of Illyria, Croatia, Slavonia, and Hungary; the Save, which marks the SE frontier of the Austrian territory, passes Gurkfeld, Agram, Gradisca, Brodt, and is joined by the Kulpa, which flows past Carlstadt, and the Unna, which bathes Costanicza. The chief tributaries of the Danube on the l. are the Morawa or March, which intersects Moravia, passing Olmutz, and receives the Taga, which waters Znaim, and is joined by the Iglava, &c.; the Waag, which passes Rosenberg and Trenschin; the Grau, from the direction of Bries and Neusol; the Ipoly or Eipel; the Theiss or Tisza, the largest of the affluents of the Danube, which passes Szigeth, Tokay, Csongrad, Szegedin, and receives the Szamos, which passes Zalmar; the Bodrogh, from the direction of Saros- Patah and Tokay; the Hernauth, which passes Kassau, and the Erlau, which waters the town of the same name; the Sago and the Koeroes; the Marosch, which passes Karlsburg and Neu-Arad; the Bega, from the direction of Temeswas; and the Temes, from Lugosch, all of which belong to Hungary and Transylvania; the Atuta, which passes Forgaras in Transylvania; the Sereth, which waters Sereth; and the Pruth, which flows past Snyatin and Czernowitz in Galicia.— The Dniester issues from a branch of the Carpathian mountains in Galicia, passes Sambor and Halitz, and enters Russia in its passage to the Black sea. Its principal affluents within the A. territory are the Stry and the Bistriz on the r., and on the l. the Sered and Podhorze. The Adriatic sea receives the Po, which rises in Piedmont, and runs along the greater extent of the southern frontier of Venetian Lombardy, waters Cremone, Cassalmaggiore, and Viadana, and falls by several embouchures into the sea. Its chief tributaries are the Ticino, which issues from Lake Maggiore, and waters Pavia; the Olono, which bathes Milan; the Adda, which passes Sondrio, traverses the lake of Como, flows past Lodi and Pizzighettone, and is joined by the Serio from the direction of Crema; the Oglio, which passes through the lake of Iseo, bathes Pontevico, and receives the Milla and the Chiese, the latter of which waters Monte-Chiaro; the Mincio, which issues from the lake of Garda, passes Peschiara, and traverses the lake of Mantone. The Kerka, Cettina, and Narenta, intersect Dalmatia, and fall into the Adriatic; the first after passing Knin and Sebenico; the second, Sing and Almissa; and the third, Fort Opus. Lakes.] The lakes of A. are numerous, and many of them are extensive. The Plattensee or Balaton, and the Neusiedlersee, belong to Hungary; the Huof Austria. Lakes are numerous in Illyria, Dalma- Lago di Garda, are wholly within the Austrian territory.-Extensive marshes occur along the banks of the Danube, the Theiss, the Drave, and other rivers. The northern region, between the 49th and 51st parallels, comprising Bohemia, and the higher parts of Moravia, Galicia, and Silesia. The domestic animals are numerous. In Transylvania and the Buckovine horses of a fine breed are reared; and the government maintains immense studs in Hungary and Galicia for the supply of the army. The choicest breeds of cattle are reared in Hungary, Styria, and Lombardy. The pastures of Eastern Hungary and Transylvania support immense flocks of sheep. The total production of wool in the A. empire is officially estimated, according to a French report, at weight. Foreign wool is imported for 2\frac{1}{2} millions of kilogrammes; and 8 millions are exported chiefly to England and France. The remainder is worked up in the domestic manufactories. The silkworm is extensively reared in Lombardy and Venice, and in the southern districts of Tyrol and Dalmatia. Bees are an object of much attention in Hungary, Galicia, and Transylvania. Minerals: ] In metalliferous products A. is unrivalled in Europe, though its mines have been comparatively neglected till within these few years. As far as the precious metals are concerned, the A. empire comprises the most productive mining-districts in Europe. In the five years, from 1830 to 1834 inclusive, its mines yielded, on an average, 5,134 marks (equal to ½ lb. each) of gold, and 93,362 marks of silver yearly. About 2,148 marks of gold were raised yearly in Hungary, and 2,845 in Transylvania; these two provinces thus contributing together nearly 49-50ths of the entire average; while of silver, Hungary contributed 63,754 marks; Bohemia, 22,124 ditto; and Transylvania, 4,611 ditto; as their yearly average within the same period. In 1837, the produce of Hungary, in gold, was 2,149 marks, and in silver 64,224 marks, as officially reported; but there is reason to believe that the quantities actually produced were considerably more. The quantities here stated are certainly not at all in proportion with the immense mineral resources of the territory. A. abounds also with salt, alum, bismuth, manganese, with clays proper for porcelain and pottery, and pyrites, granites, &c. The average production of coal for the last few years is estimated at 600,000 tons. That of salt some years ago was 5,885,451 cwt. Manufactures.] The city of Vienna engrosses by far the greater proportion of the manufacturing wealth and industry of A. Proper; but in the Italian provinces every branch of trade is carried on by more individuals, and is usually brought to greater perfection than even at Vienna. While Vienna, in 1839, was rated for the industry-tax on an amount of capital of 24,150,000 florins, and Bohemia on only 6,888,697 florins, Lombardy and Venice were rated at 139,000,000 florins. An article in the Augsburg Gazette, states that without reckoning Hungary, Transylvania, and the Military Frontiers, the A. empire contained, at the close of 1839, 13,361 manufactories and fabrics, specified as follows:- Silk-spinning and weaving . . . Of the silk-spinning and winding establishments above enumerated, 4,979 were in Italy, and 69 in Tyrol. In Bohemia, Moravia, and Silesia there are from 210 to 215 establishments in which the spinning of carded wool is carried on, producing, from an aggregate of 425,000 spindles, and 170,000 metrical quintals of wool, about 100,000 metrical quintals of woollen yarn; the French metrical quintal being equal to about 1 cwt. 3 qrs. 25 lbs. The spinning is, for the most part, conducted in connexion, and on the same premises, with the manufacture of woollen fabrics. There are, however, some extensive spinneries devoted solely to the production of yarn for sale among the neighbouring manufacturers. That of Soxhlet Brothers, at Brunn in Moravia, may be cited as one of the most important and best organised establishments of the kind. These gentlemen had, a few years ago, for moving-power only what is known here as a gin horse turning a wheel. Now they have a steam engine of 60 horse-power, employ more than 900 operatives, work 30,000 spindles, and consume 600,000 kilogrammes of wool, or 1,312,500 lbs. per annum. In the spinning of combed wool, A. is far in arrear of production for the supply of her own consumption. There are only fifteen or sixteen factories, with an aggregate of 32 or 33,000 spindles among them, throwing-off about 5,000 metrical quintals of yarn of the value of some £260,000, and employing 16 or 17,000 work-people. The yarn produced, both of the fine and lower numbers, is, however, much esteemed for its quality.
