AUSTRIA (ARCHDUCHY OF), an important province, "the very core," of the A. empire, lying on both sides of the Danube, and divided by the river Ens or Ems into the provinces of the Upper and the Lower Ens, or Upper and Lower Austria. It is bounded by Bohemia and Moravia on the N; by Hungary on the E; by Styria on the S; and by Bavaria on the W. In former times, the lower part formed the duchy of A., the most ancient possessions of the house of A.; the upper belonged to Bavaria. The former had been conquered from the Avari, and peopled by Franks and Rhenish tribes; the latter, by Bojarians or Bavarians. Even at this day the different races may be distinguished in the pop. of the country. Physical features.] One-third of the superficies of this country is made up of mountains, forests, lakes, and pasture; the other two-thirds consist of meadows, corn-fields, vineyards, and the sites of towns and villages. The soil—especially on the banks of the Danube, and of the larger streams which run into that river—is either a deep clayey loam, or a deposition of schistose and calcareous matter hurried down by the torrents from the mountains. On the higher grounds it is lighter and thinner, but is well-adapted to corn and grass husbandry. The surface of the whole prov. is a gradual slope, from the southern and the northern hills, towards the Danube, which flows through the heart of the country, and receives every stream which pervades it, nearly at right angles from both sides. The traveller following the course of the Danube, whether ascending or descending the river, sees a large expanse of the country on either hand. This general declivity to the Danube—which is uniform and gradual for many leagues on each bank—greatly facilitates the draining of the ground, and the carriage of bulky commodities from the interior of the province to the markets and places of resort on the river. The soil is well-cultivated, and affords a rich and beautiful prospect; while the numerous towns and villages on the Danube,—the variegated and majestic woods skirting the hills which rise gradually on either side,—the monasteries, castles, spires, farm-houses, and villages, peeping out here and there from the trees,—the majestic river with its swiftly gliding stream, appearing and disappearing amidst its wooded islands,—the whole A. is supplied with salt, to the value of £400,000 sterscene girdled round by a varied range of distant mountains,-all these features strike every stranger who visits the archd., and justly entitle it to rank among the most picturesque and charming countries in the world. Mountains. A. is divided on the south from Styria, by a branch of the Noric Alps, called the Simmering heights, over which a noble road was made in 1728. A range—supposed by Busching to be the Mons Cetius of the Romans, and which was the eastern boundary of Noricum, dividing it from Pannonia—extends from near the source of the Save towards the Danube. This range is generally known by the name of the Kalenberg; and 9 m. to the W of Vienna is denominated the Leopoldsberg. The Schneeberg, in the SW part of the Lower Wienerwald circle, is the most remarkable mountain in Lower A., and is distinctly seen from the ramparts of Vienna in a clear day. Its height indeed is not very considerable, when compared with other mountains of the same range, being only 5,200 ft. above the level of the Danube at Vienna, or 5,646 above the level of the Mediterranean; but being insulated, and almost always clothed with snow near the summit—from which circumstance it obtains the appellation of Schneeberg, or 'the Snowy mountain'-it strikes a stranger more than any other mountain in the duchy.—Upper A. abounds in mountains, especially the parts S of the Danube. In the circle of Salzaal are the Grossglockner 12,221 ft. in alt.; the Ankogel 11,798 ft.; the Krummhorn, 11,770 ft. Rivers.] The Danube rolls in great and rapid majesty through this fine country. Hemmed in by mountains it flows past Lintz in one unbroken stream. Below that city it again expands, and divides into several arms, one of which may be considered the main channel, as offering the principal course of the intricate navigation on this river. Thus it continues till it reaches a celebrated whirlpool near Grein, where all its waters unite in one channel, and thence flow majestically onwards till they have worked their way through the mountains and passes near Krems, and, coming to level ground again, divide and form numerous arms and islands beyond Vienna. Several other rivers of considerable magnitude, as the Ens, the Traun, the Ips, and the Trasin, traverse this duchy. Vast quantities of timber are annually floated down these rivers from the woody mountains, to provide fuel to the inhabitants of the plains. This labour gives employment to about onefourth of the population, during spring and winter. The A. rivers not only vary greatly in colour from one another, but also present waters of a different aspect at different seasons of the year. The Danube alone, retains a milky-green colour all the year round. No green can be more beautiful and lively than that of the waters of the Traun and the Ens until they begin to be affected by the autumnal rains. Near their sources, amidst high mountains 6,000 or 7,000 ft. above the level of the Danube, the head-springs of the A. waters are always green, and impregnated with fine particles of schistose and calcareous sand, which it is conjectured produce those glandular swellings of the neck, here so common, in Switzerland as well as in A. called kropf, and in France goitres .- The Vienna, or Neustadt canal, connects the Donau canal, an enlarged branch of the Danube near Vienna, with Wiener-Neustadt, 34 m. S of the capital. Lakes.] The lake of Traun or the Gmundersee, with the fine river Traun running through it, and the two charming towns of Gmundin and Ebsdorff, at each extremity, 12 m. distant from one another, is much frequented by tourists, on account both of the scenery of its banks, and the salt-manufactures which are established there. From this lake and its vicinity, ling annually. The Attersee, the Abersee, the Albensee, the Hallstättersee, the Mattsee or Mondsee, the Neussee, the Zellersee, the Altausee, and the Gosachsee, are small sheets of water, but afford fine and varied landscapes, and greatly facilitate the carriage of wood to the salt-pans, and of all sorts of commodities to the numerous population which dwells along their shores.-Swamps and morasses of considerable extent occur.—The principal mineral waters are those of Baden in the Lower Wienerwald, and of Gastein in Salzach. Climate.] The climate of this portion of the empire varies greatly, from the mountainous borders of Styria and Bohemia, to the lower frontiers of Hungary, and the banks of the Danube. That of Upper A. is much more severe than that of Lower A. The summer is there short and precarious; and the hopes of the husbandman are often blasted by frosts and tempests in the autumnal months. Winter sets in about the end of October; and the ground is, for the most part, covered with snow to the middle of March. The average quantity of rain which falls at the towns of Gmunden and Hallstadt, in Upper A., which are environed by mountains, and lie on the lakes to which they give their names, is from 38 to 46 inches; while the quantity which falls at Vienna, rarely exceeds 28 inches. The most common diseases in the mountainous parts, are pulmonary complaints, typhus and intermitting fevers, colds, rheumatisms, and epidemical distempers brought from Italy and Turkey. S and SW winds, blowing from the Styrian, Carinthian, and Tyrolese Alps, over a snowy region of several hundred miles in extent, are severely felt. N winds are the pleasantest; but the E winds are the most piercing and constant. During the months of July, August, and September, the heat is excessive along the banks of the Danube, and in the lower country, Fahrenheit's thermometer frequently indicating 95° and 98° in the shade. Tempestuous winds seldom annoy the lower districts; and the climate is as favourable for animals, for grass, corn, wood, and even some species of wines, as any part of Europe in the same latitude.—Lintz, the most western city of A., on the Danube, is said to be 1,000 ft. above the level of the Black sea; and Hainburg, near Presburg, the most easterly, is about 780 ft. above the same level. Animal kingdom.] Few countries are more productive, in proportion to extent, whether in the animal, the vegetable, or mineral department, than the archd. of A. The breeds of horses, mules, asses, cattle, sheep, goats, and all the common European domesticated animals, as well as of game and wild fowls, are among the best in Germany. Much attention has been paid to the improvement of the breed of horses since the reign of Joseph II., by introducing English, Mecklenburg, and the best Turkish stallions, and by encouraging English grooms to settle in the country. The Pinzgauer horses are the largest breed in Europe, standing generally 19 hands high. The horned cattle of Upper A. are likewise of a large breed. The sheep of Lower A. have been greatly improved by careful crossing; those of Upper A. are inferior. The lynx, wolf, bear, and most of the smaller European animals of prey, occur in the forests and mountains of Upper A. Minerals.] Iron, silver, copper, lead, cobalt, gypsum, lime, marble, coals, salt, and sulphur, are among the mineral products of the archd.; but the mineral wealth of this portion of A. is not remarkable. Agriculture.] As compared with the other provinces, the archd. of A. may be denominated a wellmanaged and rich agricultural country. In this respect it surpasses Hungary and Bavaria. In 1829, the productive surface of Lower A. was estimated at 3,301,453 jochs, of which 1,399,910 were arable land; 80,153 vineyards; 447,758 in meadows and gardens; 251,347 in commons; and 1,122,285 in forests. The productive surface of Upper A. was 2,742,914 jochs, of which 975,523 were arable land; 73 vineyards; 530,916 meadows and gardens; 119,640 commons; and 1,116,914 forests. The corn-produce of Lower A. amounted in 1829 to 8,744,901, and in 1837 to 13,200,851 metzen; a metzen being equal to 1.7 of an English bushel; and that of Upper A. in 1829 to 7,288,910, and in 1837 to 6,337,217 metzen. The grain chiefly cultivated was rye and oats. A sort of rotation of white and green crops is observed, and the raising and harvesting of hay is perfectly wellunderstood. The crops commonly cultivated are wheat, barley, oats, rye, pease, beans, potatoes, saffron, mustard, hemp, flax, and a few grasses as vetches, tares, and clovers. In comparison with Northern Germany-some parts of Mecklenburg and Holstein excepted—the crops are heavy and productive; but if compared with the best-managed counties in England and Scotland, they are by no means considerable in proportion to the fertility of the soil. Six bolls, Linlithgow measure, or three quarters of wheat, per acre, are esteemed a good crop; and four bolls of barley or oats, rather exceed the common average. The A. peasant is not a tenant in our sense of the word, but a feuar, who has his land very cheap, and does not calculate upon what a certain quantity of seed-corn will yield him. Hence he sows but very sparingly, perhaps 6 or 7 pecks, or 21 bushels, per acre; and is perfectly contented if he has 6 or 7 fold from his seed. He ploughs to the depth of two or three inches, and manages his ground precisely as his forefathers did in the days of Charles V., or of Rudolph of Hapsburg. Yet, it is the opinion of Kohl, that the peasants of Upper and Lower A. have, with the exception of a part of those of Lombardy, reached a higher degree of wealth and comparative independence, than any other peasants in the empire. An odd law prevails in this class: the farm descends to the youngest instead of the eldest son on the death of the father. The land-tax in Lower A. averages 1s. 5d. per joch, or about 1s. per acre; in Upper A., it is nearly 1s. per joch. Wine is a chief article of produce in the country east of the Ens. A considerable proportion of the arable land of the whole of Lower A. is occupied by vineyards, which pay at least one-fourth of what we call the landed rent of the province. The wine made is white, and of an acidulous taste; and when kept for a year or two, is both palatable and wholesome. It improves till the age of twenty years, and sells wholesale, from the cellars on Vienna, at about 8d. per bottle. The forests are extensive. The vine is not cultivated in Upper A.; but cider and perry are made in large quantities. Manufactures.] Manufactures are carried on in the archd. of A. to a considerable extent. In Vienna woollens, silks, cottons, leather, iron, steel, glass, porcelain, paper, toys, household furniture, &c., are manufactured; and the exports from the cap. to the different prov. of the empire, amount to the value of £1,200,000 annually in manufactured goods. In 1837 the number of manufacturers in Vienna was 162; of bankers 18; of merchants 91; and the cap. employed was estimated at 4,594,700 florins. In Lower A., exclusive of Vienna, there were 237 manufacturers, and a cap. of 2,519,800 fl. In Upper A. 132 manufacturers, and a cap. of 2,905,828 fl. As late as 1783, the woollen manufactures of Lintz were almost the only establishments of the kind in A. Since the abolition of the monopolies held by the manufacturing company in that city, these manufactures have extended to Brunn, Vienna, and throughout Lower A. and Moravia. The carpets and woollen tablecovers of Lintz, however, still retain a superiority in the home-markets. Population.] Hoeck estimated the pop. of Upper and Lower Austria at 1,820,000, in 1832; Schlosser, a German journalist, at 2,100,000. De Serres—who, in 1814, published a statistical account of the Austrian empire, in four volumes 8vo.—at 1,700,000: Lower Austria containing 1,050,000, and Upper Austria 650,000. In 1841 the pop. of Lower A. amounted to 1,410,448, exclusive of the pop. of Vienna which in 1840 was 357,927. The pop. of Upper A. in 1841 was 858,746. The mass of the pop. is German; but on the Moravian frontiers we find some Slavonians. High Dutch is the language chiefly spoken. Topographical divisions.] Lower A. is divided into the government of Vienna, and the four bailiwicks of the Upper and Lower Wienerwald, and the Upper and Lower Manhartsberg. The general statistics of these divisions in 1837 were as follow: Area in Towns. Villages. Pop. A. sq. m. City of Vienna, Vienna, the cap. of the empire, and the largest city in Germany, is situated on the r. bank of the Danube, in 48° 12′ 36" N lat., and 16° 16′ 42" E long. See Vienna.—Upper A is divided into the five circles of Muhl-Viertel, Traun-Viertel, Hausruck- Viertel, Inn-Viertel, and Salzburg. The general statistics of these divisions in 1837 were as follow: Area in Towns, Villages, Pop. A. sq. m. [Linz city, Administration.] The following are the fundamental features of the new electoral law for Lower A. The landtag is to consist of 90 members, divided into three classes, which will form one assembly. Vienna chooses 26. The smallest number of highesttaxed electors must be 25. Every one, who has been an Austrian citizen (staats-bürger) during 5 years, is at least 30 years of age, and possesses the active right of election in a commune of the province, is eligible for election. Voting at the elections is to be viva voce and public. The deputies are to be chosen for 4 years, and then to be re-eligible. The landtag is to be called together for 6 weeks in each year; and elects as a landes-ausschuss, or standing committee, two deputies out of each class. The landesausschuss is to be in immediate relations of business with the statthalter, or governor. The matters which are considered as belonging to the jurisdiction of the provincial diets are "1. The agriculture of the crownlands. 2. Public buildings erected at the expense of the crownland. 3. The charitable institutions. 4. The revenues arising from the provincial property; the taxation for carrying out provincial objects; and the employment of the provincial credit for covering its usual and extraordinary outlay."
AUTAS, a town of Arabia, in the Hedjaz, 70 m. NE of Mecca, in N lat. 21° 50'.

AUTERIVE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Haute-Garonne, arrond. of Muret. The cant. comprises 11 communes, and in 1831 contained a pop. of 9,221. The town lies on the r. bank of the Arriege, 12 m. SE of Muret, and 17 SSE of Toulouse. Pop. 3,172.
AUTES, a town of Peru, in the prov. of Junin, on the l. bank of the Perene, about 15 m. above its confluence with the Apurimac.

AUTEUIL, a commune and village of France, in | France, in the dep. of Haute-Saone, and arrond. of AUT the dep. of Seine-et-Oise, and canton of Neuilly, 4 m. W of Paris. The church contains the tombs of Aguesseau and Helvetius. There are printing-mills, chemical-works, &c., here. Pop. 2,764.

AUTGURH, a district, fortress, and town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Orissa and district of Cuttack. The district extends to the state of Ourpun on the N, and to the fortress of Tigria on the W; and comprises an area 15 m. in length from N to S, and about 12 from E to W. It is to a great extent covered with jungle, and is consequently very unhealthy. Its principal productions are grain-inclusive of rice,-tobacco, sugar, and oil. The annual tribute amounted in 1813 to only 6,868 rupees.—The fortress lies about 12 m. WNW of Cuttack.
AUTHEZAT-LA-SAUVETAT, a village of France, in the dep. of Puy-de-Dôme, cant. of Veyne. Pop. 1,811.

AUTHIE, a river of France, which takes its rise in the dep. of Pas-de-Calais, about 6 m. ENE of Doulens, passes through a small portion of the dep. of Somme, and running along its northern confines, a small lake in the dep. of Indre-et-Loire, about 3 m. WSW of Savigne, and directs its course to the S, under the name of the Doil. On reaching Bourgueil it takes the appellation of the Authion; thence running to the W it enters the dep. of Maine-et- Loire, and running in a direction nearly parallel with the Loire, unites, after a course of 66 m., of which about 18 are navigable, with that river at St. Aubin-des-Ponts-de-Cé. The narrow span of the bridges, and the number of fords and fisheries impede the navigation of this river. Its principal Nogent-le-Rotrou. The cant. comprises 16 com- Its only manufacture is bolting-cloth. Pop. 1,312.
AUTIGNY, a village of Switzerland, in the canton of Friburg, on the l. bank of the Glane, 16 m. SW of Friburg. Pop. 398.

AUTISE, a river of France, which rises in the dep. of Deux-Sevres, about 2 m. to the W of Mazieres, and running in a generally SW direction, falls into the Sevre-Niortaise in the dep. of Vendee, after a course of about 36 m., of which 6 m. from Port-de-Souille are navigable.

AUTONE, a small river of France, which takes its rise in the dep. of Aisne, near Villers-Coterets, and passing into the dep. of Oise falls, after a course WNW of about 21 m., into the Oise near Verberie. This river forms the means of transit of considerable quantities of timber from the forests of Villers-Coterets, Compiegne, &c.
AUTUR, a town of Hindostan, in the province and 25 m. SW of the city of Hyderabad, in N lat. 17° 17'.
AUTRANS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Isere, and cant. of Villard-de-Lans. Pop. 1,107.
AUTRECHES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Oise, and cant. of Attichy. Pop. 950.
AUTRECOURT, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Ardennes, and canton of Mouzon, 6 m. ESE of Sedan. Pop. 907. Here are some carding and spinning-mills.
AUTRETOT, a commune of France, in the dep. of Seine-Inferieure, and cant. of Yvetot. Pop. 951.
AUTREY, a canton, commune, and village of Gray. The cant. comprises 17 com., and contained in 1831 a pop. of 9,377.—The v., 6 m. NW of Gray, has some blast furnaces and forges. Pop. 1,078.
AUTREY-SUR-LA-MORTAGNE, a village of France, in the dep. of Vosges, in the canton and 3 m. SSE of Remberviller. Pop. 300.
AUTRICOURT - SUR - OURCE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Cote-d'Or, cant. of Montignysur-Aube. There are some furnaces here. Pop. 960.
AUTRY, a commune of France, in the dep. of Loiret, cant. of Chatillon-sur-Loire. Pop. 1,225.
AUTRIDGE BAY, a bay in N America, extending from Fury and Hecla strait into Cockburn island, in N lat. 70° 10', and W long. 85° 10'.

AUTUN, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Saône-et-Loire, the cap. of the arrond. and canton of the same name, in N lat. 46° 56′ 43", E long. 4° 18'; 55 m. NNW of Macon, and 30 m. ENE of Chalons-sur-Saone. The town occupies a picturesque situation on the declivity of a hill, the base of which is watered by the Arroux. It still retains many monuments of its former imperial magnificence. Besides several fine temples now in ruins, two of the ancient gates of the city, are in good preservation,—the porte St. Andrè, erected or repaired by Constantine, of a mixed order of architecture, and the porte D'Arroux, surmounted by a richly ornamented gallery. The cathedral is a Gothic edifice of the middle century. There is also an ancient seminary built by Louis XIV. Of modern buildings the principal are, the university, connected with which is a valuable library, the townhall, and a theatre. Many ancient marbles and bronzes have been discovered in the vicinity of A., and an extensive collection of medals is deposited in the mayorality, some of which are unique. The manufactures of this town are carpets, porcelain, and paper. There are also numerous tanneries, brewhouses, and forges. Argentiferous lead and copper have been discovered in the neighbourhood of A., but neither has been worked. The commerce consists of grain, wine, timber, hemp, skins, leather, horses, and cattle. Several fairs are held annually in this town. The pop. of A. in 1789 was 7,410; in 1831, 9,221; in 1846, 10,350. It is the seat of a bishop, a suffragan of the archbishop of Lyons, whose diocese comprises the dep. of Saone-et-Loire. Talleyrand filled the see of A. at the commencement of the Revolution.— Autun is one of the most ancient cities in France, and occupies the site of Bibracte, the capital of the Eduens. It became a Roman colony under Augustus, whose name it assumed, in conjunction with the Celtic termination dun—'a hill'—forming the appellation Augusto - dunum, of which the present name is a corruption. After a siege of 7 months it was taken by Tetricus, by whom it was pillaged and entirely destroyed. In the 4th century it was rebuilt by Constantine. In 731 it was sacked by the Saracens, and again by the Normans in 888; in 1379 it was burnt by the English, and in 1814 again suffered from the ravages of war. — The arrond. of A. is divided into 8 cantons, Autun, Couches, Epinac, Issy l'Eveque, Lucenay l'Eveque, Mesvres, Montcenis, and Saint-Leger-sous Beuvray; and is subdivided into 87 communes. Area 187,691 hectares. Pop. in 1836, 87,356; in 1846, 93,329.
AUTURA. See EURE.
AUTZ, a town of Russia, in the gov. of Courland, 36 m. SSE of Goldingen.
AUVENT (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Haute-Vienne, in the canton and 3 m. NNW of St. Laurent-de-Gorre, on the l. bank of the Gorre. Pop. 1,951.

AUVERGNE, an ancient province of France, now forming the departments of Cantal and Puy-de- AUV Dôme, and the arrond. of Brionde in the dep. of Haute-Loire; bounded on the N by Bourbonnais; on the E by Velay and Forez; on the S by the Cevennes and Rouergue; on the W by Quercy, Limousin, and Marche; and on the NW by Berry. This prov., which comprised an area of 1,388,218 sq. hectares, in superficial extent, presents a generally mountainous surface. It is intersected from N to S by a lofty mountain-range, of which the culminating points form the Puy-de-Dôme, alt. 4,797 ft. [Pascal], the Mont-d'Or, to the SW of which the Puy-de- Sausi, alt. 6,650 ft., is the loftiest; and to the SW of these, the Salers or Lezallier, and the Plomb-du-Cantal, alt. 6,338 ft. On the last, snow remains unmelted during the greater portion of the year. A ramification on the SE connects this chain with the Margeride ridge; and on the NW another branch —which separates the upper waters of the Sioule and Chavanoux — unites it with the mountains of Limousin. These mountain-ranges are of a highly volcanic character, and exhibit throughout their entire extent numerous indications of former igneous activity. Desmarest describes them as cones of a volcanic origin reared on the top of a lofty ridge of granite, and distributed irregularly round the Puy-de-Dôme. The general elevation of the granitic ridge above the Lemagne, or valley of the Allier, is 1,500 ft.; and the basaltic cones frequently rise to an equal or greater height above the granite. The district is also curiously intersected by coulées or basaltic currents—here called cheire or cherre—which descend from the granitic plateau into the valleys, "rolling their tide of desolation into the midst of fertility." With respect to the epoch when these extinct volcanoes were in action, it is perhaps impossible to come to any certain conclusion; but some of the coulées are quite fresh in appearance, and it is said that bones marked by the teeth of hyenas have been found under the volcanic tufa. "The marks of the powerful agency of fire are so fresh that the spectator might suppose that in some parts it had scarcely ceased to burn; yet there is no record of any volcanic eruption having taken place since the time when Cæsar was encamped in A." [Bakewell.] The whole district is of peculiar interest to the geologist as constituting what has been called "the Phlegeæan fields of France." The district is also remarkable for its cromlechs. Of the two subjoined cuts from Bakewell, the first represents a section of the country from the plains of Allier to the volcanic rocks W of Clermont. The section is taken in the direction from east to west. The lowest rock, or granite, a a, is a continuation of the granite of the Forez mountains. bb represents the fresh-water limestone in the valley of Clermont; the strata are nearly horizontal. cccc are beds of volcanic tufa, intermixed with bitumen; d d d, basalt, capping the tufa in various parts. There is also basalt in some of the valleys, for the currents of lava pass into basalt. e is a dome-shaped hill of trachyte; whether this be a part of the granite melted and forced up, or the remains of a bed of felspathic lava, is undecided. The nature of the rock h, immediately under the trachyte mountains, is not ascertained. ff are volcanic mountains with craters, rising among the other mountains; from many of these, currents of scoriaceous and cellular lava, gg, may be traced for several miles, descending into the valleys. Clermont is the usual head-quarters of tourists visiting this district. And the subjoined cut represents the mountains to the NW of Clermont as seen from the Puy-de-Cruelle which rises 500 ft. above the plain of Clermont. No. 1 is the Puy-de-Dôme; 2 the Puyde-Cherson; 3 Sarconi; 4 the volcano of Pariou; 5 the position of La Barraque, a mountain-rim which affords a good station for the explorer; 6 Montadoux, 3 m. W of Clermont. The position of Clermont is indicated by the church in the plain. The mountains immediately round the plain are composed of basalt and tufa, resting on fresh-water limestone. H The most important mineral productions of this district are lead, iron, mercury, and antimony. Silver is found in small quantities, but gold is now rarely to be met with. Basalt and granite are abundant; and topazes, emeralds, and amethysts, are not uncommon. Numerous rivers water the various districts of this prov. Of these the principal are the Allier, with its tributaries the Sioule and Alaignon, and on the western confines the Dordogne. Mineral and thermal springs are also abundant. This prov. consisted of two subdivisions, strikingly dissimilar in climate and productions. Haute-Auvergne, which comprises the mountainous region in the S, is wild and picturesque, its rich pasturages constituting all its agricultural wealth, while Basse-Auvergne, which extends along the banks of the Allier, presents a succession of hills and valleys thickly bestrewn with towns and villages, and adorned with orchards and vineyards. The name Auvergne was derived from the Averni, the ancient inhabitants of the territory, and the formidable opponents of the Romans. The music of the Auvergnats is the bagpipe; and in their peculiarly dark complexion, and in some customs, they appear to retain marks of their Celtic origin.—Dictionnaire Geographique.—Bakewell's Travels in the Alps.—Annales scientifiques de l'Auvergne, 7 vols. in 8vo, 1828-34.—Professor Forbes in Edin. New Phil. Journ., vol. xxi.
AUVERNE' (GRAND), a commune of France, in the dep. of Loire-Inferieure, and cant. of Moisdon-la- Riviere. Pop. 1,409.
AUVERNE' (Petit), a commune of France, in the dep. of Loire-Inferieure, and cant. of St. Juliende-Vouvantes. Pop. 957.
AUVERNIER, a village of Switzerland, in the canton of Neufchatel, on the lake and about 3 m. SW of the town of the same name. Its wines are esteemed the best in the canton. Pop. 800.
AUVERS-LE-HAMON, a commune of France, in the dep. of Sarthe, and cant. of Sable. Pop. 2,170.
AUVERS-SUR-OISE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Seine-et-Oise, in the canton and 31 m. NE of Pontoise. Pop. 1,806.

AUVILLARS, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Tarn-et-Garonne, and arrond. of Moissac. The cant. comprises 9 communes, and contained in 1831 a pop. of 8,303. The town, which is 8 m. WSW of Moissac, possesses some manufactories of china. Pop. 2,302. I. AUX
AUVILLERS-LES-FORGES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Ardennes, and cant. of Signyle-Petit, 10 m. from Rocroi. Pop. 661.
AUWAL, a town of Bohemia, in the circle of Kauerzim, 6 m. W of Brod, and about 13 m. E of the cap. It belongs to the prince of Lichtenstein.
AUXELLES-HAUT, a commune of France, in the dep. of Haut-Rhin, and cant. of Giromagny, 8 m. NW of Belfort. Pop. 932. In this locality are mines of argentiferous lead.

AUXERRE, an ancient city of France, in the dep. of Yonne, and capital of an arrond. and canton; in N lat. 47° 54', E long. 3° 34' 10"; 112 m. SE of Paris. A. is beautifully situated on an eminence, on the left bank of the river, which gives its name to the dep., 283 ft. above sea-level. The town is surrounded by fine vineyards, and is in general well built. Its principal architectural ornaments are the cathedral, dedicated to St. Stephen, considered one of the finest specimens of Gothic architecture in France; the church of St. Eusebius, founded in 640; and that of St. Germain, celebrated for its fine crypts. There is also an ancient episcopal palace. This town contains likewise a college, museum, an extensive library and a theatre. A botanic garden was formed in 1827. A large hospital has recently been built. The manufactures of A. are hosiery, hats, coarse cloth, druggets, delft ware, musical instrument strings, casks, and paints. There are also brew-houses and tanneries. The commerce consists of wine, timber, charcoal, staves, and brick. Pop. in 1846, 12,464.—A. attained considerable importance while under the Roman domination, and was then called Autesiodurum. It was subsequently united to the duchy of Bourgoyne, and suffered much from the successive invasion of the Huns, Saracens, Normans, and English.—The arrond. of A. is divided into the 12 cantons of Auxerre, E and W Chablis, Coulanges-la-Vineuse, Coulanges-sur- Yonne, Courçon, Ligny-le-Chatel, St. Florentin, St. Sauveur-en-Puissaye, Seignelay, Toucy, and Vermenton, comprising 129 communes. Area 197,981 hectares. Pop. in 1836, 112,109; in 1846, 119,057.
AUXILLOU, a commune of France, in the dep. of Tarn, and cant. of Mazamet. Pop. 1,118.
AUXIMUM. See Osimo.
AUX-LIARDS, a river of the Sioux territory, U. S., which falls into the r. side of St. Peter's river, between Red Wood and Blue Earth rivers.
AUXON, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Aube, and canton of Ervy, 15 m. SSW of Troyes. Pop. 2,400.

AUXONNE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Côte-d'Or, and arrond. of Dijon.—The cant. comprises 17 communes, and contained, in 1831, a pop. of 12,279. The town lies on the l. bank of the Saone, 20 m. ESE of Dijon, and is strongly fortified. Pop. in 1841, 5,287. It contains a castle, arsenal, barracks, powder and provision magazines, and a military hospital. It has also a military school, a communal college, a public library containing 3,000 volumes, and a chamber of commerce. The river is crossed by a handsome bridge, which is connected with the town by a viaduct of 23 arches. The parade-ground is extensive, and the old ramparts form delightful promenades. A. possesses extensive manufactories of oil; and has an active trade in grain, wine, timber, charcoal, cattle, china, mercury, various descriptions of cotton, woollen, and linen fabrics, and printed calicoes. Four fairs are held annually. Marble is quarried, and species of the turquoise and fossil coral are found in the environs. The surrounding district is low, and imperfectly drained by the numerous streamlets by which it is intersected.—The origin of A. is uncertain. It is by some supposed to belong to the early period of the 5th century. Under 2 H the domination of the dukes of Burgundy, it became the cap. of the county of the same name. It was subsequently ceded by the crown of France to Charles V., but after a protracted siege, the Spanish general was obliged to relinquish the claim.
AUXY, a commune of France, in the dep. of Loiret, and cant. of Beaune-la-Rolande. Pop. 1,568.
AUXY, a commune of France, in the dep. of Saone-et-Loire, and cant. of Autun. Pop. 1,573.

AUXY-LE-CHATEAU, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Pas-de-Calais, and arrond. of St. Pol. The cant. comprises 28 communes, and contained, in 1831, a pop. of 15,253. The town lies on the r. bank of the Authie river, 14 m. SW of St. Pol. It possesses a cotton-mill. The environs are marshy. Pop. 2,725.

AUZANCES, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Creuse, and arrond. of Aubusson. The cant. comprises 12 communes, and contained, in 1831, a pop. of 9,749. The town lies 16 m. ENE of Aubusson. Its commerce consists principally in leather, wool, feathers, hemp, linen, and cattle. Pop. 1,251.
AUZAT, a village of France, in the dep. of Arriege, and cant. of Vicdessos. Pop. 1,875.
AUZAT-SUR-ALLIER, a commune of France, in the dep. of Puy-de-Dôme, and cant. of Jumeaux, 7 m. SE of Issoire, which contains some fine glassworks and coal. Pop. 1,750.
AUZELLES, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Puy-de-Dome, and canton of Cunlhat. Pop. 2,635.
AUZERS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Cantal, and cant. of Mauriac. Pop. 1,050.
AUZITS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Aveyron, and cant. of Rignac. Pop. 1,346.

AUZON, a canton, commune, and village of France, in the dep. of Haute-Loire, and arrond. of Brioude. The canton comprises 13 communes, and contained, in 1831, a pop. of 10,341. The village lies on the r. side of the Allier, about 7 m. N of Brioude. Coal is worked in the commune. Pop. 1,238.—Also a small river of France, in the dep. of Vaucluse, which takes its rise near Flassan, and running towards the W, falls, after a course of 27 m., into the Sorgues. It is of considerable utility for irrigation.

AVA, the upper portion of the Birman empire, now generally recognised by geographers under the name of BIRMAH: which see .- Also the present capital of the Birman empire, situated on the l. bank of the Irawadi, here 1,094 yards broad, in N lat. 21° 50', E long. 96°, 4 m. WSW of Amarapura. It consists of an outer, and an inner town; the former lying to the SW of the latter, and the whole being surrounded by a brick wall 15½ ft. in height, and a ditch. The city, or inner town, has additional defences. In general the houses are mere huts, thatched with grass; but it has probably regained somewhat of its ancient splendour. Crawfurd estimated its pop. in 1826 at only 25,000. On the 23d of March, 1839, it suffered severely from an earthquake, the shock of which was felt throughout a space of 1,000 m. from the borders of China. The native popular name of the city is Augwa, which the Hindus have corrupted into Awa, and Europeans into Ava, -a word which we have extended to the whole kingdom. In all public writings it is denominated Ratanapura, or 'the city of Gems.' The town of Sagaing is situated on the opposite side of the Irawadi, directly fronting A.; and the Birman capital is not confined to the city of A., but embraces also Sagaing and Amarapura. The district of A. extends along the river 6 taings, or 12 m., and half that extent inland. -Crawfurd.

AVAILLES, or AVAILLES-LIMOUSIN, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Vienne, and arrond. of Civray. The cant. comprises 4 com., and contained, in 1831, a pop. of 4,683. The town lies on the I. bank of the Vienne, about 18 m. ESE of Civray. In the vicinity are quarries of mill-stone and chalybeate springs. Wine forms its chief article of commerce. Pop. 1,905.
AVAL. See BAHREIN.
AVALATS (LES), a village of France, in the dep. of Tarn, and canton of Villefranche, on the l. bank of the Tarn, 6 m. ENE of Aldy. It affords excellent wines, and has a paper-mill.

AVALLON, an arrond., canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Yonne.—The arrond. comprises an area of 97,879 hect., and contains 5 cantons, -Avallon, Guillon, Isle-sur-le-Serein, Quarre-les-Tombes, and Vezelay. Pop. in 1831, 46,966; in 1846, 47,576.—The cant. comprises 15 communes, and had, in 1831, a pop. of 12,982.—The town is beautifully situated on a rocky height on the r. bank of the Voisin. The streets are generally broad, and the houses well-built. It has a parishchurch, the portal of which is handsome, a theatre, a communal college, an hospital, and boards of trade and agriculture. The race-course commands a beautiful view of the valley of the Voisin. The manufactures of A. consist of cloth of various descriptions, paper, leather, and mustard; and it possesses an active trade in wine, grain, spiceries, timber, and casks. -This town is of considerable antiquity; it is referred to under the name of Aballo by Antoninus. It formed one of the strongholds of the duchy of Burgundy.

AVALON, an extensive peninsula in the SE of Newfoundland, formed by the deep bays of Trinity and Placentia, and united to the island by an isthmus only 2 m. in breadth. Its figure is extremely irregular,— Conception bay on the N, and St. Mary's bay on the S, deeply indenting it, and rendering its coast remarkable for the variety and extent of its outline. Its shores consist of rocky ridges, forming often bold and lofty cliffs and headlands, broken into numerous inlets, and affording many excellent and commodious harbours. Conception bay, which is about 50 m. in length, and 20 in breadth, forms the richest, the best cultivated, and generally the most flourishing district of Newfoundland. The pop. of its shores amounts to nearly 25,000; and its western side is studded with numerous towns and harbours of rising importance. Of these the principal is Harbour-Grace, about 10 m. from the mouth of the bay. The small island of Belle Isle, to the SE, is remarkably fertile. About 15 m. SSE of Cape St. Francis, on the E coast, is St. John's, the capital of the island, finely situated in a spacious and well-sheltered embrasure. On the southern coast, beyond the long reach of headlands and coves which extends from Cape Race to French Mistaken Point, is Trepassy bay, enclosing Cape Pine and a harbour of the same name. St. Mary's bay is an embrasure about 30 m. in depth, separated at its extremity by a tract only 10 m. in breadth from the head of Conception bay, and by about 8 m. from that of Trinity. It possesses several excellent cod-fishing establishments, but is still scantily inhabited. The pop. of the district of Placentia—once the most important in the island— is now reduced to about 3,000, and little more remains of the town than the ruins of its fortifications. The navigation of the coast between Capes Pine and Shotts is peculiarly dangerous, from the strength and variety of currents, occasioned by the indraught of the great bays to the W. The cod, with which the southern, in common with all the other shores of the peninsula, abounds, exceed those of the westward in size. The proximity of A. to the Great Bank, and its facility of approach from Europe, constitute this peninsula the most important district of Newfoundland. Baccalao island, at the NW extremity of Conception bay, is reputed the first land discovered by Sebastian Cabot in 1497.
AVANATOK, one of the Aleutian islands, in N lat. 54° 15'.
AVANOS, a town of Turkey in Asia, in Karamania, on the r. side of the Tum, or eastern branch of the Kizil-Irmak, in N lat. 38° 50'.
AVARAY, a commune of France, in the dep. of Loire-et-Cher, and cant. of Mer, on the r. bank of the Loire, 15 m. NE of Blois. Pop. 865.

AVARES, a khanate of Russia, in the Caucasus, extending between the Koïsou and Aksai, and bounded on the S by the Upper Samur and the snowy ridge of the Caucasus. It is extremely mountainous; and, except in the valleys, incapable of cultivation. The Avares form the most powerful of the Lesghiz tribes. They number 30,000, and are governed by an independent khan or chief. They speak a dialect of the Lesghian, and profess Mahommedanism. An annual subsidy of 10,000 rubles was, and perhaps is still paid them by Russia, in consideration of their abstaining from depredations on the adjacent provinces.
AVARELLA (CAPE), a promontory on the E coast of Cochin-China, in N lat. 12° 55', E long. 109° 25'.
AVARENA (POINT), a promontory on the SE coast of Hayti, in N lat. 18°, W long. 71°.

AVASI-SIMA, or AVADSI, one of the Japan islands, situated at the head of Kino channel, between the islands of Nifon and Silkokf. It extends from 34° 12′ to 34° 4′ N lat., and from 134° 44′ to 135° 8′ E long.; and is about 66 m. in circumference. It is mountainous; and its soil is generally stony and unproductive. Cotton is almost the only article of cultivation. Fishing constitutes the principal employment of the inhabitants. The lagunes afford salt in small quantities. The island is divided into 2 jurisdictions.—The chief town, of the same name, is situated on the W coast.

AVATCHA, or AWATSKA, a bay of Russia, in Asia, on the SE coast of Kamtschatka, at the mouth of a river of the same name, and of the Paratounka or Ilmitsh, in N lat. 52° 52', E long. 158° 47', forming a noble harbour. Its coast-line is about 27 m. in extent; and it is entered by a channel about 4 m. in length, and 2 m. in breadth. It varies from 14 to 18 ft. in depth. The highest tides rarely exceed 5 ft. It is enclosed on the SW and W by wooded hills; and, although its temperature falls below that of some of the more northern districts of the peninsula, it is never entirely, and but rarely partially frozen. This bay forms the principal Russian port on the Pacific, and is the only harbour on the coast of Kamtschatka accessible to large vessels. It contains several minor bays. One of these, Petropaulski, on which a town of the same name, formerly called Avatcha, is situated, and which is separated from the bay by a narrow neck of land, is one of the finest harbours in the world. About 20 m. N by E of Petropaulski, is the active volcanic mountain of Avatcha, 11,500 feet in height.
AVAVIRI, or AYAVIRI, a town of Upper Peru, on the southern side of the Cordillera de Vilcanota, in S lat. 15° 5'; 90 m. NNW of the town of Puno.
AVDEMO, a town and harbour in the island of Scarpanto, in the Grecian archipelago, on a bay of the same name, in N lat. 35° 37', E long. 27° 10'.
AVE, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Namur, and arrond. of Dinant. Pop. 262. It contains lime-quarries, and mines of barytes and lead.

AVE, a river of Portugal, in the prov. of Entre- Douro-e-Minho, which rises in the mountains of Sta. Catarina, runs in a generally W direction, receiving on the r. the Solho and D'Este, and after a course of 48 m. falls into the Atlantic, at Villa-de-Conde, about 25 m. N of Oporto.
AVE (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Morbihan, and cant. of Vannes. Pop. 1,263.
AVEBURY. See ABURY.
AVECHICA, a river of Guayana, which rises in the Sierra-Usupama, and, curving to the NE, unites, after the junction of the Juruario, with the Cayune about 20 m. E of Cura.
AVEGNO. See AVEYNO.

AVEIRO, formerly called Braganca Nova, a town and a district of Portugal, in the prov. of Beira, on the S side of the estuary of the Vouga, 6 m. from the Barra-Nova, and 34 m. NNW of Coimbra, in N lat. 40° 38′ 24", and W long. 8° 38'. It is partly enclosed by walls; and contains five churches, a college, two hospitals, and a custom-house. Its harbour is safe and commodious; but its entrance possesses little depth. Salt is extensively manufactured from the adjacent saline marshes. The estuary of the Vouga abounds with sardines and oysters, the latter esteemed the finest in the kingdom. A. conducts an active trade in salt, fish, oil, wine and oranges. The improvements which have been effected on the harbour have annihilated the fetid marshes with which it was formerly surrounded, and greatly contributed to the salubrity of the locality. A. is the see of a bishop, suffragan of the archb. of Braga. Pop. 5,000. The pop. of the district was estimated at 214,610, or 57,222 hearths in 1840.

AVEIRO, a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Para, on the r. bank of the Tapojos, 70 m. SSW of Santarem. A considerable quantity of cocoa is exported from this place.—Also an Indian settlement on the upper part of the Rio-da-Santa-Cruz, in Bahia.
AVEIRON. See AVEYRON.
AVEIROS, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Estremadura, 18 m. SW of Santarem.
AVEIZE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Rhone, cant. of St. Symphorien-sur-Coise. Pop. 1,071.

AVELAINS (Castro-D'), a fort and a district in the Brazilian prov. of Rio Negro, on the r. bank of the Amazon, at the junction of the Iça or Putumayo, 27 m. SW of Matura, in S lat. 4° 35'. The district is chiefly inhabited by Cambevas Indians.
AVELAND, a wapentake in the southern part of the co. of Lincoln, containing 23 parishes. Area 53,220 acres. Pop. in 1831, 9,978; in 1841, 11,557.
AVELANS, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Beira, 20 m. N of Coimbra, on the road from that town to Oporto.
AVELEY, a parish in Essex, 2 m. NE of Purfleet. Area 3,920 acres. Pop. 849.

AVELGHEM, a town of Belgium, in West Flanders, on the l. bank of the Schelde, in the arrond. and 9 m. ESE of Courtray. It possesses extensive brine-pits, dye-works, and manufactories of oil and tobacco. Pop. 4,711.
AVELIN, a commune of France, in the dep. of Nord, cant. of Pont-a-Marcq, 6 m. SE of Lille. It has extensive potteries and oil-mills. Pop. 1,570.

AVELLA, a town of Naples, in the Terra-di- Lavoro, beautifully situated on a lofty eminence on the l. bank of the Lagni, 5 m. NE of Nola, and about 20 m. ENE of Naples. It contains four parish churches, and has a pop. of 6,030.—In the environs are the ruins of the ancient Abella, celebrated by Virgil for its apples and honey.
AVELLANEDA, a town of Spain, in the prov. of New Castile, and division of Toledo, on the r. bank of the Ibor, 23 m. SSW of Oropesa, and about 25 m. NW of Guadalupe, on the road to Almarez.

AVELLI'NO, a district, canton, and town of Naples, in Principato-Ultra. The town lies at the foot of Monte Virgine, to the W of the Sabato, 10 m. SSE of Monte Fusco, and about 30 m. E of Naples. It is strongly fortified, and is to a considerable extent well-built. The streets are broad but irregular, and there is a square adorned with an obelisk. It contains a cathedral, three parish churches, and a royal college, and has agreeable promenades. It possesses manufactories of common cloth and of maccaroni, and dye-works. Chestnuts, and a peculiar species of the hazel-nut, abound in the environs. The principal articles of commerce are grain and other productions of the locality. This town is the see of a bishop, suffragan of the archb. of Benevento, possesses a civil and a criminal court, and is governed by a syndic and four other magistrates, whose offices are hereditary. It has repeatedly suffered severely from earthquakes; the last occurred in 1805. Pop. 15,586. In the vicinity, on Monte Virgine, on the site of an ancient temple of Cybele, is the celebrated convent from which the mountain takes it name.—The district of A. comprises 14 cantons, viz. Altavilla, Atripalda, Avellino, Cervinara, Chiusano, Santo-Giorgio-la-Montagna, Mercogliano, Monteforte, Montefusco, Montemiletto, Montesarchio, Serino, Solofra, and Vitolano.

AVEN, or Avon, the name of numerous streams in Scotland and England. Richards, in his 'Antiquæ Linguæ Britannicæ Thesaurus,' under the article Afon, observes: "Avon is the proper name of several rivers in England; as Avon, the river of Bristol; the Avon in Northamptonshire; another in Warwickshire, where there is a town called Stratford-upon-Avon, &c., for which this reason is to be assigned, viz. that the English, when they drove the Britons out of that part of Great Britain, called from them England, took the appellatives of the old inhabitants for proper names; and so, by mistaking Avon, which, with us, signifies only a river in general, it came to serve with them for the proper name of several of their rivers." The Celtæ had a deity called Divona, or the river-god, as we know from Ausonius,-" Divona Celtarum lingua fons addite divi." Ireland says that the name Avon, or Evon, is common to rivers whose course is easy and gentle. There are three rivers in Scotland which bear this name, besides several minor streams. The term Avon is also prefixed to the names of several Scottish streams; such as the Avon-Brouchaig, and the Avon-Coll, in Ross-shire; the Avon-Adail, and the Avon- Araig, in Argyleshire. Chalmers says that the term Amon is merely a variation of Avon; and, in confirmation of this, we may remark that the Almond of Perthshire is sometimes called Amon, and sometimes Avon.

AVEN, a river in Kincardineshire, which rises in the N of the Ganiach hill, and flows by a NE course into the Dee at Banchory-Tarnan.—Also a stream issuing from a small loch of the same name, in the Cairngorm mountains, in the SW extremity of Banffshire, at an alt. of 1,800 ft. above sea-level, and flowing N to the Spey, into which it falls at Ballindalloch.—Also a river of Stirlingshire, which rises in Cumbernauld parish, and flows into the firth of Forth, between Grangemouth and Borrowstonness. The Union canal, and the Edinburgh and Glasgow railroad, are both carried across this stream on magnificent lines of arches.-Also a river of France, in the dep. of Finisterre, which rises in the hilly district to the ENE of Quimper, and running to the S falls into the ocean, about 4 m. below Pont-Aven.

AVENAKOE, or AVERNAKOE, an island of Denmark, in the Little Belt to the S of Fyen. It is about 5 m. in length from NW to SE; varying from 11 m. to one-third of that extent in breadth. It forms part of the bailiwick of Svendborg.
AVENASKY, a large village of Hindostan, in the Carnatic, 36 m. NW of Daraporam. It possesses an ancient and curiously sculptured Hindu temple.
AVENAY, a commune of France, in the dep. of Marne, and cant. of Ay, 22 m. SSE of Rheims. It has considerable commerce in wine and timber. Pop. 1,104.
AVENBURY, a parish in Herefordshire, 2 m. SSE of Bromyard. Area 3,140 acres. Pop. 382. AVE

AVENCE, a river of France, in the dep. of Lotet-Garonne, which rises near the SW confines, and runs to the N past Castel-Jaloux and Gaujac, divides into three branches, and unites, after a course of about 24 m., with the Garonne, 3 m. NNW of Marmande.

AVENCHES, a district and town of Switzerland, in the detached portion of the cant. of Vaud, on the NE of Friburg, 57 m. NE of Lausanne. Pop. 1,450. This town, the ancient Aventicum, is of great antiquity, and numerous architectural relics of its former splendour still exist. It, however, occupies scarcely one-tenth of the site of the metropolis of the Helvetii,—the rest is covered with fields of maize, tobacco, and other species of agricultural produce. The walls of the port of "levelled Aventicum" [Byron] formerly washed by Lake Morat, now more than 1 m. distant, are still standing. The ancient town was much embellished by the emperor Vespasian, in whose reign it contained 60,000 souls; and was the most flourishing of the last existing colonies of the Romans. A. was devastated first by the Germans in 307, and again in 447 by the Huns. It was partially restored by the bishops of Lausanne.
AVENHEIM, a commune of France, in the dep. of Bas-Rhin, and canton of Truchtersheim. It possesses a remarkable alkaline spring, which is cold in summer and warm in winter. Pop. 230.
AVENHORN, a town of Holland, in the province of N Holland, 9 m. ESE of Alkmaar.
AVENICO, a river of Ecuador, in the prov. of Cuenca, which takes its rise in the Andes near Assuay, and, running to the E, forms one of the head streams of the Marona, a tributary of the Amazon.
AVENIERES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Mayenne, cant. of Laval. It has some marble works. Pop. 2,574.
AVENIERES (LES-CIERS), a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Isere, and canton of Morestel. Pop. 3,428.
AVENING, a parish in Gloucestershire, 3 m. N by W of Tutbury. Area 4,660 acres. Pop. 227.
AVENNES, a canton and commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Liege, arrond. and 9 m. NW of Huy. Pop. 389.
AVENSAN, a commune of France, in the dep. of Gironde, cant. of Castelnau-de-Médoc. Pop. 1,005.
AVENT, a town of Switzerland, in the cant. of Vallais, near the l. bank of the Lucerne, and about 6 m. WNW of Sion.

AVENTINO (Monte), the most considerable of the seven hills upon which Rome is built. To the E it had the city-walls in ancient times; on the S, the Campus Figulinus; on the W, the Tiber; and on the N, Mons Palatinus. It is now covered with gardens, with here and there a solitary church erected from the ruins of ancient edifices. Of these the principal are St. Sabina, St. Maria Aventino, and St. Alexis, on the supposed sites of the temples of Juno Regina and the Bona Dea, and of the Armilustrum. At its NW base, which is washed by the Tiber, stood the ancient Navalia or docks of Rome; and adjacent were the public granaries, several temples, &c. A. is supposed to have been one of the least populous of the Roman hills. The name is generally conceived to be derived from Aventinus Silvius, king of Alba, whose burial place it was.

AVENZA, or LAVENZA, a parish and town of Italy, in the duchy of Modena, about 4 m. SW of Carrara. This town, built in the 12th cent., stood originally at the mouth of the river of the same name, now nearly 1 m. distant, and was the principal place of shipment for the Carrara marbles. The port is now at the village of Marina d'Avenza, or Speggia d'Avenza.
AVEREEST, a town of Holland, in the province of Overyssel, on the l. bank of the river Reest, 7 m. N of Ommen.

AVERGALE, a province of Abyssinia, in the district of Tigre, consisting of a narrow tract of land about 50 m. in length, stretching along the r. bank of the Tacazze. It is inhabited by a tribe of Agows.
AVERHAM, a parish of Nottinghamshire, 2 m. W by N of Newark. Area 2,530 acres. Pop. 264.
AVERILE, a township of Vermont, U.S., in Essex co., watered by the head-streams of the Nuthegan, and by tributaries of the St. Francis.
AVERNES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Seine-et-Oise, cant. of Marines, 12 m. from Pontoise. It has manufactories of red-lead. Pop. 536.

AVERNO, a lake of Naples, 23 m. NW of Pozzuoli. This lake, the ancient Avernus Lacus, celebrated in the history and mythology of Rome, occupies the crater of an extinct volcano, and has no outlet. It is nearly circular in form, and, according to Ferrari, 12 m. in circumf.; but Romanelli makes it 3 m. in circumf. The fancy of poets has for ages assigned it an unfathomable depth; but Captain Smith found its extreme depth to be only from 100 to 102 ft., beginning to shoal from about 40 ft. from the banks. [Geological Trans., N. S., II., 347.] Its banks, formerly covered with forests and pestilential marshes, are now adorned with gardens and vineyards. On the r. side are still to be seen the ruins of the temple of Apollo; and on the opposite, the grotto of the sibyl of Cuma. By the construction of a communication with Lacus Lucrinus, this lake was connected, by Agrippa, with the bay of Baia; but in 1538 the Lucrine lake and the canal were destroyed by the eruption of the Monte Nuovo. Mineral baths exist in the neighbourhood of the lake.
AVEROE, an island of Norway, off the coast of Romsdal, in N lat. 63°, E long. 70° 30'.

AVERSA, a town of Naples, in the Terra di Lavoro, and district of Caserta, 9 m. NNW of Naples, and about the same distance S of Capua. It is situated in a luxuriant plain abounding with vineyards and orange-groves, and is well and regularly built. It contains 9 churches, several convents, and an admirably conducted asylum for the insane. It is an episcopal see, suffragan of St. Siege. This town, which occupies the site of the ancient Atella, was founded by the Normans in 1,030. Pop. 16,158.
AVERTIN (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Indre-et-Loire, cant. of Tours. Pop. 1,296.
AVERTON, a commune of France, in the dep. of Mayenne, cant. of Villaines-la-Juhel. It has some paper-mills. Pop. 1,214.
AVERY'S GORE, a township of Vermont, U. S., in Addison co., near the summit of the Green mountains. Pop. 78.—Also a township in Franklin co. Pop. 35.

AVES, or BIRD ISLAND, a small island of the Antilles, in N lat. 15° 39', and W long. 63° 40' [Purdy], N lat. 15° 40′ 56", W long. 63° 36′ 54" [Nautical Mag., 1838.] It is about \( \frac{3}{4} \) m. in length, and is nearly flat; its greatest elevation not exceeding 15 ft. above highwater level. It is consequently invisible, even in daylight, at a distance of 6 or 8 m. Its shores are sandy, and it affords good anchorage on its W side. The name is derived from the sea-fowl with which it abounds; whose eggs are collected here in vast quantities.

AVES (ILAS DE), or BIRD ISLANDS, a small group, or rather two groups of islets, in the Dutch Antilles, in N lat. 12°, and W long. 67° 38'. The principal island, about 4 m. in length, is narrow, rock-begirt, and extremely dangerous. On it the French squadron under Admiral Estrees suffered shipwreck in 1678. They are generally sterile, but exhibit a few orange and citron trees. Their only inhabitants are a few Dutch fishermen.
AVES, a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Goyaz, which falls into the Araguaya, in S lat. 8° 55'.
AVESA, a village of Venetian Lombardy, near Verona, in a beautiful valley to which it gives its name.
AVESNE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Herault, cant. of Lunas, 10 m. W of Lodève. There are thermal springs and baths here. Pop. 1,407.
AVESNELLES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Nord, in the cant. of Avesnes. It has extensive tile and brick works, lint manufactories, and quarries of marble. Pop. 671.

AVESNES, or AVESNE, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Nord. The arrond. comprises an area of 139,722 hect.; and is subdivided into 8 cantons, viz., Avesnes, Bavay, Berlaimont, Landrecies, Manbeuge, Quesnoy, Solre-le-Chateau, and Trelon. Its pop. amounted in 1836 to 132,335, in 1846 it was 139,248.—The cant., comprising 25 com., had a pop. in 1831 of 22,816.—The town, which is the cap. of the arrond., is situated on the Helpe Majeure, a branch of the Sambre, in N lat. 50° 7' 22", E long. 3° 55' 47"; 26 m. SE of Valenciennes, and 57 m. SE of Lille. It is well-fortified. Its principal building is the cathedral, which has a tower 300 ft. in height, with a fine chime. There are manufactories of coarse serge and hosiery in the town; also salt-refineries, breweries, soap-works, and marble-works. In the environs are brick and tile works, marble and freestone quarries, and several ironfounderies. The trade, which is considerable, consists chiefly in coarse hosiery, which is exported in large quantities to the Netherlands, besides lint, timber-deals, marble, freestone, slate, lime, hops, Maroilles cheese, and cattle. A monthly cattle-fair is held here, and a general fair once a year.—A. was built in the 11th cent., and belonged originally to the counts of Hainault, Holland, and Zealand. It was taken by Louis XI., when its inhabitants were almost all destroyed, and again, in 1559, by the Spaniards. In 1659 it was ceded to France. Pop. in 1789, 3,068; in 1821, 3,129; in 1831, 3,166; in 1836, 3,030.
AVESNES-LES-AUBERT, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Nord, and cant. of Carnieres. Pop. 2,535.

AVESNES-LE-COMTE, a canton, commune, and village of France, in the dep. of Pas-de-Calais, and arrond. of St. Pol. The cant. comprises 35 communes, and contained, in 1831, a pop. of 14,723. The village is 10 m. SE of St. Pol, and nearly the same distance W of Arras. It has oil manufactories, saltrefineries, breweries, tile-works, and nursery-gardens.
AVESNES-LE-SEC, a commune of France, in the dep. of Nord, and cant. of Bouchain. Pop. 1,607.
AVESSAC, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Loire-Inferieure, and cant. of St. Nicolas. Pop. 2,370.
AVESSE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Sarthe, and cant. of Brulon. Pop. 1,027.

AVESTA, a town of Sweden, in the district of Fahlun, on the r. bank of the Dal-elf, 39 m. SSE of Fahlun. It possesses the most extensive copperfounderies and manufactories in the kingdom, engrossing the entire produce of the mines of Fahlun. These works have existed since 1636. There are here also iron-founderies and forges, and saw-mills. Pop. 800. The country between Fahlun and A. is highly picturesque. [Kuttner.]
AVETON-GIFFORD, a parish in Devonshire, 3 m. SE of Modbury. Area 1,840 acres. Pop. 1,057.
AVETRANA, a town of Naples, in the Terra d' Otranto, 30 m. ESE of Tarento, and 6 m. ESE of Mandurra. Pop. 1,050.

AVEYRON, or AVEIRON, a department of France, consisting of the ancient Rouergue, in the prov. of Guienne; bounded on the N by the dep. of Cantal; on the NE by Loyère; on the E by Gard; on the SE by Herault; on the SW by Tarn; on the W by Tarn-et-Garonne; and on the NW by Lot. It is situated between N lat. 43° 41′ 30", and 44° 55′ 25". Its length from N to S is 96 m.; its breadth from E to W 66 m.; its superficies 887,873 hectares, or 2,137,934 acres. This dep., one of the most elevated in France, is intersected by numerous mountain-ridges, all of which, with the exception of the volcanized plateaux to the NW of the Trueyre, are ramifications of the Cevennes. Of these the principal is the Leveson, which extends from NE to SW, dividing the basins of the Tarn and Viaur; and giving rise, near the western frontier, to the river from which the dep. derives its name. The Lot, by which the western districts are watered, is the only navigable river. The surface of the dep. presents three different species of soil,-calcareous, volcanic, and quartzy micaceous schist, more or less commingled with clay and magnesia, -and is to a great extent entirely unproductive. The climate, though generally pure, is otherwise extremely diversified. In the mountainous regions the cold is excessive; and in some districts winter is prolonged during half the year. The most important branch of agricultural industry consists in the rearing of domestic animals. The number of horses and mules belonging to the dep. is estimated at 15,000; of asses, 4,000; of horned cattle, 112,200; of goats, 16,300; of hogs, 60,000; and of sheep, 990,000. With the exception of the roebuck, there are scarcely any other species of game. Fish abound in all the rivers. Foxes and wolves are numerous. There are also several reptiles, such as snakes, vipers, and lizards. The forests consist chiefly of the oak, beech, and fir. Of fruit-trees there is a considerable variety; the most abundant are, the sweet and bitter almond, the plum, and the chestnut. In the eastern cantons, the vine is extensively cultivated; but the wines, excepting those of Lancedat, Agnac, and Mareillac, are of inferior quality. A considerable number of mulberry-trees are grown; and the rearing of silk-worms is becoming an increasingly important article of industry. The agricultural productions consist of oats, rye, wheat grown principally in the western districts, barley, maize, and buck wheat. The supply, however, does not exceed the local demand. The metallurgical operations of Aveyron have been greatly on the increase. There are very extensive iron founderies at Decayerille, and in the arrond. of Villefranche several copper-works. Coal is abundant, and there are mines of silver, alum, and lead, and quarries of marble and stone. The most celebrated mineral waters are those of Sylvanes, and Pont-de-Camarès, in the southern canton, and of Cransac in the coal-district of Albin. Near the latter is the burning mountain of Fontagnes, in which a mass of coal accidentally ignited continues in a constant state of conflagration. The principal manufactures are grey cloth, linsey-woolsey, and felt for paper-mills. Silk manufactories have recently been established. There are also tanneries, dye-works, and cooperages, and several leather, glove, and hat manufactories. The agricultural produce, which is exported annually, amounts in value to about 6,000,000 francs, of which the cheese of Roquefort and Guyole amounts to 1,100,000 frs.; sheep to 1,000,000; wool to 800,000 frs. The number of fairs in the department amounts to 580; and are held in 165 communes. The principal articles of commerce are, cattle, grain, skins, coarse cloth, wool, hemp, chestnuts, wax, cheese, &c. Aveyron is divided into 5 arrond.—Rodez, in which the capital of the department is situated, Espalion, Milhau, CONTRACT CONTRACT CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRA AVI Saint-Affrique, and Villefranche, containing 42 cantons, and 241 communes. It constitutes the diocese of a bishop, suffragan of the archb. of Albi. The dep. is in the military division of Montpellier. It returned 5 deputies to the chamber under the late regime. The pop. in 1801 was 326,340; in 1836, 370,951; in 1846, 389,121, being 43.83 to the sq. kilometre; while the mean density of the pop. of France is 67.088 per sq. kilom. In density of pop. it ranks 76th in the 86 dep. of the kingdom. Its cap. is Rodez; besides which it has 17 towns, each containing a pop. of upwards of 3,000.

AVEYRON, or AVEIRON, a river of France, which takes its rise at the fountain of Veyron, in the Cevennes, to the S of Ceverac-le-Chateau, in the E of the dep. to which it gives its name; runs towards the W, past Rodez and Villefranche, and after bending to the S, forms for a few miles the boundary-line between the dep. of Tarn and Tarn-et-Garonne; thence, entering the latter, pursues its course to the E, passing St. Antoine, Penne, Bruniquei, Negrepelisse, and Realville; and, after a course of about 153 m., unites with the Tarn between Montauban and Moissac. This river is navigable as far as Negrepelisse, a distance of 21 m. Its depth, however, is unequal, and it is much obstructed by manufactories. Its principal tributaries are the Serre, Alson, and Caude on the r.; and on the l. the Viaur, Ceron, and Verre. This river is generally supposed to be the Veronius of the Romans.
AVEYNO, or Avegno, a town of Switzerland, in the canton of Tessin, on the l. bank of the Val Maggia, 5 m. NW of Locarno.
AVEZE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Gard, in the cant. of Le Vigan. It has silk-spinning mills and manufactures of silk hosiery. Pop. 1,322.
AVEZE', a commune of France, in the dep. of Sarthe, and canton of La-Ferte-Bernard. Pop. 1,231.

AVEZZANO, a district and town of Naples, in Abruzzo Ultra 2da. The district comprises 7 cantons, viz., Avezzano, Carsoli, Celano, Civita-Roveto, Gioja, Pescina, and Tagliacozzo. The town is situated in a fine plain, on the NW side of Lake Fucino or Colano, 22 m. S of Aquila. It is surrounded by walls; and contains 10 churches, of which one is collegiate, and an extensive square adorned with many fine buildings, including the ducal palace. It has two annual fairs. This town is supposed to occupy the site of the ancient Alba. Pop. in 1831, 3,166.

AVIA, a river of Spain, in the prov. of Galicia, formed by the junction of several streams which issue from the mountains of Testeyro and Faro, and which, after a course of about 30 m. in a generally S direction, falls into the Minho, near Ribadavia.— Also a small island in the Fiji group, to the NE of Vanuavalavo.
